message.content:
<system-reminder>
The user named this session "overseer-design". This may indicate the session's focus or intent.
</system-reminder>

This is going to be a planning, research, design, and brainstorming session. 

We recently made a new overseer skill to try to create a meta loop above the individual ongoing tracks of epic work that I was manually driving. 

Zooming all the way out, this is the next evolution of the whole Livespec  system that we have been building up progressively from first principles and dogfooding. 

We have just landed or are about to land a plan skill, which is another high-level abstraction. 

And we currently have an instance of the Overseer skill running and ongoing in the livespec-overseer TMUX session and Claude session, As one of the current tracks it is managing 

But it is already running into issues because there is not a deterministic state machine and project management system to back the overseer skill. You can see this from its current and recent logs. You can check on Honeycomb to see the history of logs if you want. 

The planned skill that we made was one attempt to have a higher level of abstraction. 

And we are building the entire console repo from scratch to try to put a human-level TUI and GUI to help drive things. 

And the console repo may have some concept of a state machine in it but I'm not sure that it ties everything together as clearly as we want or that it is the right abstraction level and API. Because it interacts directly with the plan skill 

But what I think that the console may still be missing is a first-class concept of a work-in-limit queue, That is a Kanban-style concept where only a certain number of active items can exist in one lane and state to get started in concurrent working at the same time. 

That is one of the things that the overseer skill is attempting to do. And it is also introducing a tighter level of interaction and control through the tmux panes than the console app currently plans, I think. 

So stepping back the main states that we need are as follows:
1. Anything that is groomed and ready
2. Things that are actually actively being worked
3. things that Are blocked because they need human involvement or input or feedback or action. 
4. Things that are claimed to be done but need to be verified or exploratory tested even after the factory is done as a final check-off and approval step 
5. Things that are actually done.
6. Things that are blocked for a nonhuman reason, for example infrastructure errors or external problems. 
7. Things that are not actively being worked but are instead waiting on something besides human involvement or errors or external problems. For example an approval that has not been received yet or something else. 

These states can easily sit on top of the current livespec ledger system in BEDS or JSON-L as metadata types. Whether they sit alongside or expand the current groomed status and ready status, or whatever else we have, can be decided. 

That may be overcomplicating it and I realize that some of them are redundant with the factory loop themselves. For example the extra review step. But you can never have too many reviews. At least when AI is involved 

Here is a new article and system from Nate B. Jones that talks about these sorts of loops of loops. It describes such a system as I described but with simpler states and also using linear, where clearly we would rely on beads.  

https://unlock-ai.natebjones.com/open-engine

It also contains links to many prompts which you can read and use as part of the brainstorming, research, and analysis for this. 

You should also research prior art such as Gastown because this has many corollaries to the system that Steve Yeagey has created there. 

And this is clearly something that is going to be done with the new plan skill and have an associated research document and directory and a prompt to follow up on it if this context gets full. 

Clarify that you understand everything and if you need any further clarification before we start. 

And also confirm for me the actual state of the planned skill. I don't know if it is still getting wrapped up by one of the ongoing tracks or if it is already usable by you or not. Even if it's not fully usable, you can pretend that it mostly is and do what it is documented or specified as doing.

content.text: Understood — this is a design/research/brainstorming session, and before producing anything I owe you (a) a restatement of what I understand, (b) the actual state of the `plan` skill, and (c) any genuine clarifying questions. Let me ground myself in the current state first with some read-only investigation.

input.command: tmux ls 2>/dev/null || echo "no tmux server / no sessions"

content.content:
1	---
2	name: overseer
3	description: >-
4	  Oversee multiple livespec tmux sessions, each running a different track of
5	  work, and keep them all moving. Kicks off tracks from session=prompt pairs,
6	  watches for stalls/idle/context-limits, makes safe decisions autonomously,
7	  surfaces only genuinely unavoidable gates, and NEVER parks one session waiting
8	  on another. Periodically prints a status table (track, prompt, epic, status,
9	  %complete). LOCAL-ONLY to this repo — not part of the plugin, not synced.
10	---
11	
12	# Overseer — drive multiple livespec tmux sessions in parallel
13	
14	You are the **overseer**: a coordinator session that keeps several other
15	Claude Code sessions (each in its own tmux window) productively working
16	different tracks. You do *not* do the track work yourself — you dispatch it,
17	watch it, unblock it, hand it off when context fills, and report.
18	
19	This skill exists because doing this by hand failed in two specific ways that
20	must never recur. Read the **Prime directive** and **Anti-stall laws** first;
21	they are the whole point.
22	
23	---
24	
25	## API / invocation
26	
27	```
28	/overseer <session>=<prompt> [<session>=<prompt> ...]
29	```
30	
31	Each space-delimited pair is **`<tmux-session-name>=<kickoff-prompt-path>`**.
32	
33	- `/overseer livespec2=prompts/zs22-handoff.md livespec3=prompts/other-track.md`
34	  — register two tracks and kick each off.
35	- **Bare session, no `=prompt`** (e.g. `livespec4`) → ask the maintainer for its
36	  prompt before starting it; don't guess.
37	- **No args at all** → ask which sessions + prompts to oversee.
38	- **Ad-hoc additions mid-run**: at any time the maintainer can say
39	  "add `livespec5=prompts/x.md`" (or just describe it). Register it, kick it
40	  off, add it to the table. The overseer loop must keep accepting new pairs.
41	- A "prompt" is a handoff/runbook file the session reads to start its track —
42	  you start it by sending the session `run <prompt-path>` (see **Kicking off**).
43	
44	Maintain a **registry** of every track for the table (keep it in your working
45	context; optionally mirror to `tmp/overseer/registry.json` — `tmp/` is
46	maintainer-owned, so use the scoped `tmp/overseer/` subdir, never the root).
47	Registry row per track:
48	
49	```
50	{ session, prompt, session_id, epic_id, status, pct, last_seen }
51	```
52	
53	---
54	
55	## Prime directive
56	
57	**Keep every track moving.** Two failure modes, both unacceptable:
58	
59	1. **A session stalls** — sits idle/waiting and nobody notices. (Caught late = wasted hours.)
60	2. **You stall everything** — you block the overseer loop on a human decision, and while you wait, *no* session can be watched or unblocked. **This is worse**, and it is exactly what happened: a single blocking question froze *all* sessions overnight while the maintainer was away.
61	
62	Everything below is in service of preventing both.
63	
64	---
65	
66	## The anti-stall laws (non-negotiable)
67	
68	1. **Never block the overseer loop on a human answer while other sessions are live.**
69	   While you are waiting for a human (e.g. an `AskUserQuestion`), every background
70	   watcher is frozen and you cannot unblock *any* session. A pending question is a
71	   total monitoring outage. Treat blocking the maintainer as a last resort.
72	
73	2. **Decide-and-inform beats ask-and-wait.** For anything reversible/recoverable or
74	   clearly within established intent, **make the call yourself and tell the maintainer
75	   what you did** — don't gate. Reserve genuine asks for *irreversible / outward-facing
76	   / values* calls (publishing, destroying unrecoverable state, product direction).
77	
78	3. **Before any unavoidable human gate, make every OTHER session self-sustaining first.**
79	   If track A genuinely needs the human, send tracks B/C/… a "continue autonomously"
80	   directive *before* you surface A's question, so they keep working while A waits.
81	   Better still: surface A's question in a way that does **not** block your loop
82	   (state it, set A to a safe holding state, keep watching B/C, and act on A when the
83	   answer arrives) rather than via a hard-blocking prompt.
84	
85	4. **Never park a session for a non-blocker.** If a session goes idle having flagged
86	   something it *could* resolve itself ("worth your call when you're back…"), tell it
87	   to **track the item and continue**. Don't let "nice-to-flag" become "stop and wait."
88	
89	5. **Sessions run autonomously by default.** Every kickoff/handoff prompt should tell
90	   the session: own the work, gate only on a *genuine* architectural/intent blocker or
91	   an unauthorized destructive op, track flaggable side-issues and keep going, and hand
92	   off at ~50% context. When a session idles on a non-blocker, re-send that directive.
93	
94	6. **The human may be away for hours.** Optimize for maximum unattended progress. A
95	   genuine must-have-human decision may park *one* track until they return — it must
96	   **never** park the others, and you must surface it without freezing yourself.
97	
98	### Decisions you make yourself (do NOT ask)
99	
100	- A session force-pushing **its own** branch to update **its own** PR after a clean
101	  rebase. (Standard. The force-push *prohibition* is only about **another** session's
102	  branch/worktree — the cross-boundary reach that caused the collision incident.)
103	- Disposing a flagged side-issue the session already leaned on ("keep it separate /
104	  track it in its own work-item / don't expand scope").
105	- "Continue the plan / proceed to the next milestone / resume after an idle."
106	- Choosing the conservative option a session already recommended.
107	- The wrap-up→`/clear`→handoff cycle at a context/completion boundary.
108	
109	### Decisions you genuinely surface (but without freezing the loop)
110	
111	- Modifying / overwriting **another** owner's in-flight work.
112	- Anything outward-facing or irreversible the maintainer hasn't pre-authorized.
113	- A real product/values fork with no conservative default.
114	- Force-pushing / `reset --hard` / `branch -D` on a branch the session **didn't create**.
115	
116	When you must surface one: set the affected track to a clean holding state, tell the
117	*other* tracks to continue, then present the decision. Prefer presenting it as
118	"here's what I'm doing unless you object" over a hard gate whenever the action is
119	recoverable.
120	
121	---
122	
123	## Cross-repo maintainer actions — prompt-and-track (standing order)
124	
125	When a maintainer-gated action must be performed in **another repo** (not the
126	overseer's host repo) — an owner-only GitHub setting, a secret/host mutation,
127	any step only the maintainer can take — do NOT merely surface it as a queue
128	note. Convert it into a self-documenting, committed, tracked unit of work:
129	
130	1. **Write a self-contained run-prompt INTO that repo's `prompts/` directory.**
131	   It MUST (a) walk the maintainer through every owner-only manual step in
132	   concrete, copy-pasteable detail, (b) drive the rest of the work
133	   autonomously once the manual step is done, and (c) end by reporting back so
134	   the overseer can resume any dependent tracks. Make it cold-startable like any
135	   other handoff (derive status from the ledger; no shadow queue).
136	2. **Land it via that repo's `worktree → PR → rebase-merge` discipline** — the
137	   same standards used fleet-wide; `mise exec -- git`, never `--no-verify`,
138	   doc-only (`docs(...)`) so it skips the TDD ritual. Auto-merge may be off on a
139	   given repo — merge manually on green.
140	3. **Spin up a dedicated tracking session named EXACTLY `livespec-<repo-name>`**
141	   (e.g. `livespec-console-beads-fabro`) that runs the prompt and tracks the
142	   work to completion, then fold it into the watcher + status table like any
143	   other track. This is the one naming exception to the `livespecN` rule below:
144	   cross-repo tracking sessions are repo-named.
145	
146	The result: every cross-repo maintainer action is committed (survives a crash),
147	walks the human through exactly what only they can do, and is tracked in its own
148	session rather than as a fragile in-context overseer note. **Landing the prompt
149	is the durable artifact; spinning up the tracking session is a SEPARATE go** —
150	do it only when the maintainer is ready to run the work, not automatically.
151	
152	---
153	
154	## The operating loop
155	
156	1. **Register & kick off** each `session=prompt` pair (see **Kicking off**).
157	2. **Arm the watcher** (see **The watcher**). It samples each session, detects
158	   stalls/context/epic-closure, and exits on a trigger or a ~6-min heartbeat.
159	3. **On every watcher notification** (trigger *or* heartbeat): re-read state, act on
160	   anything that needs it (unblock idle, hand off at ~50%, surface a true gate without
161	   blocking), **re-arm the watcher**, and — every few heartbeats or on request —
162	   **print the status table**.
163	4. Keep accepting ad-hoc `session=prompt` additions and maintainer steers.
164	5. A track is **done** when its epic closes; drop it from active watching (still list
165	   it as done in the table).
166	
167	> The harness reaps long background tasks after a few minutes — that's fine. Each
168	> reap/exit notification re-invokes you; you re-check and re-arm. That cadence *is* the
169	> heartbeat. The only thing that breaks it is **you** being blocked (law 1).
170	
171	---
172	
173	## Kicking off a track
174	
175	A session pane sits at an empty `❯` prompt when idle. To start (or hand off) a track:
176	
177	```bash
178	# inject a line and submit it
179	command tmux send-keys -t <session> -l "run prompts/<name>.md"
180	sleep 0.6
181	command tmux send-keys -t <session> Enter
182	```
183	
184	- Always use **`command tmux`** (bypasses zsh plugin shims that swallow `tmux`).
185	- **Long messages** get collapsed by the TUI to `[Pasted text]` and a single Enter may
186	  not submit. After sending, capture the pane; if the input box still shows
187	  `❯ [Pasted text…]` (non-empty), send **Enter again**. Verify submission by grepping
188	  the session transcript for a unique phrase from your message (see paths below).
189	- Avoid `!` (zsh history expansion) and `$`/backticks in `-l` payloads.
190	
191	If the pair names a session that's mid-work on the *wrong* thing, or you're handing off
192	a fresh milestone, do the **context-handoff cycle** below instead of a bare `run`.
193	
194	---
195	
196	## Detecting busy vs idle/stalled (pane-based — robust)
197	
198	Read a pane with `command tmux capture-pane -p -t <session>`.
199	
200	- **BUSY** if the last lines show a spinner or interrupt hint: `esc to interrupt`, an
201	  elapsed-time token like `· 6m 48s ·`, or any of the rotating spinner words
202	  (`Vibing`, `Unfurling`, `Moonwalking`, `Churning`, `Bunning`, `thinking`, …).
203	- **IDLE/WAITING** if no spinner and an empty `❯` box.
204	- **STALLED** if idle for **≥ ~2 min** (≈5 samples at 25s) — it's waiting for input or
205	  has nothing queued. Investigate: read its last assistant message (transcript) to see
206	  *why* (finished? hit a decision? blocked on a gate?). Then act per the anti-stall laws.
207	- **HUNG** (rare): pane shows a spinner but the transcript mtime hasn't advanced in
208	  minutes. Inspect; consider an `Escape` + re-prompt.
209	
210	Pane-based detection survives `/clear` (session-id changes) because it tracks the
211	**tmux pane name**, not the id. Make stall detection the backbone of the watcher.
212	
213	---
214	
215	## Measuring context % (the 50% rule)
216	
217	These are **1M-context** sessions (Opus 4.8). 50% = **500k tokens**. Wrap up / hand off
218	as a session **approaches ~48–50%** so the handoff itself has headroom.
219	
220	Read the latest usage from the session's transcript JSONL:
221	
222	- Path: `~/.claude/projects/-data-projects-livespec/<session-id>*.jsonl`
223	- Context size = last assistant message's
224	  `usage.input_tokens + cache_read_input_tokens + cache_creation_input_tokens`.
225	
226	```python
227	import json, glob
228	u=None
229	for line in open(glob.glob("/home/ubuntu/.claude/projects/-data-projects-livespec/<id>*.jsonl")[0]):
230	    line=line.strip()
231	    if not line: continue
232	    try: r=json.loads(line)
233	    except: continue
234	    m=r.get("message",{})
235	    if isinstance(m,dict) and isinstance(m.get("usage"),dict): u=m["usage"]
236	ctx = u["input_tokens"]+u["cache_read_input_tokens"]+u["cache_creation_input_tokens"]
237	```
238	
239	**Resolving pane → session id** (needed for context; Claude does NOT keep the
240	transcript fd open, so you can't bind by open files): pick the **most-recently-written**
241	transcript that contains a marker unique to the track (the epic id / milestone keyword)
242	**excluding your own overseer session id and any other known session ids**. This is the
243	one real footgun: your own overseer transcript discusses every track, so it will match
244	naively — **always exclude self**. After a `/clear`, the session gets a **new id**;
245	re-resolve.
246	
247	**Completion exception to the 50% rule:** a session that is *finishing* its track (about
248	to close its epic) should be allowed to run past 50% — finishing IS the wrap-up, and
249	handing off to a prompt that's about to be deleted is wasteful. But alarm if it balloons
250	(e.g. **>70%**) *without* completing.
251	
252	---
253	
254	## The context-handoff cycle (wrap up → `/clear` → run)
255	
256	When a session approaches ~50% (and is NOT in its final completion stretch), or hits a
257	clean milestone boundary with more large work ahead:
258	
259	1. **Tell it to wrap up**: land in-flight work, **refresh its handoff prompt** to the
260	   current state (what's done, what's next, any carry-overs), land that refresh via the
261	   repo's `worktree → PR → rebase-merge` flow, and then reply with an exact signal phrase
262	   (e.g. `WRAP-UP COMPLETE -- ready for context clear`).
263	2. **Verify** the wrap-up actually landed before clearing — handoff PR merged, ledger
264	   updated, primary clean. Don't trust the session's summary; check git/ledger directly.
265	   `/clear` is irreversible to context, so confirm first.
266	3. **Clear**: `command tmux send-keys -t <session> -l "/clear"` → `Enter`. Confirm the
267	   fresh welcome banner appears and the box is empty.
268	4. **Run the refreshed prompt**: `send-keys … -l "run <handoff-prompt>"` → `Enter`.
269	5. **Re-resolve** the new session id for context tracking.
270	
271	---
272	
273	## The watcher (background sampler)
274	
275	Run a short background loop that samples both panes + context + epic status, exits on
276	any trigger or a ~6-min heartbeat, and writes a snapshot you read on the notification.
277	Template (adapt the session names, epic id, and thresholds):
278	
279	```bash
280	out="<scratchpad>/stallwatch.log"
281	TR="/home/ubuntu/.claude/projects/-data-projects-livespec"
282	# resolve a tracked session id by marker, EXCLUDING self + known others:
283	SID=$(grep -lE '<EPIC-OR-MILESTONE-MARKER>' $TR/*.jsonl 2>/dev/null \
284	      | grep -vE '<SELF_ID>|<OTHER_KNOWN_IDS>' | xargs -r ls -t 2>/dev/null | head -1)
285	busy(){ printf '%s\n' "$1" | tail -6 | grep -qiE 'esc to interrupt|· [0-9]+m? ?[0-9]*s ·|Vibing|Unfurling|Moonwalk|Churn|Bunning|thinking|Reading|Running [0-9]' && echo 1 || echo 0; }
286	ctxfile(){ python3 -c "import json,sys;u=None
287	for l in open(sys.argv[1]):
288	 l=l.strip()
289	 if not l: continue
290	 try: r=json.loads(l)
291	 except: continue
292	 m=r.get('message',{})
293	 if isinstance(m,dict) and isinstance(m.get('usage'),dict): u=m['usage']
294	print((u.get('input_tokens',0)+u.get('cache_read_input_tokens',0)+u.get('cache_creation_input_tokens',0)) if u else 0)" "$1" 2>/dev/null; }
295	# epic status badge (parse the badge, NOT any substring — 'disclosed' contains 'closed'):
296	epic(){ ( source /data/projects/1password-env-wrapper/with-livespec-env.sh bd -C /data/projects/livespec show <EPIC> 2>/dev/null ) | head -1 | grep -oE '(OPEN|CLOSED|IN_PROGRESS)\]' | head -1 | tr -d ']'; }
297	idle=0
298	for i in $(seq 1 14); do            # ~14 x 25s ≈ 6-min heartbeat
299	  sleep 25
300	  p=$(command tmux capture-pane -p -t <session> 2>/dev/null)
301	  b=$(busy "$p"); [ "$b" = 0 ] && idle=$((idle+1)) || idle=0
302	  c=$(ctxfile "$SID"); st=""; [ $((i%3)) -eq 0 ] && st=$(epic)
303	  { echo "iter $i $(date +%H:%M:%S) epic=[$st] busy=$b idle=$idle ctx=$((c/10000)).%"; \
304	    printf '%s\n' "$p" | grep -vE '^[[:space:]]*$' | tail -7; } > "$out"
305	  trig=""
306	  [ "$st" = CLOSED ] && trig="$trig EPIC-CLOSED"
307	  [ $idle -ge 5 ]   && trig="$trig STALL(~$((idle*25))s)"
308	  [ "${c:-0}" -ge 480000 ] && trig="$trig CTX-$((c/10000))%"
309	  [ -n "$trig" ] && { echo "TRIGGER:$trig" >> "$out"; break; }
310	done
311	echo "heartbeat done $(date +%H:%M:%S)" >> "$out"
312	```
313	
314	Run it with a background Bash (`run_in_background: true`); on its completion
315	notification, read the log, act, re-arm. Generalize the single-session template above to
316	loop over all registered sessions (track a per-session idle counter; trigger on any).
317	
318	**Watcher gotchas learned the hard way:**
319	- **Use the FULL session-id filename** (`<id>-….jsonl`), not the short prefix — a short
320	  name like `ab59de90.jsonl` does not exist and silently reads `0` tokens.
321	- **Exclude your own session id** from marker-based resolution or you'll measure
322	  *yourself* (every track keyword appears in the overseer transcript).
323	- **Parse the epic status badge** (`… OPEN]` / `… CLOSED]`), never a bare `grep CLOSED`
324	  (the word "disclosed" in descriptions yields false closures).
325	- The env wrapper `exec`s, so use `( source … bd … )` in a subshell per call.
326	- Don't poll a background task you launched with a *second* background task — the
327	  completion notification is the signal. (Watching *other* sessions' panes is the
328	  legitimate exception this skill is built around.)
329	
330	---
331	
332	## The status table (print periodically + on request)
333	
334	Every few heartbeats, and whenever asked, print a table of all tracks. Derive
335	`%complete` from the ledger: `bd show <epic>` lists children with ✓/◐/○ glyphs — use
336	`closed_children / total_children` (the epic line also prints `N/M complete (P%)`).
337	
338	```
339	TRACK / SESSION     PROMPT                              EPIC          STATUS        CTX    ~%DONE
340	livespec2           prompts/zs22-handoff.md             livespec-zs22 working M6    37%    6/7 (86%)
341	livespec3           prompts/conformance-handoff.md      livespec-7xx  blocked:gate  41%    2/5 (40%)
342	livespec-runtime    prompts/ai-convention.md            livespec-hso8 completing    56%    4/5 (80%)
343	```
344	
345	Columns: **track/session**, **prompt**, **epic id**, **status** (working / idle /
346	stalled / blocked:<why> / completing / done), **context %**, **est %complete**
347	(closed/total children). Add a one-line note under the table for anything needing the
348	maintainer's eventual attention — but keep working; the note is not a gate.
349	
350	---
351	
352	## Verify before you act (trust, but check)
353	
354	- Before `/clear`: confirm the wrap-up landed (handoff PR **merged**, ledger updated,
355	  primary clean on `master`). `/clear` is irreversible to context.
356	- Before authorizing a force-push: confirm it's the session's **own** branch (the one it
357	  created), not another's.
358	- Read git / PR / ledger state **directly** (`git show origin/master:…`, `gh pr view`,
359	  `bd show`) rather than trusting a session's self-summary.
360	- When a session reports "done," verify the epic actually closed before marking it done.
361	
362	---
363	
364	## Boundaries to enforce on the sessions you drive
365	
366	- **Worktree ownership**: every session/subagent operates only in the worktree it
367	  created; never `cd`/commit/push/PR into another track's worktree or branch; never
368	  force-push a branch it didn't create. (This is the collision that started all of this —
369	  see work-item `livespec-9msu`.)
370	- When a session dispatches its own subagents, its brief must carry that fence verbatim
371	  and must not run them unmonitored.
372	- Own-branch force-push to update an own-PR after a rebase is fine and pre-authorized by
373	  you (the overseer); a not-owned branch is never, without explicit maintainer sign-off.
374	
375	---
376	
377	## House rules (this repo)
378	
379	- Repo mutations go `worktree → PR → rebase-merge`; never commit on the primary checkout;
380	  never `--no-verify`. (The sessions know this; don't undercut it.) **This skill itself
381	  was landed that way.**
382	- Beads via the env wrapper: `source /data/projects/1password-env-wrapper/with-livespec-env.sh bd -C /data/projects/livespec <args>`.
383	- Secrets are probe-only (`printenv NAME | wc -c`); never echo values.
384	- Scratch under `tmp/overseer/` (never the `tmp/` root; it's maintainer-owned).
385	
386	---
387	
388	## This skill is local-only
389	
390	It lives at `.claude/skills/overseer/SKILL.md` in *this* repo and is **not** part of the
391	livespec plugin, the spec, the copier template, or any fleet-propagated surface — do not
392	add it to manifests, conformance checks, or other repos. It's a maintainer tool we may
393	evolve in place.
394

file.content:
---
name: overseer
description: >-
  Oversee multiple livespec tmux sessions, each running a different track of
  work, and keep them all moving. Kicks off tracks from session=prompt pairs,
  watches for stalls/idle/context-limits, makes safe decisions autonomously,
  surfaces only genuinely unavoidable gates, and NEVER parks one session waiting
  on another. Periodically prints a status table (track, prompt, epic, status,
  %complete). LOCAL-ONLY to this repo — not part of the plugin, not synced.
---

# Overseer — drive multiple livespec tmux sessions in parallel

You are the **overseer**: a coordinator session that keeps several other
Claude Code sessions (each in its own tmux window) productively working
different tracks. You do *not* do the track work yourself — you dispatch it,
watch it, unblock it, hand it off when context fills, and report.

This skill exists because doing this by hand failed in two specific ways that
must never recur. Read the **Prime directive** and **Anti-stall laws** first;
they are the whole point.

---

## API / invocation

```
/overseer <session>=<prompt> [<session>=<prompt> ...]
```

Each space-delimited pair is **`<tmux-session-name>=<kickoff-prompt-path>`**.

- `/overseer livespec2=prompts/zs22-handoff.md livespec3=prompts/other-track.md`
  — register two tracks and kick each off.
- **Bare session, no `=prompt`** (e.g. `livespec4`) → ask the maintainer for its
  prompt before starting it; don't guess.
- **No args at all** → ask which sessions + prompts to oversee.
- **Ad-hoc additions mid-run**: at any time the maintainer can say
  "add `livespec5=prompts/x.md`" (or just describe it). Register it, kick it
  off, add it to the table. The overseer loop must keep accepting new pairs.
- A "prompt" is a handoff/runbook file the session reads to start its track —
  you start it by sending the session `run <prompt-path>` (see **Kicking off**).

Maintain a **registry** of every track for the table (keep it in your working
context; optionally mirror to `tmp/overseer/registry.json` — `tmp/` is
maintainer-owned, so use the scoped `tmp/overseer/` subdir, never the root).
Registry row per track:

```
{ session, prompt, session_id, epic_id, status, pct, last_seen }
```

---

## Prime directive

**Keep every track moving.** Two failure modes, both unacceptable:

1. **A session stalls** — sits idle/waiting and nobody notices. (Caught late = wasted hours.)
2. **You stall everything** — you block the overseer loop on a human decision, and while you wait, *no* session can be watched or unblocked. **This is worse**, and it is exactly what happened: a single blocking question froze *all* sessions overnight while the maintainer was away.

Everything below is in service of preventing both.

---

## The anti-stall laws (non-negotiable)

1. **Never block the overseer loop on a human answer while other sessions are live.**
   While you are waiting for a human (e.g. an `AskUserQuestion`), every background
   watcher is frozen and you cannot unblock *any* session. A pending question is a
   total monitoring outage. Treat blocking the maintainer as a last resort.

2. **Decide-and-inform beats ask-and-wait.** For anything reversible/recoverable or
   clearly within established intent, **make the call yourself and tell the maintainer
   what you did** — don't gate. Reserve genuine asks for *irreversible / outward-facing
   / values* calls (publishing, destroying unrecoverable state, product direction).

3. **Before any unavoidable human gate, make every OTHER session self-sustaining first.**
   If track A genuinely needs the human, send tracks B/C/… a "continue autonomously"
   directive *before* you surface A's question, so they keep working while A waits.
   Better still: surface A's question in a way that does **not** block your loop
   (state it, set A to a safe holding state, keep watching B/C, and act on A when the
   answer arrives) rather than via a hard-blocking prompt.

4. **Never park a session for a non-blocker.** If a session goes idle having flagged
   something it *could* resolve itself ("worth your call when you're back…"), tell it
   to **track the item and continue**. Don't let "nice-to-flag" become "stop and wait."

5. **Sessions run autonomously by default.** Every kickoff/handoff prompt should tell
   the session: own the work, gate only on a *genuine* architectural/intent blocker or
   an unauthorized destructive op, track flaggable side-issues and keep going, and hand
   off at ~50% context. When a session idles on a non-blocker, re-send that directive.

6. **The human may be away for hours.** Optimize for maximum unattended progress. A
   genuine must-have-human decision may park *one* track until they return — it must
   **never** park the others, and you must surface it without freezing yourself.

### Decisions you make yourself (do NOT ask)

- A session force-pushing **its own** branch to update **its own** PR after a clean
  rebase. (Standard. The force-push *prohibition* is only about **another** session's
  branch/worktree — the cross-boundary reach that caused the collision incident.)
- Disposing a flagged side-issue the session already leaned on ("keep it separate /
  track it in its own work-item / don't expand scope").
- "Continue the plan / proceed to the next milestone / resume after an idle."
- Choosing the conservative option a session already recommended.
- The wrap-up→`/clear`→handoff cycle at a context/completion boundary.

### Decisions you genuinely surface (but without freezing the loop)

- Modifying / overwriting **another** owner's in-flight work.
- Anything outward-facing or irreversible the maintainer hasn't pre-authorized.
- A real product/values fork with no conservative default.
- Force-pushing / `reset --hard` / `branch -D` on a branch the session **didn't create**.

When you must surface one: set the affected track to a clean holding state, tell the
*other* tracks to continue, then present the decision. Prefer presenting it as
"here's what I'm doing unless you object" over a hard gate whenever the action is
recoverable.

---

## Cross-repo maintainer actions — prompt-and-track (standing order)

When a maintainer-gated action must be performed in **another repo** (not the
overseer's host repo) — an owner-only GitHub setting, a secret/host mutation,
any step only the maintainer can take — do NOT merely surface it as a queue
note. Convert it into a self-documenting, committed, tracked unit of work:

1. **Write a self-contained run-prompt INTO that repo's `prompts/` directory.**
   It MUST (a) walk the maintainer through every owner-only manual step in
   concrete, copy-pasteable detail, (b) drive the rest of the work
   autonomously once the manual step is done, and (c) end by reporting back so
   the overseer can resume any dependent tracks. Make it cold-startable like any
   other handoff (derive status from the ledger; no shadow queue).
2. **Land it via that repo's `worktree → PR → rebase-merge` discipline** — the
   same standards used fleet-wide; `mise exec -- git`, never `--no-verify`,
   doc-only (`docs(...)`) so it skips the TDD ritual. Auto-merge may be off on a
   given repo — merge manually on green.
3. **Spin up a dedicated tracking session named EXACTLY `livespec-<repo-name>`**
   (e.g. `livespec-console-beads-fabro`) that runs the prompt and tracks the
   work to completion, then fold it into the watcher + status table like any
   other track. This is the one naming exception to the `livespecN` rule below:
   cross-repo tracking sessions are repo-named.

The result: every cross-repo maintainer action is committed (survives a crash),
walks the human through exactly what only they can do, and is tracked in its own
session rather than as a fragile in-context overseer note. **Landing the prompt
is the durable artifact; spinning up the tracking session is a SEPARATE go** —
do it only when the maintainer is ready to run the work, not automatically.

---

## The operating loop

1. **Register & kick off** each `session=prompt` pair (see **Kicking off**).
2. **Arm the watcher** (see **The watcher**). It samples each session, detects
   stalls/context/epic-closure, and exits on a trigger or a ~6-min heartbeat.
3. **On every watcher notification** (trigger *or* heartbeat): re-read state, act on
   anything that needs it (unblock idle, hand off at ~50%, surface a true gate without
   blocking), **re-arm the watcher**, and — every few heartbeats or on request —
   **print the status table**.
4. Keep accepting ad-hoc `session=prompt` additions and maintainer steers.
5. A track is **done** when its epic closes; drop it from active watching (still list
   it as done in the table).

> The harness reaps long background tasks after a few minutes — that's fine. Each
> reap/exit notification re-invokes you; you re-check and re-arm. That cadence *is* the
> heartbeat. The only thing that breaks it is **you** being blocked (law 1).

---

## Kicking off a track

A session pane sits at an empty `❯` prompt when idle. To start (or hand off) a track:

```bash
# inject a line and submit it
command tmux send-keys -t <session> -l "run prompts/<name>.md"
sleep 0.6
command tmux send-keys -t <session> Enter
```

- Always use **`command tmux`** (bypasses zsh plugin shims that swallow `tmux`).
- **Long messages** get collapsed by the TUI to `[Pasted text]` and a single Enter may
  not submit. After sending, capture the pane; if the input box still shows
  `❯ [Pasted text…]` (non-empty), send **Enter again**. Verify submission by grepping
  the session transcript for a unique phrase from your message (see paths below).
- Avoid `!` (zsh history expansion) and `$`/backticks in `-l` payloads.

If the pair names a session that's mid-work on the *wrong* thing, or you're handing off
a fresh milestone, do the **context-handoff cycle** below instead of a bare `run`.

---

## Detecting busy vs idle/stalled (pane-based — robust)

Read a pane with `command tmux capture-pane -p -t <session>`.

- **BUSY** if the last lines show a spinner or interrupt hint: `esc to interrupt`, an
  elapsed-time token like `· 6m 48s ·`, or any of the rotating spinner words
  (`Vibing`, `Unfurling`, `Moonwalking`, `Churning`, `Bunning`, `thinking`, …).
- **IDLE/WAITING** if no spinner and an empty `❯` box.
- **STALLED** if idle for **≥ ~2 min** (≈5 samples at 25s) — it's waiting for input or
  has nothing queued. Investigate: read its last assistant message (transcript) to see
  *why* (finished? hit a decision? blocked on a gate?). Then act per the anti-stall laws.
- **HUNG** (rare): pane shows a spinner but the transcript mtime hasn't advanced in
  minutes. Inspect; consider an `Escape` + re-prompt.

Pane-based detection survives `/clear` (session-id changes) because it tracks the
**tmux pane name**, not the id. Make stall detection the backbone of the watcher.

---

## Measuring context % (the 50% rule)

These are **1M-context** sessions (Opus 4.8). 50% = **500k tokens**. Wrap up / hand off
as a session **approaches ~48–50%** so the handoff itself has headroom.

Read the latest usage from the session's transcript JSONL:

- Path: `~/.claude/projects/-data-projects-livespec/<session-id>*.jsonl`
- Context size = last assistant message's
  `usage.input_tokens + cache_read_input_tokens + cache_creation_input_tokens`.

```python
import json, glob
u=None
for line in open(glob.glob("/home/ubuntu/.claude/projects/-data-projects-livespec/<id>*.jsonl")[0]):
    line=line.strip()
    if not line: continue
    try: r=json.loads(line)
    except: continue
    m=r.get("message",{})
    if isinstance(m,dict) and isinstance(m.get("usage"),dict): u=m["usage"]
ctx = u["input_tokens"]+u["cache_read_input_tokens"]+u["cache_creation_input_tokens"]
```

**Resolving pane → session id** (needed for context; Claude does NOT keep the
transcript fd open, so you can't bind by open files): pick the **most-recently-written**
transcript that contains a marker unique to the track (the epic id / milestone keyword)
**excluding your own overseer session id and any other known session ids**. This is the
one real footgun: your own overseer transcript discusses every track, so it will match
naively — **always exclude self**. After a `/clear`, the session gets a **new id**;
re-resolve.

**Completion exception to the 50% rule:** a session that is *finishing* its track (about
to close its epic) should be allowed to run past 50% — finishing IS the wrap-up, and
handing off to a prompt that's about to be deleted is wasteful. But alarm if it balloons
(e.g. **>70%**) *without* completing.

---

## The context-handoff cycle (wrap up → `/clear` → run)

When a session approaches ~50% (and is NOT in its final completion stretch), or hits a
clean milestone boundary with more large work ahead:

1. **Tell it to wrap up**: land in-flight work, **refresh its handoff prompt** to the
   current state (what's done, what's next, any carry-overs), land that refresh via the
   repo's `worktree → PR → rebase-merge` flow, and then reply with an exact signal phrase
   (e.g. `WRAP-UP COMPLETE -- ready for context clear`).
2. **Verify** the wrap-up actually landed before clearing — handoff PR merged, ledger
   updated, primary clean. Don't trust the session's summary; check git/ledger directly.
   `/clear` is irreversible to context, so confirm first.
3. **Clear**: `command tmux send-keys -t <session> -l "/clear"` → `Enter`. Confirm the
   fresh welcome banner appears and the box is empty.
4. **Run the refreshed prompt**: `send-keys … -l "run <handoff-prompt>"` → `Enter`.
5. **Re-resolve** the new session id for context tracking.

---

## The watcher (background sampler)

Run a short background loop that samples both panes + context + epic status, exits on
any trigger or a ~6-min heartbeat, and writes a snapshot you read on the notification.
Template (adapt the session names, epic id, and thresholds):

```bash
out="<scratchpad>/stallwatch.log"
TR="/home/ubuntu/.claude/projects/-data-projects-livespec"
# resolve a tracked session id by marker, EXCLUDING self + known others:
SID=$(grep -lE '<EPIC-OR-MILESTONE-MARKER>' $TR/*.jsonl 2>/dev/null \
      | grep -vE '<SELF_ID>|<OTHER_KNOWN_IDS>' | xargs -r ls -t 2>/dev/null | head -1)
busy(){ printf '%s\n' "$1" | tail -6 | grep -qiE 'esc to interrupt|· [0-9]+m? ?[0-9]*s ·|Vibing|Unfurling|Moonwalk|Churn|Bunning|thinking|Reading|Running [0-9]' && echo 1 || echo 0; }
ctxfile(){ python3 -c "import json,sys;u=None
for l in open(sys.argv[1]):
 l=l.strip()
 if not l: continue
 try: r=json.loads(l)
 except: continue
 m=r.get('message',{})
 if isinstance(m,dict) and isinstance(m.get('usage'),dict): u=m['usage']
print((u.get('input_tokens',0)+u.get('cache_read_input_tokens',0)+u.get('cache_creation_input_tokens',0)) if u else 0)" "$1" 2>/dev/null; }
# epic status badge (parse the badge, NOT any substring — 'disclosed' contains 'closed'):
epic(){ ( source /data/projects/1password-env-wrapper/with-livespec-env.sh bd -C /data/projects/livespec show <EPIC> 2>/dev/null ) | head -1 | grep -oE '(OPEN|CLOSED|IN_PROGRESS)\]' | head -1 | tr -d ']'; }
idle=0
for i in $(seq 1 14); do            # ~14 x 25s ≈ 6-min heartbeat
  sleep 25
  p=$(command tmux capture-pane -p -t <session> 2>/dev/null)
  b=$(busy "$p"); [ "$b" = 0 ] && idle=$((idle+1)) || idle=0
  c=$(ctxfile "$SID"); st=""; [ $((i%3)) -eq 0 ] && st=$(epic)
  { echo "iter $i $(date +%H:%M:%S) epic=[$st] busy=$b idle=$idle ctx=$((c/10000)).%"; \
    printf '%s\n' "$p" | grep -vE '^[[:space:]]*$' | tail -7; } > "$out"
  trig=""
  [ "$st" = CLOSED ] && trig="$trig EPIC-CLOSED"
  [ $idle -ge 5 ]   && trig="$trig STALL(~$((idle*25))s)"
  [ "${c:-0}" -ge 480000 ] && trig="$trig CTX-$((c/10000))%"
  [ -n "$trig" ] && { echo "TRIGGER:$trig" >> "$out"; break; }
done
echo "heartbeat done $(date +%H:%M:%S)" >> "$out"
```

Run it with a background Bash (`run_in_background: true`); on its completion
notification, read the log, act, re-arm. Generalize the single-session template above to
loop over all registered sessions (track a per-session idle counter; trigger on any).

**Watcher gotchas learned the hard way:**
- **Use the FULL session-id filename** (`<id>-….jsonl`), not the short prefix — a short
  name like `ab59de90.jsonl` does not exist and silently reads `0` tokens.
- **Exclude your own session id** from marker-based resolution or you'll measure
  *yourself* (every track keyword appears in the overseer transcript).
- **Parse the epic status badge** (`… OPEN]` / `… CLOSED]`), never a bare `grep CLOSED`
  (the word "disclosed" in descriptions yields false closures).
- The env wrapper `exec`s, so use `( source … bd … )` in a subshell per call.
- Don't poll a background task you launched with a *second* background task — the
  completion notification is the signal. (Watching *other* sessions' panes is the
  legitimate exception this skill is built around.)

---

## The status table (print periodically + on request)

Every few heartbeats, and whenever asked, print a table of all tracks. Derive
`%complete` from the ledger: `bd show <epic>` lists children with ✓/◐/○ glyphs — use
`closed_children / total_children` (the epic line also prints `N/M complete (P%)`).

```
TRACK / SESSION     PROMPT                              EPIC          STATUS        CTX    ~%DONE
livespec2           prompts/zs22-handoff.md             livespec-zs22 working M6    37%    6/7 (86%)
livespec3           prompts/conformance-handoff.md      livespec-7xx  blocked:gate  41%    2/5 (40%)
livespec-runtime    prompts/ai-convention.md            livespec-hso8 completing    56%    4/5 (80%)
```

Columns: **track/session**, **prompt**, **epic id**, **status** (working / idle /
stalled / blocked:<why> / completing / done), **context %**, **est %complete**
(closed/total children). Add a one-line note under the table for anything needing the
maintainer's eventual attention — but keep working; the note is not a gate.

---

## Verify before you act (trust, but check)

- Before `/clear`: confirm the wrap-up landed (handoff PR **merged**, ledger updated,
  primary clean on `master`). `/clear` is irreversible to context.
- Before authorizing a force-push: confirm it's the session's **own** branch (the one it
  created), not another's.
- Read git / PR / ledger state **directly** (`git show origin/master:…`, `gh pr view`,
  `bd show`) rather than trusting a session's self-summary.
- When a session reports "done," verify the epic actually closed before marking it done.

---

## Boundaries to enforce on the sessions you drive

- **Worktree ownership**: every session/subagent operates only in the worktree it
  created; never `cd`/commit/push/PR into another track's worktree or branch; never
  force-push a branch it didn't create. (This is the collision that started all of this —
  see work-item `livespec-9msu`.)
- When a session dispatches its own subagents, its brief must carry that fence verbatim
  and must not run them unmonitored.
- Own-branch force-push to update an own-PR after a rebase is fine and pre-authorized by
  you (the overseer); a not-owned branch is never, without explicit maintainer sign-off.

---

## House rules (this repo)

- Repo mutations go `worktree → PR → rebase-merge`; never commit on the primary checkout;
  never `--no-verify`. (The sessions know this; don't undercut it.) **This skill itself
  was landed that way.**
- Beads via the env wrapper: `source /data/projects/1password-env-wrapper/with-livespec-env.sh bd -C /data/projects/livespec <args>`.
- Secrets are probe-only (`printenv NAME | wc -c`); never echo values.
- Scratch under `tmp/overseer/` (never the `tmp/` root; it's maintainer-owned).

---

## This skill is local-only

It lives at `.claude/skills/overseer/SKILL.md` in *this* repo and is **not** part of the
livespec plugin, the spec, the copier template, or any fleet-propagated surface — do not
add it to manifests, conformance checks, or other repos. It's a maintainer tool we may
evolve in place.

file.content:
---
name: plan
description: Open or resume a durable, multi-session planning thread in `plan/<topic>/` (reasoning + a self-sufficient handoff), anchor a ledger epic, route matured pieces to the spec lifecycle or the ledger, and archive on close. Required heavyweight authored skill per livespec/SPECIFICATION/contracts.md; the Orchestrator-Plane realization of the Planning Lane. Invoke bare as `/livespec-orchestrator-beads-fabro:plan` to create or resume interactively, or `/livespec-orchestrator-beads-fabro:plan <slug>` to resume an existing thread strictly.
allowed-tools: Bash, Read, Grep, Glob, Write
---

# plan — Claude Code binding

This file is the thin Claude Code binding for the `plan` operation of
the **livespec-orchestrator-beads-fabro** plugin. The complete
harness-neutral driving prose — the planning-thread create/resume
dialogue, the reasoning-capture and handoff-refresh writes, the
matured-piece routing, the handoff self-sufficiency gate, the
archive-on-close transition, and the `livespec_orchestrator_beads_fabro.*`
package calls — is the plugin's own artifact at
`${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/prose/plan.md`. Read that prose file in full,
then execute it end-to-end, binding its harness-neutral vocabulary to
this runtime per `## Runtime bindings` below.

```bash
cat "${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/prose/plan.md"
```

This binding adds NO operation behavior of its own; all orchestration
lives in the prose.

## Runtime bindings

- **`<plugin-root>`** — the live `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}` token in this
  Claude Code skill. Any `python3 "<plugin-root>/scripts/bin/<x>.py"`
  invocation in the prose runs via the Bash tool with
  `<plugin-root>` → `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}`.
- **"ask the user" / "confirm with the user" / "surface" / "narrate" /
  "present the draft to the maintainer"** — conversational turns in this
  session (the AskUserQuestion tool or plain narration, as appropriate;
  ask one question at a time).
- **"read `<file>`"** — the Read tool. **"write `<file>`"** — the Write
  tool. **Enumerate `plan/` directories** — the Glob tool.
  **Python snippets** and **`git` checks** — run via the Bash tool
  against the bundled `livespec_orchestrator_beads_fabro` package (the
  wrappers self-bootstrap the import path).
- **"fresh-context reader" (the cold-open readiness test)** — the
  Task/Agent sub-agent facility when this runtime exposes one to skills
  (launch a reader that opens ONLY the handoff and its read-first
  chain); otherwise a deliberately cleared re-read via the Read tool
  that consults only those paths and ignores conversational context.
- **"the `list-work-items` / `next` operation"** — the
  `/livespec-orchestrator-beads-fabro:list-work-items` and
  `/livespec-orchestrator-beads-fabro:next` skills in this plugin (the
  read-only status surface).
- **"the `capture-work-item` operation"** — the
  `/livespec-orchestrator-beads-fabro:capture-work-item` skill in this
  plugin (the epic-anchor and child-work-item filing seam).
- **"the `propose-change` operation"** — the cross-boundary
  `/livespec:propose-change` skill of the **livespec** plugin (the
  matured-to-spec handoff target).

content.content:
1	# plan
2	
3	Harness-neutral driving prose for the `plan` operation, per
4	`SPECIFICATION/constraints.md` §"Skill orchestration constraints":
5	this artifact is the plugin-owned LLM-facing half of the operation —
6	the planning-thread create/resume dialogue, the reasoning-capture and
7	handoff-refresh writes, the matured-piece routing, the handoff
8	self-sufficiency gate, and the archive-on-close transition. Each
9	per-runtime SKILL.md is a THIN binding that resolves the plugin root,
10	reads this prose in full, and maps its harness-neutral vocabulary (the
11	`<plugin-root>` token, the "ask the user" / "read the file" / "write
12	the file" / "fresh-context reader" verbs, the named sibling operations)
13	to that runtime's tools. Nothing in this file names a specific agent
14	runtime's tools or command namespace.
15	
16	`plan` is the Orchestrator-Plane realization of the **Planning Lane** —
17	the durable, multi-session *planning* work that decides what should
18	become spec, implementation, or research before any of those lanes is
19	committed to. It realizes the repo-agnostic pattern that
20	`livespec`'s `non-functional-requirements.md` §"Planning Lane guidance"
21	carries (the same cut as grooming: core records the pattern, this
22	plugin realizes it), and the contract for this realization is
23	`SPECIFICATION/contracts.md` §"Planning Lane realization". Unlike the
24	one-shot `capture-*` family, a planning thread is **stateful and
25	re-entered** for the same topic, like `groom`.
26	
27	## Pre-requisites
28	
29	- The `livespec-orchestrator-beads-fabro` Python package is on the
30	  import path (the wrappers self-bootstrap it).
31	- A reachable work-items store (a planning thread anchors a ledger
32	  epic and routes ripe work into the ledger).
33	- `livespec` installed (a matured-reasoning piece routes to the
34	  `propose-change` operation).
35	- A `plan/` directory at the project root is the thread store; the
36	  operation creates it on first use.
37	
38	## The planning-thread store
39	
40	A planning thread is a first-class directory **`plan/<topic>/`** holding
41	two facets:
42	
43	- **At most one handoff** — the reserved filename
44	  `plan/<topic>/handoff.md`, the single resumable execution-coordination
45	  point for the thread. Only one resumption point may be active per
46	  topic; a second handoff (any other `handoff*.md`) is malformed and
47	  this operation refuses to create it.
48	- **Zero or more research files** — durable reasoning ("why this
49	  shape"). A single note MAY sit directly in `plan/<topic>/`; multiple
50	  sub-topic notes live under `plan/<topic>/research/` (the directory
51	  carries the topic, so the filenames do not repeat it). A young thread
52	  MAY be research-only (no handoff yet).
53	
54	The broader `research/` tree stays for standalone analysis that is not
55	an active planning thread. Archived threads live under
56	`plan/archive/<topic>/`.
57	
58	## Flow
59	
60	### Step 1 — Resolve the invocation mode
61	
62	This operation has two entry modes, fixed by whether a `<slug>`
63	argument was supplied:
64	
65	- **No argument → interactive entry** (Step 2). Used to resume an open
66	  thread or start a new one.
67	- **`<slug>` argument → strict resume** (Step 3, against an existing
68	  thread only). Deterministic: it MUST match an existing `plan/<slug>/`
69	  exactly. If no such directory exists, FAIL HARD with an error that
70	  lists the existing slugs (the open threads). There is NO fuzzy match
71	  and NO accidental create-on-typo — creation happens only through the
72	  no-argument interview path (Step 2).
73	
74	### Step 2 — Interactive entry (no argument): resume or create
75	
76	Compose the open-thread list from BOTH sources and present it:
77	
78	1. The ledger's open planning epics — via the `list-work-items`
79	   operation (`--json`), the ledger ids whose thread the human may
80	   resume. Status is READ from the ledger here; it is never stored in a
81	   planning artifact (the no-shadow-ledger rule).
82	2. The on-disk threads — the `plan/<topic>/` directories (excluding
83	   `plan/archive/`).
84	
85	Ask the user whether to **resume** one of the listed threads (→ Step 3
86	against that slug) or **start a new** thread.
87	
88	To start a new thread, ask the user to describe it in one or two
89	sentences, then **propose a canonical dash-cased slug** derived from
90	that description using the SAME canonicalization the `propose-change`
91	operation applies to a topic hint: lowercase → replace every run of
92	non-`[a-z0-9]` characters with a single hyphen → strip leading and
93	trailing hyphens → truncate to 64 characters. The human never
94	hand-crafts the identifier, so there are no spaces, no over-long
95	strings, and the output is deterministic. Confirm the proposed slug
96	with the user (offer to adjust the description if the slug reads
97	poorly), then on confirmation:
98	
99	- **Create the thread directory** `plan/<slug>/` (write the initial
100	  reasoning note the user described, or leave it for a later
101	  invocation).
102	- **Anchor a ledger epic** for the thread via the `capture-work-item`
103	  operation — an `epic`-type work-item whose title names the thread.
104	  This is the thread's status anchor; its id is what the handoff and
105	  the open-thread list cite. Filing through `capture-work-item` keeps
106	  the cross-plane write per-operation consented and routes it through
107	  the one consented store-writer (never a direct cross-plane write).
108	
109	### Step 3 — Work the thread
110	
111	Within a thread (resumed by slug or just created), any of the following
112	may happen this invocation; ask the user which, one at a time:
113	
114	- **Update the reasoning.** Write or revise a research note under
115	  `plan/<slug>/` (or `plan/<slug>/research/` for a sub-topic).
116	- **Refresh the handoff.** Write or update `plan/<slug>/handoff.md`,
117	  then run the **handoff self-sufficiency gate** (Step 4) before
118	  declaring it ready. A handoff cites the thread's ledger ids
119	  read-only and composes status from `list-work-items` / `next`; it
120	  never embeds a parallel `[ ]`/`[x]` work queue that shadows the
121	  ledger.
122	- **Route a matured piece.** When a piece of the thread is ripe:
123	  - *becomes spec* → hand off to the `propose-change` operation
124	    (cross-boundary, human-accepts).
125	  - *becomes ledger work* → file it via the `capture-work-item`
126	    operation as a CHILD of the thread's epic anchor (`depends_on` /
127	    parent link as appropriate). Routing ripe work into the ledger is
128	    the second of the two one-directional seams; it always goes through
129	    `capture-work-item`, never a direct store write.
130	- **Close the thread** → archive it (Step 5).
131	
132	### Step 4 — The handoff self-sufficiency gate
133	
134	A handoff is NOT ready until it is self-sufficient: a fresh session
135	opening ONLY the handoff can execute its next action without
136	re-deriving anything. Enforce all three before declaring a handoff
137	ready; if any fails, repair the handoff and re-run the gate.
138	
139	1. **Cold-open readiness test.** Have a **fresh-context reader** (a
140	   sub-agent with no prior session context in runtimes that provide
141	   one; otherwise a deliberately cleared re-read) open ONLY the handoff
142	   and the artifacts on its read-first chain, and confirm it can
143	   proceed to the named next action without consulting chat history or
144	   re-deriving anything. If the reader cannot proceed, the handoff is
145	   not self-sufficient — fix it (commit the missing artifact, add it to
146	   the read-first chain) rather than relying on conversational context.
147	2. **One path.** The next-session command names exactly ONE path — the
148	   handoff. If the handoff's "next" text needs to ALSO list other
149	   files, that is the smell that the handoff is not self-sufficient:
150	   fold those references into the handoff's read-first chain and fix the
151	   handoff, do not list more paths.
152	3. **No dangling reference (fail-closed).** Every artifact the handoff
153	   cites — its read-first chain and its next action — MUST exist and be
154	   committed. Check each referenced path with the runtime's shell
155	   (`git ls-files --error-unmatch <path>`, or existence + tracked
156	   status); a missing or uncommitted reference FAILS the gate. Surface
157	   the offending path and stop — do not declare the handoff ready.
158	
159	### Step 5 — Archive on epic close
160	
161	A plan thread's lifecycle binds to its ledger epic: `plan/<topic>/` is
162	active if and only if its epic is open, and archived to
163	`plan/archive/<topic>/` if and only if the epic is closed. When the
164	user closes the thread, close the epic anchor (via the ledger) AND move
165	the directory:
166	
167	```text
168	git mv plan/<topic>/ plan/archive/<topic>/
169	```
170	
171	Reopening the epic unarchives it (move back). Nothing is lost — the
172	archived thread stays under `plan/archive/` and in git history; to keep
173	a research note as living reference, copy it to `research/`
174	deliberately. In spirit this is `prune-history` for planning threads:
175	the active view stays clean, completed threads move aside rather than
176	getting deleted.
177	
178	The mechanical backstops (exactly one handoff per topic; `archived`
179	matches `epic-closed`) are five-slot conformance concerns whose
180	always-on enforcement is realized by the Conformance Pattern, not by
181	this operation; this prose enforces them behaviorally (it writes only
182	the reserved `handoff.md`, and it archives on close).
183	
184	## Important properties
185	
186	- **Status is derived, never stored** — the open-thread list and every
187	  handoff compose status from the ledger (`list-work-items` / `next`)
188	  as a read-only first action; no planning artifact stores a status or
189	  a shadow work queue (the no-shadow-ledger rule).
190	- **Two one-directional seams** — *prompt → ledger* is read-only (cite
191	  ids, compose status); *plan → work* routes ripe work through the
192	  `capture-work-item` operation. Never a direct cross-plane store
193	  write.
194	- **Strict `plan <slug>`** — the argument form never creates; it
195	  resolves an existing thread or fails listing slugs. Creation is the
196	  interview path's job, with a canonical slug the human confirms.
197	- **Self-sufficient handoffs** — a handoff is declared ready only after
198	  the Step 4 gate (cold-open test + one path + no dangling reference).
199	- **No new ledger state, no new store path beyond `plan/`** — the
200	  thread anchors a plain Beads `epic` and reuses the `capture-work-item`
201	  machinery; `plan/<topic>/` and `plan/archive/<topic>/` are the only
202	  new paths.
203	
204	## What this operation does NOT do
205	
206	- Does NOT write the ledger directly — epic anchors and child work-items
207	  are filed through the `capture-work-item` operation; status is read
208	  through `list-work-items` / `next`.
209	- Does NOT create a thread from the `<slug>` argument form — that form
210	  is strict-resume-or-fail.
211	- Does NOT decompose an epic into ready slices — use the `groom`
212	  operation.
213	- Does NOT detect gaps or drift — use the `capture-impl-gaps` /
214	  `capture-spec-drift` operations.
215	- Does NOT dispatch work — the Dispatcher drains `ready` items.
216

# plan

Harness-neutral driving prose for the `plan` operation, per
`SPECIFICATION/constraints.md` §"Skill orchestration constraints":
this artifact is the plugin-owned LLM-facing half of the operation —
the planning-thread create/resume dialogue, the reasoning-capture and
handoff-refresh writes, the matured-piece routing, the handoff
self-sufficiency gate, and the archive-on-close transition. Each
per-runtime SKILL.md is a THIN binding that resolves the plugin root,
reads this prose in full, and maps its harness-neutral vocabulary (the
`<plugin-root>` token, the "ask the user" / "read the file" / "write
the file" / "fresh-context reader" verbs, the named sibling operations)
to that runtime's tools. Nothing in this file names a specific agent
runtime's tools or command namespace.

`plan` is the Orchestrator-Plane realization of the **Planning Lane** —
the durable, multi-session *planning* work that decides what should
become spec, implementation, or research before any of those lanes is
committed to. It realizes the repo-agnostic pattern that
`livespec`'s `non-functional-requirements.md` §"Planning Lane guidance"
carries (the same cut as grooming: core records the pattern, this
plugin realizes it), and the contract for this realization is
`SPECIFICATION/contracts.md` §"Planning Lane realization". Unlike the
one-shot `capture-*` family, a planning thread is **stateful and
re-entered** for the same topic, like `groom`.

## Pre-requisites

- The `livespec-orchestrator-beads-fabro` Python package is on the
  import path (the wrappers self-bootstrap it).
- A reachable work-items store (a planning thread anchors a ledger
  epic and routes ripe work into the ledger).
- `livespec` installed (a matured-reasoning piece routes to the
  `propose-change` operation).
- A `plan/` directory at the project root is the thread store; the
  operation creates it on first use.

## The planning-thread store

A planning thread is a first-class directory **`plan/<topic>/`** holding
two facets:

- **At most one handoff** — the reserved filename
  `plan/<topic>/handoff.md`, the single resumable execution-coordination
  point for the thread. Only one resumption point may be active per
  topic; a second handoff (any other `handoff*.md`) is malformed and
  this operation refuses to create it.
- **Zero or more research files** — durable reasoning ("why this
  shape"). A single note MAY sit directly in `plan/<topic>/`; multiple
  sub-topic notes live under `plan/<topic>/research/` (the directory
  carries the topic, so the filenames do not repeat it). A young thread
  MAY be research-only (no handoff yet).

The broader `research/` tree stays for standalone analysis that is not
an active planning thread. Archived threads live under
`plan/archive/<topic>/`.

## Flow

### Step 1 — Resolve the invocation mode

This operation has two entry modes, fixed by whether a `<slug>`
argument was supplied:

- **No argument → interactive entry** (Step 2). Used to resume an open
  thread or start a new one.
- **`<slug>` argument → strict resume** (Step 3, against an existing
  thread only). Deterministic: it MUST match an existing `plan/<slug>/`
  exactly. If no such directory exists, FAIL HARD with an error that
  lists the existing slugs (the open threads). There is NO fuzzy match
  and NO accidental create-on-typo — creation happens only through the
  no-argument interview path (Step 2).

### Step 2 — Interactive entry (no argument): resume or create

Compose the open-thread list from BOTH sources and present it:

1. The ledger's open planning epics — via the `list-work-items`
   operation (`--json`), the ledger ids whose thread the human may
   resume. Status is READ from the ledger here; it is never stored in a
   planning artifact (the no-shadow-ledger rule).
2. The on-disk threads — the `plan/<topic>/` directories (excluding
   `plan/archive/`).

Ask the user whether to **resume** one of the listed threads (→ Step 3
against that slug) or **start a new** thread.

To start a new thread, ask the user to describe it in one or two
sentences, then **propose a canonical dash-cased slug** derived from
that description using the SAME canonicalization the `propose-change`
operation applies to a topic hint: lowercase → replace every run of
non-`[a-z0-9]` characters with a single hyphen → strip leading and
trailing hyphens → truncate to 64 characters. The human never
hand-crafts the identifier, so there are no spaces, no over-long
strings, and the output is deterministic. Confirm the proposed slug
with the user (offer to adjust the description if the slug reads
poorly), then on confirmation:

- **Create the thread directory** `plan/<slug>/` (write the initial
  reasoning note the user described, or leave it for a later
  invocation).
- **Anchor a ledger epic** for the thread via the `capture-work-item`
  operation — an `epic`-type work-item whose title names the thread.
  This is the thread's status anchor; its id is what the handoff and
  the open-thread list cite. Filing through `capture-work-item` keeps
  the cross-plane write per-operation consented and routes it through
  the one consented store-writer (never a direct cross-plane write).

### Step 3 — Work the thread

Within a thread (resumed by slug or just created), any of the following
may happen this invocation; ask the user which, one at a time:

- **Update the reasoning.** Write or revise a research note under
  `plan/<slug>/` (or `plan/<slug>/research/` for a sub-topic).
- **Refresh the handoff.** Write or update `plan/<slug>/handoff.md`,
  then run the **handoff self-sufficiency gate** (Step 4) before
  declaring it ready. A handoff cites the thread's ledger ids
  read-only and composes status from `list-work-items` / `next`; it
  never embeds a parallel `[ ]`/`[x]` work queue that shadows the
  ledger.
- **Route a matured piece.** When a piece of the thread is ripe:
  - *becomes spec* → hand off to the `propose-change` operation
    (cross-boundary, human-accepts).
  - *becomes ledger work* → file it via the `capture-work-item`
    operation as a CHILD of the thread's epic anchor (`depends_on` /
    parent link as appropriate). Routing ripe work into the ledger is
    the second of the two one-directional seams; it always goes through
    `capture-work-item`, never a direct store write.
- **Close the thread** → archive it (Step 5).

### Step 4 — The handoff self-sufficiency gate

A handoff is NOT ready until it is self-sufficient: a fresh session
opening ONLY the handoff can execute its next action without
re-deriving anything. Enforce all three before declaring a handoff
ready; if any fails, repair the handoff and re-run the gate.

1. **Cold-open readiness test.** Have a **fresh-context reader** (a
   sub-agent with no prior session context in runtimes that provide
   one; otherwise a deliberately cleared re-read) open ONLY the handoff
   and the artifacts on its read-first chain, and confirm it can
   proceed to the named next action without consulting chat history or
   re-deriving anything. If the reader cannot proceed, the handoff is
   not self-sufficient — fix it (commit the missing artifact, add it to
   the read-first chain) rather than relying on conversational context.
2. **One path.** The next-session command names exactly ONE path — the
   handoff. If the handoff's "next" text needs to ALSO list other
   files, that is the smell that the handoff is not self-sufficient:
   fold those references into the handoff's read-first chain and fix the
   handoff, do not list more paths.
3. **No dangling reference (fail-closed).** Every artifact the handoff
   cites — its read-first chain and its next action — MUST exist and be
   committed. Check each referenced path with the runtime's shell
   (`git ls-files --error-unmatch <path>`, or existence + tracked
   status); a missing or uncommitted reference FAILS the gate. Surface
   the offending path and stop — do not declare the handoff ready.

### Step 5 — Archive on epic close

A plan thread's lifecycle binds to its ledger epic: `plan/<topic>/` is
active if and only if its epic is open, and archived to
`plan/archive/<topic>/` if and only if the epic is closed. When the
user closes the thread, close the epic anchor (via the ledger) AND move
the directory:

```text
git mv plan/<topic>/ plan/archive/<topic>/
```

Reopening the epic unarchives it (move back). Nothing is lost — the
archived thread stays under `plan/archive/` and in git history; to keep
a research note as living reference, copy it to `research/`
deliberately. In spirit this is `prune-history` for planning threads:
the active view stays clean, completed threads move aside rather than
getting deleted.

The mechanical backstops (exactly one handoff per topic; `archived`
matches `epic-closed`) are five-slot conformance concerns whose
always-on enforcement is realized by the Conformance Pattern, not by
this operation; this prose enforces them behaviorally (it writes only
the reserved `handoff.md`, and it archives on close).

## Important properties

- **Status is derived, never stored** — the open-thread list and every
  handoff compose status from the ledger (`list-work-items` / `next`)
  as a read-only first action; no planning artifact stores a status or
  a shadow work queue (the no-shadow-ledger rule).
- **Two one-directional seams** — *prompt → ledger* is read-only (cite
  ids, compose status); *plan → work* routes ripe work through the
  `capture-work-item` operation. Never a direct cross-plane store
  write.
- **Strict `plan <slug>`** — the argument form never creates; it
  resolves an existing thread or fails listing slugs. Creation is the
  interview path's job, with a canonical slug the human confirms.
- **Self-sufficient handoffs** — a handoff is declared ready only after
  the Step 4 gate (cold-open test + one path + no dangling reference).
- **No new ledger state, no new store path beyond `plan/`** — the
  thread anchors a plain Beads `epic` and reuses the `capture-work-item`
  machinery; `plan/<topic>/` and `plan/archive/<topic>/` are the only
  new paths.

## What this operation does NOT do

- Does NOT write the ledger directly — epic anchors and child work-items
  are filed through the `capture-work-item` operation; status is read
  through `list-work-items` / `next`.
- Does NOT create a thread from the `<slug>` argument form — that form
  is strict-resume-or-fail.
- Does NOT decompose an epic into ready slices — use the `groom`
  operation.
- Does NOT detect gaps or drift — use the `capture-impl-gaps` /
  `capture-spec-drift` operations.
- Does NOT dispatch work — the Dispatcher drains `ready` items.

1	# Overseer startup — drive the livespec multi-track fleet
2	
3	Run this to (re)start the **overseer** in the `livespec-overseer` tmux session.
4	Your job is NOT to do track work yourself — it is to keep every active track
5	moving in parallel: dispatch, watch, unblock, hand off at context limits, and
6	report. Load and follow the local overseer skill at
7	`.claude/skills/overseer/SKILL.md` (invoke `/overseer`); everything below is the
8	concrete track registry + the resume / cold-start contract for this fleet.
9	
10	## Prime law (do not violate)
11	
12	**Never block the overseer loop on a human answer while other sessions are
13	live.** A pending blocking prompt freezes every watcher and strands all tracks.
14	Decide-and-inform over ask-and-wait; make every other track self-sustaining
15	before you surface any unavoidable gate. (This rule exists because a single
16	blocking question once froze every session overnight.)
17	
18	## FIRST ACTION — resume what's live, cold-start what's gone
19	
20	A session may be **warm** (already running — resume it) or **gone** (after a VPS
21	crash/reboot *every* tmux session disappears — cold-start it). Handle both:
22	
23	1. Enumerate existing sessions: `command tmux ls`.
24	2. For each track in the registry below, determine its state:
25	   - **Session exists + on its track → resume in place.** Do NOT `/clear` or
26	     re-kick a warm session mid-track — you would destroy live context.
27	   - **Session missing (e.g. after a crash) → recover it**, preferring
28	     `claude --resume <session-id>` — this reloads the FULL pre-crash context.
29	     **Nothing is lost in a crash: every Claude session persists on disk**
30	     (`~/.claude/projects/<slug>/<id>.jsonl`); the tmux *window* dies, the
31	     *session* does not. Cold-start from the committed handoff (`run <handoff>`)
32	     is the alternative when you don't have the session id or want a clean
33	     restart — fine for any track whose state is externalized. See §"Cold start".
34	3. Fold in any other `livespec*` session running a livespec track not in the
35	   registry — discover and oversee it too.
36	
37	## Track registry
38	
39	As of the **2026-06-27 refresh**, most tracks have completed or parked on a
40	maintainer gate; the table below is the current disposition. A fresh overseer
41	session resumes by reading each row's status (and the ledger) — do NOT re-kick a
42	DONE track or a track parked on a maintainer gate. Each not-done track recovers
43	either by **resuming its Claude session** (`claude --resume <id>`) or by
44	**cold-starting from its committed handoff**. **The overseer itself runs in
45	`livespec-overseer`.**
46	
47	| Session | Track | Epic | Status (2026-06-27) | Recovery source |
48	|---|---|---|---|---|
49	| **livespec1** | Fleet-wide doctor-static enforcement | `livespec-6jfq` | ✅ **DONE** (closed; enforced across 6 repos; its handoff prompt was deleted) | — (complete) |
50	| **livespec2** | Register livespec-console in the fleet manifest | `livespec-zs22.7.8` | ✅ **DONE** (closed; GitHub-wiring deferred → `livespec-inxg`) | — (complete) |
51	| **livespec3** | Dev-tooling single-source convergence | `livespec-zs22.7.9` | ⏸ **PARKED** on the console-wiring deadlock (`livespec-inxg`): `.1`+`.7` landed, `.2` (PR #190 in dev-tooling) built & parked; resumes when the console is wired | handoff `prompts/dev-tooling-single-source-convergence-handoff.md` |
52	| **livespec4** | Architecture mermaid-diagram cleanup | *ad-hoc* | ✅ **DONE** (diagram revised into `spec.md` at v151; README link captured as `livespec-p2zv`) | — (complete) |
53	| **livespec5** | Governed-repo lifecycle (zs22 Increment 6) | `livespec-zs22.8` | ▶ **AT REST**: M0 + M1 done (M1 closed at v151); M2–M6 open; ready to groom **M2** on the maintainer's word | handoff `prompts/governed-repo-lifecycle-handoff.md` |
54	| **livespec-console-beads-fabro** | Console fleet-wiring (cross-repo) | `livespec-inxg` | 🅼 **PROMPT LANDED; session NOT yet spun up** — awaiting the maintainer's go | run-prompt `livespec-console-beads-fabro/prompts/console-fleet-wiring-prompt.md` |
