56	Notes:
57	- **The keystone blocker is `livespec-inxg`** (console GitHub App install +
58	  machine-fixable wiring). It deadlocks `livespec-dev-tooling` master (the
59	  fleet-conformance preflight is red), which is why **livespec3 is parked**. Its
60	  resolution is the committed run-prompt in the console repo (last row); spinning
61	  up the `livespec-console-beads-fabro` tracking session is a **separate
62	  maintainer go** (per the SKILL's cross-repo standing order). When that wiring
63	  goes green, **resume livespec3** (it finishes `.2/.3/.5/.6`).
64	- **livespec5 (`zs22.8`)**: M1 (the governed-repo-lifecycle entry point) is
65	  specified and accepted into `non-functional-requirements.md` (v151); the next
66	  ripe step is grooming **M2** (generalize `just bootstrap` → the lifecycle
67	  verb). Held at rest per scope; groom on the maintainer's word.
68	- **`zs22` parent epic** closes once `zs22.7.9` (livespec3) lands; that is gated
69	  on the console wiring.
70	- Sibling-repo sessions (`livespec-dev-tooling`, `livespec-impl-plaintext`, and
71	  the new `livespec-console-beads-fabro` tracker) are separate repos — overseen
72	  here only as noted (the console tracker is part of this fleet's work per the
73	  cross-repo standing order in the SKILL).
74	
75	## Cold start / disaster recovery (sessions gone after a crash)
76	
77	When `tmux ls` shows the track sessions are **missing** (VPS crash/reboot),
78	recreate each. First confirm the durable state recovered: the ledger is back
79	(`source /data/projects/1password-env-wrapper/with-livespec-env.sh bd -C /data/projects/livespec list`
80	works → Dolt up), `git status` is clean on `master`, the handoff prompts exist
81	on disk, and reap any crash-orphaned worktrees (`git worktree list` → remove the
82	merged ones).
83	
84	Then bring each track in §"Track registry" back, by **either** path:
85	
86	```bash
87	tmux new-session -d -s <livespecN> -c /data/projects/livespec   # create the window
88	
89	# A) RESUME the actual pre-crash session (preferred — reloads full context):
90	tmux send-keys -t <livespecN> -l 'claude --resume <session-id> --dangerously-skip-permissions'; tmux send-keys -t <livespecN> Enter
91	# if it offers "Resume from summary / Resume full session" → choose FULL for a mid-design track.
92	
93	# B) COLD-START from the handoff (handoff-driven tracks, or if you lack the id):
94	tmux send-keys -t <livespecN> -l 'claude --dangerously-skip-permissions'; tmux send-keys -t <livespecN> Enter
95	# poll the pane until ready ("bypass permissions" / a "❯" prompt), THEN:
96	tmux send-keys -t <livespecN> -l 'run <handoff-prompt>'; tmux send-keys -t <livespecN> Enter
97	```
98	
99	**Finding a session id** when you don't have it: search the transcripts —
100	`grep -l '<distinctive content>' ~/.claude/projects/-data-projects-livespec/*.jsonl` —
101	then **verify** by reading its last assistant message + token count before
102	resuming (a name like `w0c-mermaid-diagram` can be a TODO *inside* an unrelated
103	session, not its title — confirm the content matches).
104	
105	**The lesson the crash taught — enforce it:** the crash proved **nothing is
106	lost** — every Claude session is on disk and resumable. BUT resume depends on
107	*finding the right session* (a remembered name / distinctive content), which is a
108	fragile anchor. The deterministic anchor is a **committed handoff + ledger
109	entries**. `livespec4`'s mermaid track had no handoff — it was recoverable only
110	because its session was findable, which is luck, not discipline. **Every track
111	must externalize its state to a committed handoff early and often**, not only at
112	the ~50% context mark.
113	
114	## Operating loop (per the skill)
115	
116	- Arm a background watcher over the active sessions (pane-based busy/idle +
117	  per-session context% + epic-status). On each completion/heartbeat: re-read,
118	  act, **re-arm**. The harness reaps watchers after a few minutes — that's the
119	  heartbeat; just re-arm.
120	- **Stall detection:** a session idle ≥ ~2 min is waiting/stuck → read its last
121	  message, then act (unblock per the laws below).
122	- **Context:** 1M-token sessions; hand off as a track nears **~48–50%**
123	  (wrap up → refresh its handoff → `/clear` → `run <handoff>`), EXCEPT let a
124	  track that is *completing its epic* finish past 50%. Measure context from the
125	  session transcript's last `usage`; **exclude your own (`livespec-overseer`)
126	  session id** when resolving a track's session by marker, or you'll measure
127	  yourself.
128	- **Status table** (print periodically + on request): track · prompt · epic ·
129	  status · context% · %complete (closed/total children from `bd show <epic>`).
130	
131	## What you decide yourself vs. surface
132	
133	- **Decide + inform:** own-branch force-push to update an own-PR after a rebase;
134	  "keep this side-issue separate / track it and continue"; resume after idle;
135	  the conservative option a session already recommended; the wrap-up→clear→run
136	  cycle.
137	- **Surface (without freezing the loop):** modifying another owner's in-flight
138	  work; outward-facing/irreversible un-pre-authorized acts; a real product/values
139	  fork; force-push/`reset --hard`/`branch -D` on a branch the session did not
140	  create.
141	
142	## House rules
143	
144	- Repo mutations: `worktree → PR → rebase-merge`; never commit on the primary;
145	  never `--no-verify`.
146	- Worktree ownership: each session/subagent operates only in the worktree it
147	  created; never touch another track's branch/worktree/PR.
148	- Beads via the env wrapper:
149	  `source /data/projects/1password-env-wrapper/with-livespec-env.sh bd -C /data/projects/livespec <args>`.
150	- Scratch under `tmp/overseer/` (never the `tmp/` root).
151	- The numbered track sessions are `livespec1`–`livespec5` (see registry; several
152	  are now DONE). Name any **new** numbered track/session `livespec6`,
153	  `livespec7`, … `livespecN`. The overseer is `livespec-overseer`. A **cross-repo
154	  maintainer-action tracking session is named EXACTLY `livespec-<repo-name>`**
155	  (e.g. `livespec-console-beads-fabro`), per the SKILL's cross-repo standing
156	  order — and is spun up only on a separate maintainer go, after its run-prompt
157	  is landed in that repo's `prompts/`.

maintainer gate; the table below is the current disposition. A fresh overseer

| Session | Track | Epic | Status (2026-06-27) | Recovery source |
|---|---|---|---|---|

| **livespec4** | Architecture mermaid-diagram cleanup | *ad-hoc* | ✅ **DONE** (diagram revised into `spec.md` at v151; README link captured as `livespec-p2zv`) | — (complete) |
| **livespec5** | Governed-repo lifecycle (zs22 Increment 6) | `livespec-zs22.8` | ▶ **AT REST**: M0 + M1 done (M1 closed at v151); M2–M6 open; ready to groom **M2** on the maintainer's word | handoff `prompts/governed-repo-lifecycle-handoff.md` |
| **livespec-console-beads-fabro** | Console fleet-wiring (cross-repo) | `livespec-inxg` | 🅼 **PROMPT LANDED; session NOT yet spun up** — awaiting the maintainer's go | run-prompt `livespec-console-beads-fabro/prompts/console-fleet-wiring-prompt.md` |

Notes:
- **The keystone blocker is `livespec-inxg`** (console GitHub App install +

merged ones).

Then bring each track in §"Track registry" back, by **either** path:

```bash
tmux new-session -d -s <livespecN> -c /data/projects/livespec   # create the window

# A) RESUME the actual pre-crash session (preferred — reloads full context):

# if it offers "Resume from summary / Resume full session" → choose FULL for a mid-design track.

# B) COLD-START from the handoff (handoff-driven tracks, or if you lack the id):

# poll the pane until ready ("bypass permissions" / a "❯" prompt), THEN:
tmux send-keys -t <livespecN> -l 'run <handoff-prompt>'; tmux send-keys -t <livespecN> Enter
```

`grep -l '<distinctive content>' ~/.claude/projects/-data-projects-livespec/*.jsonl` —
then **verify** by reading its last assistant message + token count before
resuming (a name like `w0c-mermaid-diagram` can be a TODO *inside* an unrelated
session, not its title — confirm the content matches).

**The lesson the crash taught — enforce it:** the crash proved **nothing is
lost** — every Claude session is on disk and resumable. BUT resume depends on

fragile anchor. The deterministic anchor is a **committed handoff + ledger
entries**. `livespec4`'s mermaid track had no handoff — it was recoverable only
because its session was findable, which is luck, not discipline. **Every track
must externalize its state to a committed handoff early and often**, not only at
the ~50% context mark.

## Operating loop (per the skill)

- Arm a background watcher over the active sessions (pane-based busy/idle +
  per-session context% + epic-status). On each completion/heartbeat: re-read,
  act, **re-arm**. The harness reaps watchers after a few minutes — that's the
  heartbeat; just re-arm.
- **Stall detection:** a session idle ≥ ~2 min is waiting/stuck → read its last
  message, then act (unblock per the laws below).
- **Context:** 1M-token sessions; hand off as a track nears **~48–50%**
  (wrap up → refresh its handoff → `/clear` → `run <handoff>`), EXCEPT let a
  track that is *completing its epic* finish past 50%. Measure context from the

- **Status table** (print periodically + on request): track · prompt · epic ·
  status · context% · %complete (closed/total children from `bd show <epic>`).

## What you decide yourself vs. surface

- **Decide + inform:** own-branch force-push to update an own-PR after a rebase;
  "keep this side-issue separate / track it and continue"; resume after idle;
  the conservative option a session already recommended; the wrap-up→clear→run
  cycle.
- **Surface (without freezing the loop):** modifying another owner's in-flight
  work; outward-facing/irreversible un-pre-authorized acts; a real product/values

  create.

## House rules

- Repo mutations: `worktree → PR → rebase-merge`; never commit on the primary;

- Worktree ownership: each session/subagent operates only in the worktree it
  created; never touch another track's branch/worktree/PR.
- Beads via the env wrapper:

1	# spec.md -- livespec-console-beads-fabro
2	
3	`livespec-console-beads-fabro` is the LiveSpec-family operator console
4	for repositories whose implementation work is tracked in Beads and
5	driven through the Beads/Fabro orchestrator. It is a separate product
6	from LiveSpec core, the Beads/Fabro orchestrator, and Fabro itself.
7	
8	## Purpose
9	
10	The console gives a human operator one coherent place to answer:
11	
12	- What needs attention now?
13	- What spec-side action is pending?
14	- What implementation work is ready?
15	- What is currently in the factory?
16	- Which Fabro runs are blocked on human input?
17	- Which work is manual or host-only and must not enter Fabro?
18	- What commands can be safely issued next?
19	
20	The console is event-sourced. It consumes source facts from LiveSpec,
21	Beads, Dispatcher, Fabro, GitHub, and local repository state, translates
22	them into canonical console events, and derives operator projections such
23	as attention inboxes, cards, timelines, and repository health.
24	
25	## Scope Boundary
26	
27	The console owns:
28	
29	- canonical console events and commands
30	- source adapters and ingestion checkpoints
31	- event store and command persistence
32	- projections/read models for operator use
33	- TUI-first operator UI and later GUI/API surfaces
34	- human-attention routing and notification-ready alert semantics
35	
36	The console does not own:
37	
38	- `/livespec:*` spec mutation semantics
39	- Beads issue storage semantics
40	- Dispatcher's factory execution behavior
41	- Fabro workflow execution, run internals, logs, or sandbox UI
42	- GitHub pull request merge policy
43	
44	The console may invoke existing CLIs or APIs through ports/adapters, but
45	those systems remain the source of truth for their own domains.
46	
47	**Control-Plane realization.** This console is the reference realization of livespec's **Control Plane** -- the operator-cockpit role livespec core defines in its workflow-planes architecture (`spec.md`) and elaborates as non-normative *Control-Plane console guidance* in its `non-functional-requirements.md`. As that role prescribes, the console *observes* every plane read-only through that plane's own published surface, *composes* the cross-plane operator picture that no single plane can produce alone, *invokes* each plane's own operations on the operator's behalf -- issuing commands only through that plane's published command surface, never reaching around it -- and *coordinates* the human, while *never owning* any plane's semantics (the owns / does-not-own lists above are the concrete expression of that boundary). The console is the Control Plane / operator cockpit, NOT a Driver (the per-agent-runtime binding, e.g. `livespec-driver-claude`). It is also not a required dependency of the workflow it observes: the spec lifecycle and the orchestrator skills remain independently drivable without it, and the console enriches the operator experience rather than gating it.
48	
49	```mermaid
50	flowchart LR
51	  subgraph Console["CONTROL PLANE: livespec-console-beads-fabro"]
52	    Events["Canonical events"]
53	    Commands["Commands"]
54	    Projections["Operator projections"]
55	    UI["TUI / future GUI"]
56	  end
57	
58	  subgraph Spec["SPEC PLANE"]
59	    LiveSpec["livespec core\n/spec lifecycle"]
60	  end
61	  subgraph Orch["ORCHESTRATOR PLANE"]
62	    Orchestrator["livespec-orchestrator-beads-fabro\n/Beads + Dispatcher"]
63	    Fabro["Fabro\n/run execution + human gates"]
64	  end
65	  GitHub["GitHub\n/PRs + checks"]
66	
67	  LiveSpec -->|"observed through adapter"| Events
68	  Orchestrator -->|"observed through adapter"| Events
69	  Fabro -->|"observed through adapter"| Events
70	  GitHub -->|"observed through adapter"| Events
71	  Events --> Projections --> UI
72	  UI --> Commands
73	  Commands -->|"ports invoke existing systems"| Orchestrator
74	  Commands -->|"ports invoke existing systems"| LiveSpec
75	  Commands -->|"ports invoke existing systems"| Fabro
76	```
77	
78	## Product Shape
79	
80	The steady-state product is intended to be a single Rust executable
81	(its normative force is a SHOULD; see `constraints.md` -> Runtime
82	Shape):
83	
84	```text
85	livespec-console-beads-fabro serve
86	```
87	
88	`serve` starts ingestion, the durable event store, projections, API, live
89	event fanout, and the operator UI surface. Supporting subcommands may
90	include:
91	
92	```text
93	livespec-console-beads-fabro tui
94	livespec-console-beads-fabro backfill
95	livespec-console-beads-fabro events tail
96	livespec-console-beads-fabro snapshot
97	livespec-console-beads-fabro doctor
98	```
99	
100	The first UI is a TUI with arrow-driven selection lists, detail panes,
101	command modals, and live updates. A GUI can later consume the same
102	events, commands, and projections.
103	
104	Architecture checks are NOT an operator capability: the console
105	performs no architecture check for an operator. They are a
106	contributor quality-gate concern owned by
107	`non-functional-requirements.md` -> Architecture Tests and realized as
108	the separate `console-arch-check` binary. The console binary surfaces
109	an `arch-check` token only as a discoverability redirect -- it appears
110	in `help` output and its handler prints a pointer to the contributor
111	gate (`just check-arch`) instead of running any check.
112	
113	### Initial-adapter fidelity
114	
115	First-milestone adapters and command ports MAY be minimal or simulated
116	rather than performing full real source I/O, so the event flow and the
117	UI can be built incrementally. Honesty is mandatory: an adapter or port
118	that has not actually performed or observed an action MUST signal that
119	fact (an explicit not-observed / simulated / unimplemented signal) and
120	MUST NOT emit a success or observed-fact event asserting an effect it
121	did not achieve or observe. The wire-level form of this rule lives in
122	`contracts.md` -> Adapter Contract and Command Handling.
123	
124	```mermaid
125	flowchart TB
126	  Binary["Single Rust executable"]
127	  Serve["serve"]
128	  Tui["tui"]
129	  Backfill["backfill"]
130	  Tail["events tail"]
131	  Snapshot["snapshot"]
132	  Doctor["doctor"]
133	
134	  Binary --> Serve
135	  Binary --> Tui
136	  Binary --> Backfill
137	  Binary --> Tail
138	  Binary --> Snapshot
139	  Binary --> Doctor
140	
141	  Serve --> Ingest["ingestors"]
142	  Serve --> Store["SQLite WAL event store"]
143	  Serve --> Projectors["projectors"]
144	  Serve --> Api["API + live fanout"]
145	  Serve --> Ui["operator UI"]
146	```
147	
148	## Architecture
149	
150	The architecture follows event sourcing, domain-driven design, and
151	ports/adapters.
152	
153	```text
154	source systems
155	  -> pull adapters
156	  -> canonical events
157	  -> durable event log
158	  -> projectors
159	  -> TUI / GUI / API
160	
161	UI commands
162	  -> command store
163	  -> bounded-context handlers
164	  -> ports/adapters
165	  -> outcome events
166	```
167	
168	Adapters start as pull shims over existing systems. Over time, upstream
169	systems may emit stronger native events, but the console contract is the
170	canonical event shape it consumes.
171	
172	Each source adapter is a logical, per-source boundary sitting behind its
173	own port. Whether adapters are packaged as separate crates or as
174	per-source modules within one adapters crate is a contributor-facing
175	concern governed by `non-functional-requirements.md`, not an
176	operator-facing guarantee; the binding rule is that adapters stay
177	isolated behind their ports.
178	
179	```mermaid
180	flowchart LR
181	  subgraph Sources["Source systems"]
182	    LS["LiveSpec files + CLIs"]
183	    BD["Beads tenant via bd"]
184	    DJ["Dispatcher journal"]
185	    FR["Fabro API / ps / SSE"]
186	    GH["GitHub API"]
187	  end
188	
189	  subgraph Adapters["Pull adapters"]
190	    LSA["LiveSpec adapter"]
191	    BDA["Beads adapter"]
192	    DJA["Dispatcher adapter"]
193	    FRA["Fabro adapter"]
194	    GHA["GitHub adapter"]
195	  end
196	
197	  subgraph Core["Event-sourced console core"]
198	    Log["Append-only event log"]
199	    Cmd["Command inbox"]
200	    Proj["Rebuildable projections"]
201	    Health["Ingestion health"]
202	  end
203	
204	  subgraph Frontends["Operator frontends"]
205	    TUI["TUI"]
206	    GUI["Future GUI"]
207	    API["API clients"]
208	  end
209	
210	  LS --> LSA --> Log
211	  BD --> BDA --> Log
212	  DJ --> DJA --> Log
213	  FR --> FRA --> Log
214	  GH --> GHA --> Log
215	  Log --> Proj --> Frontends
216	  Frontends --> Cmd --> Core
217	  Health --> Proj
218	```
219	
220	The hexagonal boundary keeps source-specific mechanics outside the domain:
221	
222	```mermaid
223	flowchart TB
224	  subgraph Outer["Outer adapters"]
225	    TuiAdapter["TUI adapter"]
226	    WebAdapter["Future web adapter"]
227	    FabroAdapter["Fabro adapter"]
228	    BeadsAdapter["Beads adapter"]
229	    LivespecAdapter["LiveSpec adapter"]
230	    DispatcherAdapter["Dispatcher adapter"]
231	    GithubAdapter["GitHub adapter"]
232	    SqliteAdapter["SQLite event-store adapter"]
233	  end
234	
235	  subgraph Application["Application layer"]
236	    CommandHandlers["Command handlers"]
237	    ProjectorRunners["Projector runners"]
238	    IngestionRunners["Ingestion runners"]
239	    Ports["Port traits"]
240	  end
241	
242	  subgraph Domain["Domain layer"]
243	    Events["Event types"]
244	    CommandsDomain["Command types"]
245	    Aggregates["Aggregates"]
246	    Policies["Policies + invariants"]
247	    Errors["Typed domain errors"]
248	  end
249	
250	  TuiAdapter --> CommandHandlers
251	  WebAdapter --> CommandHandlers
252	  FabroAdapter --> Ports
253	  BeadsAdapter --> Ports
254	  LivespecAdapter --> Ports
255	  DispatcherAdapter --> Ports
256	  GithubAdapter --> Ports
257	  SqliteAdapter --> Ports
258	  CommandHandlers --> Domain
259	  ProjectorRunners --> Domain
260	  IngestionRunners --> Domain
261	  Ports --> Domain
262	```
263	
264	## Bounded Contexts
265	
266	Initial bounded contexts:
267	
268	- **Ingestion** -- source observation, checkpointing, backfill,
269	  reconciliation, source health.
270	- **Factory** -- Dispatcher/Fabro queue drains, selected item dispatch,
271	  factory pause/resume, human gate observation.
272	- **Spec Lifecycle** -- LiveSpec `next`, doctor, proposed changes,
273	  critique, revise-required signals.
274	- **Grooming** -- needs-regroom routing, slice proposal/approval events,
275	  factory/manual/spec routing.
276	- **Attention** -- alerts, acknowledgement, snooze, owner/triage state.
277	- **Repository Hygiene** -- janitor checks, stale PR/branch/worktree
278	  findings, primary checkout health.
279	- **Configuration** -- registered repos, source endpoints, notification
280	  policy, adapter enablement.
281	
282	Each bounded context owns its command vocabulary, events, invariants,
283	aggregates, and projections.
284	
285	```mermaid
286	flowchart LR
287	  Ingestion["Ingestion\nobserve + checkpoint + backfill"]
288	  Factory["Factory\ndrain + dispatch + gates"]
289	  Spec["Spec Lifecycle\nnext + doctor + revise signals"]
290	  Grooming["Grooming\nneeds-regroom + slicing"]
291	  Attention["Attention\nalerts + ack + snooze"]
292	  Hygiene["Repository Hygiene\njanitor + stale state"]
293	  Config["Configuration\nrepos + endpoints + policy"]
294	
295	  Ingestion -->|"source health events"| Attention
296	  Factory -->|"human gate / failure events"| Attention
297	  Spec -->|"revise / doctor events"| Attention
298	  Grooming -->|"regroom events"| Attention
299	  Hygiene -->|"hygiene findings"| Attention
300	  Config --> Ingestion
301	  Config --> Factory
302	```
303	
304	## Terminology
305	
306	**Canonical event** -- A durable fact in the console event log. It may be
307	directly emitted by the console or synthesized by an adapter from a source
308	system's native fact.
309	
310	**Command** -- An operator intention, such as requesting a factory drain.
311	Commands are persisted separately from domain events. A command may be
312	accepted, rejected, run, fail, or succeed; it is not itself proof that the
313	requested action occurred.
314	
315	**Adapter** -- A source-specific translator that observes a system such as
316	Fabro, Beads, LiveSpec, Dispatcher, or GitHub and emits canonical events.
317	
318	**Projection** -- A derived read model, rebuilt from events, such as the
319	attention inbox, work card list, event timeline, or repo health view.
320	
321	**Attention item** -- A projection entry requiring human review or action,
322	such as a Fabro human gate, LiveSpec revise need, doctor failure, host-only
323	task, or non-converging factory item.
324	
325	**Factory** -- The Beads/Fabro execution path: ready work-items selected for
326	Dispatcher, run in Fabro sandboxes, gated, merged, closed, bounced, or
327	surfaced.
328

# spec.md -- livespec-console-beads-fabro

`livespec-console-beads-fabro` is the LiveSpec-family operator console
for repositories whose implementation work is tracked in Beads and
driven through the Beads/Fabro orchestrator. It is a separate product
from LiveSpec core, the Beads/Fabro orchestrator, and Fabro itself.

## Purpose

The console gives a human operator one coherent place to answer:

- What needs attention now?
- What spec-side action is pending?
- What implementation work is ready?
- What is currently in the factory?
- Which Fabro runs are blocked on human input?
- Which work is manual or host-only and must not enter Fabro?
- What commands can be safely issued next?

The console is event-sourced. It consumes source facts from LiveSpec,
Beads, Dispatcher, Fabro, GitHub, and local repository state, translates
them into canonical console events, and derives operator projections such
as attention inboxes, cards, timelines, and repository health.

## Scope Boundary

The console owns:

- canonical console events and commands
- source adapters and ingestion checkpoints
- event store and command persistence
- projections/read models for operator use
- TUI-first operator UI and later GUI/API surfaces
- human-attention routing and notification-ready alert semantics

The console does not own:

- `/livespec:*` spec mutation semantics
- Beads issue storage semantics
- Dispatcher's factory execution behavior
- Fabro workflow execution, run internals, logs, or sandbox UI
- GitHub pull request merge policy

The console may invoke existing CLIs or APIs through ports/adapters, but
those systems remain the source of truth for their own domains.

**Control-Plane realization.** This console is the reference realization of livespec's **Control Plane** -- the operator-cockpit role livespec core defines in its workflow-planes architecture (`spec.md`) and elaborates as non-normative *Control-Plane console guidance* in its `non-functional-requirements.md`. As that role prescribes, the console *observes* every plane read-only through that plane's own published surface, *composes* the cross-plane operator picture that no single plane can produce alone, *invokes* each plane's own operations on the operator's behalf -- issuing commands only through that plane's published command surface, never reaching around it -- and *coordinates* the human, while *never owning* any plane's semantics (the owns / does-not-own lists above are the concrete expression of that boundary). The console is the Control Plane / operator cockpit, NOT a Driver (the per-agent-runtime binding, e.g. `livespec-driver-claude`). It is also not a required dependency of the workflow it observes: the spec lifecycle and the orchestrator skills remain independently drivable without it, and the console enriches the operator experience rather than gating it.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  subgraph Console["CONTROL PLANE: livespec-console-beads-fabro"]
    Events["Canonical events"]
    Commands["Commands"]
    Projections["Operator projections"]
    UI["TUI / future GUI"]
  end

  subgraph Spec["SPEC PLANE"]
    LiveSpec["livespec core\n/spec lifecycle"]
  end
  subgraph Orch["ORCHESTRATOR PLANE"]
    Orchestrator["livespec-orchestrator-beads-fabro\n/Beads + Dispatcher"]
    Fabro["Fabro\n/run execution + human gates"]
  end
  GitHub["GitHub\n/PRs + checks"]

  LiveSpec -->|"observed through adapter"| Events
  Orchestrator -->|"observed through adapter"| Events
  Fabro -->|"observed through adapter"| Events
  GitHub -->|"observed through adapter"| Events
  Events --> Projections --> UI
  UI --> Commands
  Commands -->|"ports invoke existing systems"| Orchestrator
  Commands -->|"ports invoke existing systems"| LiveSpec
  Commands -->|"ports invoke existing systems"| Fabro
```

## Product Shape

The steady-state product is intended to be a single Rust executable
(its normative force is a SHOULD; see `constraints.md` -> Runtime
Shape):

```text
livespec-console-beads-fabro serve
```

`serve` starts ingestion, the durable event store, projections, API, live
event fanout, and the operator UI surface. Supporting subcommands may
include:

```text
livespec-console-beads-fabro tui
livespec-console-beads-fabro backfill
livespec-console-beads-fabro events tail
livespec-console-beads-fabro snapshot
livespec-console-beads-fabro doctor
```

The first UI is a TUI with arrow-driven selection lists, detail panes,
command modals, and live updates. A GUI can later consume the same
events, commands, and projections.

Architecture checks are NOT an operator capability: the console
performs no architecture check for an operator. They are a
contributor quality-gate concern owned by
`non-functional-requirements.md` -> Architecture Tests and realized as
the separate `console-arch-check` binary. The console binary surfaces
an `arch-check` token only as a discoverability redirect -- it appears
in `help` output and its handler prints a pointer to the contributor
gate (`just check-arch`) instead of running any check.

### Initial-adapter fidelity

First-milestone adapters and command ports MAY be minimal or simulated
rather than performing full real source I/O, so the event flow and the
UI can be built incrementally. Honesty is mandatory: an adapter or port
that has not actually performed or observed an action MUST signal that
fact (an explicit not-observed / simulated / unimplemented signal) and
MUST NOT emit a success or observed-fact event asserting an effect it
did not achieve or observe. The wire-level form of this rule lives in
`contracts.md` -> Adapter Contract and Command Handling.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
  Binary["Single Rust executable"]
  Serve["serve"]
  Tui["tui"]
  Backfill["backfill"]
  Tail["events tail"]
  Snapshot["snapshot"]
  Doctor["doctor"]

  Binary --> Serve
  Binary --> Tui
  Binary --> Backfill
  Binary --> Tail
  Binary --> Snapshot
  Binary --> Doctor

  Serve --> Ingest["ingestors"]
  Serve --> Store["SQLite WAL event store"]
  Serve --> Projectors["projectors"]
  Serve --> Api["API + live fanout"]
  Serve --> Ui["operator UI"]
```

## Architecture

The architecture follows event sourcing, domain-driven design, and
ports/adapters.

```text
source systems
  -> pull adapters
  -> canonical events
  -> durable event log
  -> projectors
  -> TUI / GUI / API

UI commands
  -> command store
  -> bounded-context handlers
  -> ports/adapters
  -> outcome events
```

Adapters start as pull shims over existing systems. Over time, upstream
systems may emit stronger native events, but the console contract is the
canonical event shape it consumes.

Each source adapter is a logical, per-source boundary sitting behind its
own port. Whether adapters are packaged as separate crates or as
per-source modules within one adapters crate is a contributor-facing
concern governed by `non-functional-requirements.md`, not an
operator-facing guarantee; the binding rule is that adapters stay
isolated behind their ports.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  subgraph Sources["Source systems"]
    LS["LiveSpec files + CLIs"]
    BD["Beads tenant via bd"]
    DJ["Dispatcher journal"]
    FR["Fabro API / ps / SSE"]
    GH["GitHub API"]
  end

  subgraph Adapters["Pull adapters"]
    LSA["LiveSpec adapter"]
    BDA["Beads adapter"]
    DJA["Dispatcher adapter"]
    FRA["Fabro adapter"]
    GHA["GitHub adapter"]
  end

  subgraph Core["Event-sourced console core"]
    Log["Append-only event log"]
    Cmd["Command inbox"]
    Proj["Rebuildable projections"]
    Health["Ingestion health"]
  end

  subgraph Frontends["Operator frontends"]
    TUI["TUI"]
    GUI["Future GUI"]
    API["API clients"]
  end

  LS --> LSA --> Log
  BD --> BDA --> Log
  DJ --> DJA --> Log
  FR --> FRA --> Log
  GH --> GHA --> Log
  Log --> Proj --> Frontends
  Frontends --> Cmd --> Core
  Health --> Proj
```

The hexagonal boundary keeps source-specific mechanics outside the domain:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
  subgraph Outer["Outer adapters"]
    TuiAdapter["TUI adapter"]
    WebAdapter["Future web adapter"]
    FabroAdapter["Fabro adapter"]
    BeadsAdapter["Beads adapter"]
    LivespecAdapter["LiveSpec adapter"]
    DispatcherAdapter["Dispatcher adapter"]
    GithubAdapter["GitHub adapter"]
    SqliteAdapter["SQLite event-store adapter"]
  end

  subgraph Application["Application layer"]
    CommandHandlers["Command handlers"]
    ProjectorRunners["Projector runners"]
    IngestionRunners["Ingestion runners"]
    Ports["Port traits"]
  end

  subgraph Domain["Domain layer"]
    Events["Event types"]
    CommandsDomain["Command types"]
    Aggregates["Aggregates"]
    Policies["Policies + invariants"]
    Errors["Typed domain errors"]
  end

  TuiAdapter --> CommandHandlers
  WebAdapter --> CommandHandlers
  FabroAdapter --> Ports
  BeadsAdapter --> Ports
  LivespecAdapter --> Ports
  DispatcherAdapter --> Ports
  GithubAdapter --> Ports
  SqliteAdapter --> Ports
  CommandHandlers --> Domain
  ProjectorRunners --> Domain
  IngestionRunners --> Domain
  Ports --> Domain
```

## Bounded Contexts

Initial bounded contexts:

- **Ingestion** -- source observation, checkpointing, backfill,
  reconciliation, source health.
- **Factory** -- Dispatcher/Fabro queue drains, selected item dispatch,
  factory pause/resume, human gate observation.
- **Spec Lifecycle** -- LiveSpec `next`, doctor, proposed changes,
  critique, revise-required signals.
- **Grooming** -- needs-regroom routing, slice proposal/approval events,
  factory/manual/spec routing.
- **Attention** -- alerts, acknowledgement, snooze, owner/triage state.
- **Repository Hygiene** -- janitor checks, stale PR/branch/worktree
  findings, primary checkout health.
- **Configuration** -- registered repos, source endpoints, notification
  policy, adapter enablement.

Each bounded context owns its command vocabulary, events, invariants,
aggregates, and projections.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  Ingestion["Ingestion\nobserve + checkpoint + backfill"]
  Factory["Factory\ndrain + dispatch + gates"]
  Spec["Spec Lifecycle\nnext + doctor + revise signals"]
  Grooming["Grooming\nneeds-regroom + slicing"]
  Attention["Attention\nalerts + ack + snooze"]
  Hygiene["Repository Hygiene\njanitor + stale state"]
  Config["Configuration\nrepos + endpoints + policy"]

  Ingestion -->|"source health events"| Attention
  Factory -->|"human gate / failure events"| Attention
  Spec -->|"revise / doctor events"| Attention
  Grooming -->|"regroom events"| Attention
  Hygiene -->|"hygiene findings"| Attention
  Config --> Ingestion
  Config --> Factory
```

## Terminology

**Canonical event** -- A durable fact in the console event log. It may be
directly emitted by the console or synthesized by an adapter from a source
system's native fact.

**Command** -- An operator intention, such as requesting a factory drain.
Commands are persisted separately from domain events. A command may be
accepted, rejected, run, fail, or succeed; it is not itself proof that the
requested action occurred.

**Adapter** -- A source-specific translator that observes a system such as
Fabro, Beads, LiveSpec, Dispatcher, or GitHub and emits canonical events.

**Projection** -- A derived read model, rebuilt from events, such as the
attention inbox, work card list, event timeline, or repo health view.

**Attention item** -- A projection entry requiring human review or action,
such as a Fabro human gate, LiveSpec revise need, doctor failure, host-only
task, or non-converging factory item.

**Factory** -- The Beads/Fabro execution path: ready work-items selected for
Dispatcher, run in Fabro sandboxes, gated, merged, closed, bounced, or
surfaced.

1	# contracts.md -- livespec-console-beads-fabro
2	
3	This file defines the console's wire-level and persistence contracts.
4	
5	## Event Envelope
6	
7	Every canonical event MUST carry:
8	
9	```jsonc
10	{
11	  "event_id": "evt_...",
12	  "schema_version": 1,
13	  "context": "factory",
14	  "type": "factory.drain.started",
15	  "source": "console",
16	  "source_event_id": "optional-source-stable-id",
17	  "aggregate_id": "repo:livespec-runtime",
18	  "stream_id": "factory:livespec-runtime",
19	  "stream_seq": 12,
20	  "causation_id": "optional-causing-command-or-event-id",
21	  "correlation_id": "corr_...",
22	  "occurred_at": "2026-06-22T00:00:00Z",
23	  "observed_at": "2026-06-22T00:00:01Z",
24	  "payload": {},
25	  "metadata": {}
26	}
27	```
28	
29	`event_id` is globally unique. `(source, source_event_id)` MUST be unique
30	when `source_event_id` is present so adapter replay is idempotent.
31	
32	The `events` table (see SQLite Persistence) is a faithful 1:1 projection
33	of this envelope: every envelope field is a column and every column has
34	an envelope source. `correlation_id` and `causation_id` are scalar ids,
35	not structured objects; `aggregate_id` is the event's routing key (e.g.
36	`"repo:<id>"`, the same shape as a command's `aggregate_id`).
37	
38	```mermaid
39	flowchart LR
40	  Native["Native source fact"]
41	  Normalize["Adapter normalize"]
42	  Event["Canonical event envelope"]
43	  Dedupe["Dedupe by source + source_event_id"]
44	  Append["Append to event log"]
45	  Project["Project read models"]
46	
47	  Native --> Normalize --> Event --> Dedupe --> Append --> Project
48	```
49	
50	## Command Envelope
51	
52	Commands are persisted intentions, not facts. A command MUST carry:
53	
54	```jsonc
55	{
56	  "command_id": "cmd_...",
57	  "context": "factory",
58	  "type": "factory.drain_requested",
59	  "aggregate_id": "repo:livespec-runtime",
60	  "idempotency_key": "operator-provided-or-derived-key",
61	  "requested_by": "user-or-agent",
62	  "requested_at": "2026-06-22T00:00:00Z",
63	  "causation_event_id": null,
64	  "correlation_id": "corr_...",
65	  "payload": {}
66	}
67	```
68	
69	Commands MAY be rejected. State changes become durable only through
70	events such as `command.accepted`, `factory.drain.started`,
71	`factory.drain.failed`, `factory.drain.completed`, and
72	`factory.drain.not_wired` (the honest outcome a simulated or
73	unimplemented drain port emits instead of fabricating success, per the
74	honesty rule in the Command Handling section).
75	
76	```mermaid
77	sequenceDiagram
78	  participant UI as TUI or future GUI
79	  participant API as Command API
80	  participant Handler as Bounded context handler
81	  participant Port as External-system port
82	  participant Store as Event store
83	
84	  UI->>API: submit command
85	  API->>Store: persist command pending
86	  API->>Handler: handle command
87	  Handler->>Store: append command.accepted or command.rejected
88	  alt accepted
89	    Handler->>Port: invoke CLI or API
90	    Port-->>Handler: result
91	    Handler->>Store: append outcome event
92	    Handler->>Store: update command status
93	  else rejected
94	    Handler->>Store: update command rejected
95	  end
96	  Store-->>UI: projection/live update
97	```
98	
99	## SQLite Persistence
100	
101	The initial durable store is SQLite in WAL mode.
102	
103	Required tables:
104	
105	```text
106	events
107	  global_seq integer primary key
108	  event_id text unique
109	  context text
110	  aggregate_id text
111	  stream_id text
112	  stream_seq integer
113	  type text
114	  schema_version integer
115	  occurred_at text
116	  observed_at text
117	  causation_id text null
118	  correlation_id text
119	  source text
120	  source_event_id text null
121	  payload_json text
122	  metadata_json text
123	
124	commands
125	  command_id text primary key
126	  context text
127	  type text
128	  aggregate_id text null
129	  idempotency_key text unique
130	  requested_by text
131	  requested_at text
132	  causation_event_id text null
133	  correlation_id text
134	  status text
135	  payload_json text
136	  result_json text null
137	  error_json text null
138	  updated_at text
139	
140	checkpoints
141	  adapter_id text primary key
142	  checkpoint_json text
143	  advanced_at text
144	
145	projections
146	  name text
147	  version integer
148	  checkpoint_seq integer
149	  state_json text
150	```
151	
152	Events are append-only. Rollback is represented by compensating events, not
153	by deleting or mutating prior domain events.
154	
155	```mermaid
156	erDiagram
157	  COMMANDS {
158	    text command_id PK
159	    text context
160	    text type
161	    text aggregate_id
162	    text idempotency_key UK
163	    text status
164	    text correlation_id
165	    text payload_json
166	    text result_json
167	    text error_json
168	  }
169	
170	  EVENTS {
171	    integer global_seq PK
172	    text event_id UK
173	    text context
174	    text aggregate_id
175	    text stream_id
176	    integer stream_seq
177	    text type
178	    integer schema_version
179	    text causation_id
180	    text correlation_id
181	    text source
182	    text source_event_id
183	    text payload_json
184	    text metadata_json
185	  }
186	
187	  CHECKPOINTS {
188	    text adapter_id PK
189	    text checkpoint_json
190	    text advanced_at
191	  }
192	
193	  PROJECTIONS {
194	    text name
195	    integer version
196	    integer checkpoint_seq
197	    text state_json
198	  }
199	
200	  COMMANDS ||--o{ EVENTS : "causes"
201	  EVENTS ||--o{ PROJECTIONS : "replayed into"
202	  CHECKPOINTS ||--o{ EVENTS : "guards append"
203	```
204	
205	## Adapter Contract
206	
207	Every pull adapter MUST implement:
208	
209	```text
210	adapter_id
211	source_kind
212	checkpoint_key
213	initial_backfill()
214	poll_since(checkpoint)
215	normalize(native_record) -> canonical events[]
216	advance_checkpoint(only after durable append)
217	reconcile(window)
218	health()
219	```
220	
221	Adapter rules:
222	
223	- The adapter MUST persist a durable checkpoint per source instance.
224	- The adapter MUST append normalized events before advancing its checkpoint.
225	- The adapter MUST provide at-least-once delivery; duplicates are allowed
226	  and MUST be deduplicated by stable source event identity.
227	- The adapter MUST support cold-start backfill and bounded backfill.
228	- The adapter MUST re-read a sliding reconciliation window on normal polls.
229	- The adapter MUST emit explicit ingestion health events for parse failures,
230	  unavailable sources, invalid checkpoints, backfill incompleteness, or
231	  unprovable continuity.
232	- If a source cannot provide complete historical transitions, the adapter
233	  MUST emit snapshot/reconciliation events and a completeness finding rather
234	  than claiming full history.
235	- If an adapter does not actually perform real source I/O (a minimal or
236	  simulated first-milestone adapter per `spec.md` -> Initial-adapter
237	  fidelity), it MUST emit an explicit not-observed / simulated /
238	  unimplemented health signal and MUST NOT emit an event asserting an
239	  observed source fact it did not observe.
240	
241	```mermaid
242	stateDiagram-v2
243	  [*] --> LoadCheckpoint
244	  LoadCheckpoint --> PollWindow
245	  PollWindow --> Normalize
246	  Normalize --> AppendEvents
247	  AppendEvents --> AdvanceCheckpoint
248	  AdvanceCheckpoint --> HealthOk
249	  HealthOk --> PollWindow
250	
251	  PollWindow --> SourceUnavailable
252	  Normalize --> ParseFailed
253	  AppendEvents --> AppendFailed
254	  SourceUnavailable --> EmitHealthFinding
255	  ParseFailed --> EmitHealthFinding
256	  AppendFailed --> RetryWithoutAdvance
257	  EmitHealthFinding --> PollWindow
258	  RetryWithoutAdvance --> PollWindow
259	```
260	
261	```mermaid
262	sequenceDiagram
263	  participant Adapter
264	  participant Source
265	  participant Store
266	  participant Checkpoint
267	
268	  Adapter->>Checkpoint: read checkpoint
269	  Adapter->>Source: poll from checkpoint minus safety window
270	  Source-->>Adapter: native records
271	  Adapter->>Adapter: normalize to canonical events
272	  Adapter->>Store: append events idempotently
273	  Store-->>Adapter: durable append confirmed
274	  Adapter->>Checkpoint: advance checkpoint
275	```
276	
277	## Initial Adapters
278	
279	Initial adapters:
280	
281	- **Fabro adapter** -- reads Fabro API/SSE or `fabro ps` / run details and
282	  emits run, blocked, human-gate, terminal, and run-link events.
283	- **Dispatcher adapter** -- tails and backfills Dispatcher journal JSONL and
284	  emits dispatch wave/item/outcome events.
285	- **Beads adapter** -- reads Beads work-item state through the `bd` CLI and
286	  emits snapshot/ready/closed/needs-regroom/manual-routing events.
287	- **LiveSpec adapter** -- reads spec-side `next`, doctor output,
288	  proposed changes, history, and filesystem/git state.
289	- **GitHub adapter** -- reads PR, check, branch, and merge state.
290	
291	Adapters MUST call existing stable CLIs/APIs through ports. UI code MUST NOT
292	call Fabro, Beads, LiveSpec, Dispatcher, or GitHub directly.
293	
294	```mermaid
295	flowchart TB
296	  subgraph SourceContracts["Source contracts"]
297	    Fabro["Fabro API / run events"]
298	    Dispatcher["Dispatcher journal JSONL"]
299	    Beads["bd list / show / ready"]
300	    LiveSpec["/livespec next / doctor / files"]
301	    GitHub["PR / check / merge API"]
302	  end
303	
304	  subgraph AdapterContracts["Adapter contracts"]
305	    FA["Fabro adapter"]
306	    DA["Dispatcher adapter"]
307	    BA["Beads adapter"]
308	    LA["LiveSpec adapter"]
309	    GA["GitHub adapter"]
310	  end
311	
312	  subgraph Canonical["Canonical console stream"]
313	    RunEvents["fabro.* events"]
314	    DispatchEvents["dispatch.* events"]
315	    WorkEvents["beads.* events"]
316	    SpecEvents["spec.* events"]
317	    PrEvents["pr.* events"]
318	  end
319	
320	  Fabro --> FA --> RunEvents
321	  Dispatcher --> DA --> DispatchEvents
322	  Beads --> BA --> WorkEvents
323	  LiveSpec --> LA --> SpecEvents
324	  GitHub --> GA --> PrEvents
325	```
326	
327	## Command Handling
328	
329	Command handlers live in bounded contexts. A handler MUST:
330	
331	1. Validate the command against context policy and aggregate/projection state.
332	2. Persist acceptance or rejection.
333	3. Invoke external systems only through ports/adapters.
334	4. Append success/failure/outcome events.
335	5. Leave recovery to reconciliation/backfill when a crash occurs between an
336	   external side effect and outcome event append.
337	6. Never emit a success or outcome event for an effect the port did not
338	   actually achieve. A simulated or unimplemented port MUST surface a
339	   not-observed / simulated / unimplemented outcome (or a typed failure),
340	   never a fabricated success.
341	
342	Initial commands:
343	
344	- `factory.drain_requested`
345	- `factory.dispatch_item_requested`
346	- `factory.pause_requested`
347	- `factory.resume_requested`
348	- `spec.doctor_requested`
349	- `attention.acknowledge_requested`
350	- `attention.snooze_requested`
351	- `grooming.regroom_requested`
352	
353	```mermaid
354	flowchart LR
355	  Requested["command requested"]
356	  Policy["context policy validation"]
357	  Rejected["command rejected event"]
358	  Accepted["command accepted event"]
359	  SideEffect["invoke port"]
360	  Succeeded["success event"]
361	  Failed["failure event"]
362	  Reconcile["reconciliation observes external result"]
363	
364	  Requested --> Policy
365	  Policy -->|"invalid"| Rejected
366	  Policy -->|"valid"| Accepted
367	  Accepted --> SideEffect
368	  SideEffect -->|"ok"| Succeeded
369	  SideEffect -->|"expected failure"| Failed
370	  SideEffect -->|"crash gap"| Reconcile --> Succeeded
371	```
372	
373	## TUI Contract
374	
375	The TUI is the first frontend. It MUST be a projection consumer and command
376	producer, not a source-system client.
377	
378	Required TUI views:
379	
380	- Attention
381	- Spec
382	- Ready
383	- Factory
384	- Manual
385	- Done
386	- Events
387	- Repos
388	
389	The default view MUST be Attention. Navigation SHOULD use arrow-driven
390	selection lists, detail panes, command modals, `/` search, and a command
391	palette. Numeric selection MAY exist as a fallback but MUST NOT be the only
392	interaction model.
393	
394	```mermaid
395	flowchart TB
396	  subgraph Screen["TUI default screen"]
397	    Header["Header: fleet, mode, ingestion, Fabro summary"]
398	    Left["Left navigation\nAttention / Spec / Ready / Factory / Manual / Done / Events / Repos"]
399	    Center["Center list\narrow-selected work cards or attention items"]
400	    Right["Right detail pane\nsource refs, timeline, next actions"]
401	    Footer["Footer\nshortcuts, command status, live-update health"]
402	  end
403	
404	  Header --> Left
405	  Header --> Center
406	  Header --> Right
407	  Left --> Center
408	  Center --> Right
409	  Right --> Footer
410	```
411	
412	```mermaid
413	flowchart LR
414	  Projection["Projection snapshot"]
415	  Selection["Arrow selection"]
416	  Detail["Detail pane"]
417	  Modal["Command modal"]
418	  Command["Persisted command"]
419	  Live["Live event update"]
420	
421	  Projection --> Selection --> Detail
422	  Detail --> Modal --> Command
423	  Command --> Live --> Projection
424	```
425

# contracts.md -- livespec-console-beads-fabro

This file defines the console's wire-level and persistence contracts.

## Event Envelope

Every canonical event MUST carry:

```jsonc
{
  "event_id": "evt_...",
  "schema_version": 1,
  "context": "factory",
  "type": "factory.drain.started",
  "source": "console",
  "source_event_id": "optional-source-stable-id",
  "aggregate_id": "repo:livespec-runtime",
  "stream_id": "factory:livespec-runtime",
  "stream_seq": 12,
  "causation_id": "optional-causing-command-or-event-id",
  "correlation_id": "corr_...",
  "occurred_at": "2026-06-22T00:00:00Z",
  "observed_at": "2026-06-22T00:00:01Z",
  "payload": {},
  "metadata": {}
}
```

`event_id` is globally unique. `(source, source_event_id)` MUST be unique
when `source_event_id` is present so adapter replay is idempotent.

The `events` table (see SQLite Persistence) is a faithful 1:1 projection
of this envelope: every envelope field is a column and every column has
an envelope source. `correlation_id` and `causation_id` are scalar ids,
not structured objects; `aggregate_id` is the event's routing key (e.g.
`"repo:<id>"`, the same shape as a command's `aggregate_id`).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  Native["Native source fact"]
  Normalize["Adapter normalize"]
  Event["Canonical event envelope"]
  Dedupe["Dedupe by source + source_event_id"]
  Append["Append to event log"]
  Project["Project read models"]

  Native --> Normalize --> Event --> Dedupe --> Append --> Project
```

## Command Envelope

Commands are persisted intentions, not facts. A command MUST carry:

```jsonc
{
  "command_id": "cmd_...",
  "context": "factory",
  "type": "factory.drain_requested",
  "aggregate_id": "repo:livespec-runtime",
  "idempotency_key": "operator-provided-or-derived-key",
  "requested_by": "user-or-agent",
  "requested_at": "2026-06-22T00:00:00Z",
  "causation_event_id": null,
  "correlation_id": "corr_...",
  "payload": {}
}
```

Commands MAY be rejected. State changes become durable only through
events such as `command.accepted`, `factory.drain.started`,
`factory.drain.failed`, `factory.drain.completed`, and
`factory.drain.not_wired` (the honest outcome a simulated or
unimplemented drain port emits instead of fabricating success, per the
honesty rule in the Command Handling section).

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
  participant UI as TUI or future GUI
  participant API as Command API
  participant Handler as Bounded context handler
  participant Port as External-system port
  participant Store as Event store

  UI->>API: submit command
  API->>Store: persist command pending
  API->>Handler: handle command
  Handler->>Store: append command.accepted or command.rejected
  alt accepted
    Handler->>Port: invoke CLI or API
    Port-->>Handler: result
    Handler->>Store: append outcome event
    Handler->>Store: update command status
  else rejected
    Handler->>Store: update command rejected
  end
  Store-->>UI: projection/live update
```

## SQLite Persistence

The initial durable store is SQLite in WAL mode.

Required tables:

```text
events
  global_seq integer primary key
  event_id text unique
  context text
  aggregate_id text
  stream_id text
  stream_seq integer
  type text
  schema_version integer
  occurred_at text
  observed_at text
  causation_id text null
  correlation_id text
  source text
  source_event_id text null
  payload_json text
  metadata_json text

commands
  command_id text primary key
  context text
  type text
  aggregate_id text null
  idempotency_key text unique
  requested_by text
  requested_at text
  causation_event_id text null
  correlation_id text
  status text
  payload_json text
  result_json text null
  error_json text null
  updated_at text

checkpoints
  adapter_id text primary key
  checkpoint_json text
  advanced_at text

projections
  name text
  version integer
  checkpoint_seq integer
  state_json text
```

Events are append-only. Rollback is represented by compensating events, not
by deleting or mutating prior domain events.

```mermaid
erDiagram
  COMMANDS {
    text command_id PK
    text context
    text type
    text aggregate_id
    text idempotency_key UK
    text status
    text correlation_id
    text payload_json
    text result_json
    text error_json
  }

  EVENTS {
    integer global_seq PK
    text event_id UK
    text context
    text aggregate_id
    text stream_id
    integer stream_seq
    text type
    integer schema_version
    text causation_id
    text correlation_id
    text source
    text source_event_id
    text payload_json
    text metadata_json
  }

  CHECKPOINTS {
    text adapter_id PK
    text checkpoint_json
    text advanced_at
  }

  PROJECTIONS {
    text name
    integer version
    integer checkpoint_seq
    text state_json
  }

  COMMANDS ||--o{ EVENTS : "causes"
  EVENTS ||--o{ PROJECTIONS : "replayed into"
  CHECKPOINTS ||--o{ EVENTS : "guards append"
```

## Adapter Contract

Every pull adapter MUST implement:

```text
adapter_id
source_kind
checkpoint_key
initial_backfill()
poll_since(checkpoint)
normalize(native_record) -> canonical events[]
advance_checkpoint(only after durable append)
reconcile(window)
health()
```

Adapter rules:

- The adapter MUST persist a durable checkpoint per source instance.
- The adapter MUST append normalized events before advancing its checkpoint.
- The adapter MUST provide at-least-once delivery; duplicates are allowed
  and MUST be deduplicated by stable source event identity.
- The adapter MUST support cold-start backfill and bounded backfill.
- The adapter MUST re-read a sliding reconciliation window on normal polls.
- The adapter MUST emit explicit ingestion health events for parse failures,
  unavailable sources, invalid checkpoints, backfill incompleteness, or
  unprovable continuity.
- If a source cannot provide complete historical transitions, the adapter
  MUST emit snapshot/reconciliation events and a completeness finding rather
  than claiming full history.
- If an adapter does not actually perform real source I/O (a minimal or
  simulated first-milestone adapter per `spec.md` -> Initial-adapter
  fidelity), it MUST emit an explicit not-observed / simulated /
  unimplemented health signal and MUST NOT emit an event asserting an
  observed source fact it did not observe.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
  [*] --> LoadCheckpoint
  LoadCheckpoint --> PollWindow
  PollWindow --> Normalize
  Normalize --> AppendEvents
  AppendEvents --> AdvanceCheckpoint
  AdvanceCheckpoint --> HealthOk
  HealthOk --> PollWindow

  PollWindow --> SourceUnavailable
  Normalize --> ParseFailed
  AppendEvents --> AppendFailed
  SourceUnavailable --> EmitHealthFinding
  ParseFailed --> EmitHealthFinding
  AppendFailed --> RetryWithoutAdvance
  EmitHealthFinding --> PollWindow
  RetryWithoutAdvance --> PollWindow
```

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
  participant Adapter
  participant Source
  participant Store
  participant Checkpoint

  Adapter->>Checkpoint: read checkpoint
  Adapter->>Source: poll from checkpoint minus safety window
  Source-->>Adapter: native records
  Adapter->>Adapter: normalize to canonical events
  Adapter->>Store: append events idempotently
  Store-->>Adapter: durable append confirmed
  Adapter->>Checkpoint: advance checkpoint
```

## Initial Adapters

Initial adapters:

- **Fabro adapter** -- reads Fabro API/SSE or `fabro ps` / run details and
  emits run, blocked, human-gate, terminal, and run-link events.
- **Dispatcher adapter** -- tails and backfills Dispatcher journal JSONL and
  emits dispatch wave/item/outcome events.
- **Beads adapter** -- reads Beads work-item state through the `bd` CLI and
  emits snapshot/ready/closed/needs-regroom/manual-routing events.
- **LiveSpec adapter** -- reads spec-side `next`, doctor output,
  proposed changes, history, and filesystem/git state.
- **GitHub adapter** -- reads PR, check, branch, and merge state.

Adapters MUST call existing stable CLIs/APIs through ports. UI code MUST NOT
call Fabro, Beads, LiveSpec, Dispatcher, or GitHub directly.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
  subgraph SourceContracts["Source contracts"]
    Fabro["Fabro API / run events"]
    Dispatcher["Dispatcher journal JSONL"]
    Beads["bd list / show / ready"]
    LiveSpec["/livespec next / doctor / files"]
    GitHub["PR / check / merge API"]
  end

  subgraph AdapterContracts["Adapter contracts"]
    FA["Fabro adapter"]
    DA["Dispatcher adapter"]
    BA["Beads adapter"]
    LA["LiveSpec adapter"]
    GA["GitHub adapter"]
  end

  subgraph Canonical["Canonical console stream"]
    RunEvents["fabro.* events"]
    DispatchEvents["dispatch.* events"]
    WorkEvents["beads.* events"]
    SpecEvents["spec.* events"]
    PrEvents["pr.* events"]
  end

  Fabro --> FA --> RunEvents
  Dispatcher --> DA --> DispatchEvents
  Beads --> BA --> WorkEvents
  LiveSpec --> LA --> SpecEvents
  GitHub --> GA --> PrEvents
```

## Command Handling

Command handlers live in bounded contexts. A handler MUST:

1. Validate the command against context policy and aggregate/projection state.
2. Persist acceptance or rejection.
3. Invoke external systems only through ports/adapters.
4. Append success/failure/outcome events.
5. Leave recovery to reconciliation/backfill when a crash occurs between an
   external side effect and outcome event append.
6. Never emit a success or outcome event for an effect the port did not
   actually achieve. A simulated or unimplemented port MUST surface a
   not-observed / simulated / unimplemented outcome (or a typed failure),
   never a fabricated success.

Initial commands:

- `factory.drain_requested`
- `factory.dispatch_item_requested`
- `factory.pause_requested`
- `factory.resume_requested`
- `spec.doctor_requested`
- `attention.acknowledge_requested`
- `attention.snooze_requested`
- `grooming.regroom_requested`

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  Requested["command requested"]
  Policy["context policy validation"]
  Rejected["command rejected event"]
  Accepted["command accepted event"]
  SideEffect["invoke port"]
  Succeeded["success event"]
  Failed["failure event"]
  Reconcile["reconciliation observes external result"]

  Requested --> Policy
  Policy -->|"invalid"| Rejected
  Policy -->|"valid"| Accepted
  Accepted --> SideEffect
  SideEffect -->|"ok"| Succeeded
  SideEffect -->|"expected failure"| Failed
  SideEffect -->|"crash gap"| Reconcile --> Succeeded
```

## TUI Contract

The TUI is the first frontend. It MUST be a projection consumer and command
producer, not a source-system client.

Required TUI views:

- Attention
- Spec
- Ready
- Factory
- Manual
- Done
- Events
- Repos

The default view MUST be Attention. Navigation SHOULD use arrow-driven
selection lists, detail panes, command modals, `/` search, and a command
palette. Numeric selection MAY exist as a fallback but MUST NOT be the only
interaction model.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
  subgraph Screen["TUI default screen"]
    Header["Header: fleet, mode, ingestion, Fabro summary"]
    Left["Left navigation\nAttention / Spec / Ready / Factory / Manual / Done / Events / Repos"]
    Center["Center list\narrow-selected work cards or attention items"]
    Right["Right detail pane\nsource refs, timeline, next actions"]
    Footer["Footer\nshortcuts, command status, live-update health"]
  end

  Header --> Left
  Header --> Center
  Header --> Right
  Left --> Center
  Center --> Right
  Right --> Footer
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  Projection["Projection snapshot"]
  Selection["Arrow selection"]
  Detail["Detail pane"]
  Modal["Command modal"]
  Command["Persisted command"]
  Live["Live event update"]

  Projection --> Selection --> Detail
  Detail --> Modal --> Command
  Command --> Live --> Projection
```

content.text:
I've now read the overseer skill + its running state, the `plan` skill (binding + full prose), and the console's spec + contracts. Here's where things stand and what I want to confirm before we start.

## Bottom line: yes, I understand the ask

You're designing **the next layer of abstraction in Livespec: a deterministic, first-class work-item state machine with WIP-limited (Kanban) lanes** that becomes the shared backbone underneath the overseer, the Dispatcher, and the console — replacing the state the overseer is currently *improvising in-context*.

The arc as I read it: Livespec grew bottom-up by dogfooding — spec lifecycle (`seed/propose-change/.../doctor`) → the Beads/Dolt + Fabro **dark factory** (Dispatcher) → the **`plan`** skill (Planning Lane) → the local **`overseer`** skill (meta-loop over tmux tracks) → the **console** (event-sourced operator cockpit, built from scratch). The overseer is the newest rung and it's straining because **there is no deterministic state machine + project-management substrate beneath it** — it keeps work-item state in working memory + a `tmp/overseer/registry.json` mirror, resolves sessions by scraping panes and grepping transcripts for markers, and infers "%complete" from `bd show`. That's fragile coordination standing in for a real state model. (Its current `stallwatch.log` shows all five tracks idle/at-rest, so it's stable right now, not mid-incident — but the structural gap is real.)

The **seven states** you listed collapse, I think, into a smaller canonical status set plus two orthogonal dimensions — and that compression is exactly the design question:

| Your state | Likely canonical mapping |
|---|---|
| 1. Groomed & ready | `ready` |
| 2. Actively worked | `in_progress` (in the factory / dispatched) |
| 3. Blocked — needs human | `blocked` + reason=`human` |
| 4. Claimed-done, needs verify/exploratory test | **new acceptance gate** (e.g. `in_review` / `needs-acceptance`) |
| 5. Actually done | `closed` |
| 6. Blocked — infra/external | `blocked` + reason=`system/external` |
| 7. Waiting — non-human, non-error (e.g. approval not yet in) | `blocked`/`waiting` + reason=`dependency/approval` |

So most of the redundancy you flagged is real: states 3/6/7 are one `blocked` status differentiated by a **blocked-reason** field, and the only genuinely *new* lifecycle stage is **state 4** (the post-factory human acceptance/exploratory-test gate — which I agree is worth keeping even though the factory already self-reviews; "you can't over-review AI work"). Plus a cross-cutting **WIP-limit-per-lane** policy, which no current artifact owns. Nate's "Open Engine" reportedly uses *fewer* states than your seven, and Gastown/Yegge has direct corollaries — both are core research inputs.

## `plan` skill state: landed and usable (not still in flight)

Confirmed concretely: the `plan` skill is **merged to `master`** (orchestrator commit `bb4a20b` `feat(plan): add the plan skill (Planning Lane realization)`, an ancestor of HEAD), with both its `SKILL.md` binding and full `prose/plan.md` present, and it's live in my available-skills list as `/livespec-orchestrator-beads-fabro:plan`. The track you were thinking of — `zs22` — already shipped the plan skill as `zs22.5`; what's still *open* in that epic is the unrelated governed-repo-lifecycle work (`zs22.8`, M2–M6). So I can use the plan skill for real this session. One caveat: it anchors a ledger epic via `capture-work-item`, so it needs Beads up — which it is (the overseer is reading epic status fine).

## Key finding on the console (resolves part of your uncertainty)

The console is **an event-sourced, read-mostly *observer* (the Control Plane), not the owner of a state machine.** Its spec explicitly says it "does not own Beads issue storage semantics" or "Dispatcher's factory execution behavior." Its TUI views — **Attention / Spec / Ready / Factory / Manual / Done** — *look* like Kanban columns but are **projections derived from source state**, not an authoritative, WIP-enforced lifecycle. So your instinct is right: **the console does not contain the first-class WIP queue / state machine you want, and shouldn't** — it would *render* one. The canonical state machine + WIP enforcement belongs **beneath** the console, as **Beads ledger status/metadata + a thin shared transition-and-WIP contract** that all three consumers read: the **Dispatcher** respects WIP when draining, the **overseer** respects WIP when launching tracks and drives blocked→unblocked, and the **console projects it**. That's the "tie it all together" backbone.

Things I'll **self-resolve** (say so if you disagree): consuming Nate's content via the Nate B. Jones Executive Circle MCP *and* the public article; treating the console as a projector not a redesign target; exploring the 7→canonical-states compression as design (not pre-asking it); using `plan` to produce a `plan/<topic>/` thread with research notes + a self-sufficient handoff prompt.

Two genuine forks I do want your call on before I invest:

---

Realization then routes out: a thin pattern/contract into core spec, the Beads-metadata + Dispatcher-WIP realization into the orchestrator, projection updates into the console.

content.text:
Good — and I want to explicitly fold in the framing you just added: **whether this overseer/state-machine system sits *on top of* `plan`, *replaces* it, or *subsumes* it.** That's a first-class design axis, and my early instinct is "subsume the *tracking*, not the skill" — i.e., make the state machine the single lifecycle substrate that governs all long-lived units (epics, work-items, **and** planning threads), with `plan` remaining the planning-lane *interaction surface* rather than a parallel tracking mechanism. I'll treat that as a named open question in the design doc, not pre-decide it. Continuing research now.

Let me dispatch the two scoped research agents (Gastown; prior art) to run in the background while I work the Nate B. Jones source and Beads' real status model directly.

input.description: Research Gastown / Steve Yegge
input.prompt:
You are a READ-ONLY research agent. Do NOT modify any files, run git, or mutate anything. Your job is to produce a tight, well-sourced written synthesis I will fold into a design research document. Use web search/fetch tools (WebSearch, WebFetch — load their schemas via ToolSearch "select:WebSearch,WebFetch" if not already available; the Bright Data MCP is an alternative if WebFetch is blocked).

TOPIC: "Gastown" — the agentic software-engineering system associated with Steve Yegge. Context: Steve Yegge is the creator of "Beads" (an issue-tracker/memory system for AI coding agents, the `bd` CLI). He works in the agentic-coding space (Sourcegraph / "Amp" / chat-oriented programming). The user I'm designing for says "Gastown has many corollaries to the system Steve Yegge has created there." I need to know precisely what Gastown is and how it manages a fleet/factory of coding agents.

ANSWER THESE, grounded in real sources (do NOT guess — if you can't verify something, say so explicitly):
1. What IS Gastown? (A product? An internal codename? A demo/talk? A blog series? Who/what is it tied to — Sourcegraph, Amp, a personal project?) Give the canonical definition with the source.
2. Its architecture for running MANY coding agents at once: how is work queued, dispatched, and tracked? Is there a notion of a work-item lifecycle / state machine? What states or stages does work move through?
3. Concurrency / "work in progress" control: does it limit how many agents/tasks run at once? Any Kanban-/WIP-/pull-style concept? How does it decide what to start next?
4. Human-in-the-loop gates: where and how does a human review, approve, unblock, or accept agent work? Is there a distinct "needs human" vs "blocked on infra/external" distinction?
5. How does Beads (bd) relate to Gastown — is Beads the ledger underneath it? How are issues/dependencies modeled?
6. Any explicit "loops of loops" / meta-orchestration framing (an outer loop supervising inner agent loops).
7. The 3-5 most transferable design lessons for someone building a deterministic, WIP-limited work-item state machine that an "overseer" meta-loop drives across many parallel agent sessions.

Search terms to try: "Steve Yegge Gastown", "Yegge Gastown agents", "Gastown Beads Yegge", "Steve Yegge agent fleet orchestration", "Yegge Amp agents", "Steve Yegge beads issue tracker agents", "Yegge babysitting agents loop". Also check Yegge's Substack/Medium ("Stevey"), Sourcegraph blog, and the beads GitHub repo's docs.

OUTPUT: ~500-800 words of markdown, structured under the 7 headings above, every nontrivial claim followed by a source URL in parentheses. End with a "Sources:" list. If Gastown turns out to be obscure/unverifiable, say so plainly and report the closest verifiable Yegge agent-orchestration material instead. Your returned text IS the deliverable (it is not shown to a human directly — it's raw data I will synthesize), so make it dense and citation-rich.

input.description: Research WIP/Linear/agentic state models
input.prompt:
You are a READ-ONLY research agent. Do NOT modify files, run git, or mutate anything. Produce a tight, well-sourced written synthesis I will fold into a design research document about a deterministic, WIP-limited work-item state machine for an AI "software factory." Use web tools (WebSearch, WebFetch — load via ToolSearch "select:WebSearch,WebFetch" if needed; Bright Data MCP as fallback).

Cover THREE areas. Keep each grounded in real sources; cite URLs inline. Do not pad.

AREA 1 — Kanban WIP-limit / pull-system theory (concise, ~150 words):
- Why impose a Work-In-Progress limit per lane/column? The core argument that capping concurrent work forces completion over starting and exposes bottlenecks.
- Little's Law (and its practical reading: lead time = WIP / throughput) and what it implies for a factory that wants short cycle time.
- The idea of a "pull" system vs "push," and "stop starting, start finishing."
- 2-3 crisp, citable takeaways for setting per-lane WIP caps.

AREA 2 — Linear's workflow-state model (~200 words):
- Linear groups issue statuses into a small set of STATE CATEGORIES (e.g. Backlog, Unstarted/Todo, Started/In Progress, Completed/Done, Canceled). List the canonical categories and the default statuses inside them.
- How "In Review" / "In Progress" sub-states work, and how teams add custom statuses within a category.
- Triage state and what it's for.
- Why a small set of *categories* with customizable *statuses inside each* is a good pattern (the category drives behavior/automation; the status is the human-facing label). Cite Linear docs.

AREA 3 — Agentic "factory" / autonomous-engineering work-state models (~300 words):
- Pick 2-3 real systems and describe how each models a unit of work's lifecycle and its human-gate / blocked states. Good candidates: Devin (Cognition) task/session lifecycle; Factory.ai "Droids"; OpenHands (formerly OpenDevin); Cosine Genie; or similar. For each: what are the work states, how is "needs human input" represented, how is "blocked on environment/external" represented (if at all), and is there any concurrency/WIP cap on parallel agent tasks?
- Note specifically whether any of them distinguish (a) "blocked, needs a human" from (b) "blocked on infra/external/non-human" from (c) "claimed done but awaiting human verification/acceptance" — these three distinctions matter to my design.

OUTPUT: ~650-900 words of markdown under the three AREA headings, citations inline as (URL), then a final "Sources:" list. toolUseResult.prompt:
You are a READ-ONLY research agent. Do NOT modify files, run git, or mutate anything. Produce a tight, well-sourced written synthesis I will fold into a design research document about a deterministic, WIP-limited work-item state machine for an AI "software factory." Use web tools (WebSearch, WebFetch — load via ToolSearch "select:WebSearch,WebFetch" if needed; Bright Data MCP as fallback).

Cover THREE areas. Keep each grounded in real sources; cite URLs inline. Do not pad.

AREA 1 — Kanban WIP-limit / pull-system theory (concise, ~150 words):
- Why impose a Work-In-Progress limit per lane/column? The core argument that capping concurrent work forces completion over starting and exposes bottlenecks.
- Little's Law (and its practical reading: lead time = WIP / throughput) and what it implies for a factory that wants short cycle time.
- The idea of a "pull" system vs "push," and "stop starting, start finishing."
- 2-3 crisp, citable takeaways for setting per-lane WIP caps.

AREA 2 — Linear's workflow-state model (~200 words):
- Linear groups issue statuses into a small set of STATE CATEGORIES (e.g. Backlog, Unstarted/Todo, Started/In Progress, Completed/Done, Canceled). List the canonical categories and the default statuses inside them.
- How "In Review" / "In Progress" sub-states work, and how teams add custom statuses within a category.
- Triage state and what it's for.
- Why a small set of *categories* with customizable *statuses inside each* is a good pattern (the category drives behavior/automation; the status is the human-facing label). Cite Linear docs.

AREA 3 — Agentic "factory" / autonomous-engineering work-state models (~300 words):
- Pick 2-3 real systems and describe how each models a unit of work's lifecycle and its human-gate / blocked states. Good candidates: Devin (Cognition) task/session lifecycle; Factory.ai "Droids"; OpenHands (formerly OpenDevin); Cosine Genie; or similar. For each: what are the work states, how is "needs human input" represented, how is "blocked on environment/external" represented (if at all), and is there any concurrency/WIP cap on parallel agent tasks?
- Note specifically whether any of them distinguish (a) "blocked, needs a human" from (b) "blocked on infra/external/non-human" from (c) "claimed done but awaiting human verification/acceptance" — these three distinctions matter to my design.

OUTPUT: ~650-900 words of markdown under the three AREA headings, citations inline as (URL), then a final "Sources:" list. If a system can't be verified, say so and substitute the closest verifiable one. Your returned text IS the deliverable raw data (not shown to a human directly) — make it dense and citation-rich.

input.command:
cd /data/projects/beads 2>/dev/null
echo "==== status / state enum in beads.go ===="
grep -niE 'Status|StatusOpen|StatusInProgress|StatusBlocked|StatusClosed|StatusReady|"open"|"in_progress"|"blocked"|"closed"|"ready"' beads.go 2>/dev/null | head -60
