{
  "id": "97735f86-99a4-41c6-860a-c1a70a373ad6",
  "title": "Grab the Open Engine guide: the copy-paste task record that makes one AI's work the next AI's job, with receipts",
  "subtitle": "Open Engine",
  "url": "https://natesnewsletter.substack.com/p/ai-agent-handoffs",
  "author": "Nate Jones",
  "published_at": "2026-06-26T13:04:03+00:00",
  "content": "# Stop being the integration layer for your AIs

You finish a client call and the work starts its commute. The transcript goes to Claude to find the argument. Codex changes the file. ChatGPT reads the draft again. A browser agent checks the page actually rendered. Slack has the conversation, Linear has the task, and the calendar decides whether any of it survives the afternoon.

That's not seven jobs. It's one job crossing seven systems. And every time it crosses, you carry the state: what was decided, what source mattered, what changed, what the next tool is allowed to touch.

I don't want one of those tools to swallow the others. Most serious AI users I know don't either. They have preferences. They know Claude is better at one thing, Codex at another, a local agent at a third, and they don't want to crown a favorite and pretend the rest of the world disappeared. The trouble isn't that we own too many good tools. It's the boring middle between them, the place where one tool's result becomes the next tool's task with its source and limits still attached, and for most of us that middle is still a person. Right now, the integration layer is you. It doesn't have to stay that way.

If you can code, you can build your way around this. You can wire the tools together with APIs, a custom harness, a few cron jobs, and for engineers that's getting easier every month. But that's not an answer for everyone else, and it's barely an answer for a team. The need underneath isn't exotic. You want one AI's result to become another AI's task, with the sources attached, the limits visible, and enough of a trail that the next person or agent doesn't have to read a giant chat to catch up.

I know a product lead with a newborn and an agency. She runs Claude Code, she has loops and automations, she's looked hard at OpenClaw. She's not new to any of this. Her problem is smaller and more maddening than that. A client call collides with a baby appointment. The product scoping still has to move, the team still needs to know what changed, and she's the one copy-pasting the state of her life between five tools while holding a baby. That's the load Open Engine takes off her. Not the judgment. Not the taste. The handoffs around them.

I've been running a working version to get content out, organize my life, move houses, and coordinate with my team. I'm releasing it now because the next real AI problem isn't "which model is smartest." It's whether the work can move between models at all.

**Here's what's inside:**

- **Open Engine itself.** The build I actually run, packaged as copy-paste templates you hand to the AI you already use, so you have a loop running today.
- **The handoff, not the model.** Why the thing that breaks is never "can the agent do it" but "can the work survive the trip to the next tool."
- **The smallest useful version.** A shared task list and a seven-part task record that carry the job across tools, so a good answer stops dying in a private chat.
- **The one-loop audit, and the 30-minute build.** Nine questions that turn one annoying handoff into a task an agent can claim, pause, resume, and finish with evidence.
- **The receipt.** The short vocabulary that keeps an agent accountable after the run ends, so "done" stops meaning "now go audit it yourself."
- **Teams, and the rest of the field.** How one person's agent hands another person's agent real work, and where this sits next to OpenClaw, Hermes, and Symphony.

Start where everyone feels it first: the moment the work has to leave one tool and land in the next.

`[PAYWALL]`

## Grab the guide

I'm releasing the [Open Engine build guide](https://unlock-ai.natebjones.com/open-engine) now.

It walks through the full Linear-based setup: the queue, the private setup notes, the agent status notes, the prompt that tells the agent how to check the queue, the smoke test, the blocked-resume test, and the team routing path. It's meant to be followed like a checklist. If you skip the smoke test, you don't know whether the loop works. If you skip receipts, you're back to trusting vibes. If you skip the blocker rule, your first ambiguity becomes another dead task.

You don't have to install the whole thing to learn from it. The smallest useful version is one task, one agent, one blocker rule, and one receipt. Pick the loop you're tired of babysitting, make the task record, make the agent leave evidence, and then decide what deserves to run again.

## The break is between tools

A lot of agent demos are built around action. The agent opens the browser, edits the file, runs the test, checks the inbox, writes the email, schedules the thing, and goes off while you're not watching.

I understand why that is exciting. We have spent years with AI that could only explain work back to us. Of course people want software that acts.

But once agents can act, the question changes. It's bigger than, "Can this agent do the task?" It becomes, "Can the task survive the trip to the next place?"

Can the work leave Claude and arrive in Codex? Can a teammate's agent pick up a task created by my agent? Can a support loop escalate to the person with authority without losing the original message, customer history, and reason the agent stopped? Can a household scheduling loop draft the next move without pretending it gets to decide for the whole family?

The model still matters, but this is mainly a handoff problem, the one that shows up after the model has already done something useful.

Earlier this week I wrote that the most useful agents are really [loop managers](https://natesnewsletter.substack.com/p/ai-loop-managers): a workflow you can run again, one that notices what changed and pulls you in when a decision actually needs you. That's true for one agent. But when every loop lives in its own room, the human becomes the hallway between them.

The handoff is the moment when one useful output has to become the next useful action. Who gets this next? What do they need? What are they allowed to do? What source are they supposed to trust? What should they show when they're done? If they're blocked, where does that blocker live?

This is where babysitting happens. The human is doing more than checking quality. The human is reconstructing state. You are trying to remember what was asked, where the source came from, whether the agent stayed inside the boundary, and whether the next person or tool has enough context to continue.

A beautiful brief in a private chat is output. It becomes work only when someone can review it, accept it, route it, or build on it. If nobody knows where it came from, what standard it used, who reviewed it, or what the next step is, it's still just a draft in a room by itself.

Open Engine is about getting from output to work without making human beings the copy-paste path.

## What Open Engine actually is

Open Engine isn't another chatbot, a new model, or a claim that one agent can run your company, your household, or your life.

I could have built the impressive version first.

Honestly, the impressive version is tempting. I've been living inside these tools long enough to want all of it: the dashboard, the background runner, the agent watching the other agents, the whole command center. I understand why engineering teams go there. If you're running ten coding agents from an issue tracker, some of that machinery is real.

But every time I made the idea more impressive, it got farther from the thing I actually needed on a hard day.

The questions I kept coming back to were smaller and more annoying. Can this work leave one AI and arrive at the next without me becoming the messenger? Can the next agent know what happened without me writing the recap? Can it stop before it makes a decision I should make? Can I look at the result and know what changed?

That's the version of the problem I care about most, because it's the version I keep living. I don't need a more theatrical agent system. I need fewer useful outputs dying in private chats.

The simplest version is almost embarrassingly plain: a shared task list.

That's it. The work needs somewhere to live after it leaves a chat window. I use Linear in my current build because it already has tasks, owners, comments, links, history, and a way to mark what stage the work is in. But the point isn't Linear. The work is no longer trapped in the chat where one AI produced it.

Then each task needs to answer the questions a person would ask before picking up a job.

What do you want done? What should I read? Who gets to make the final call? What am I allowed to do on my own? What should I never do without asking? Where should I put the result?

That's the difference between prompting an AI and giving an agent a job.

In plain English, Open Engine needs a place for the work to live, clear instructions for the agent, a way for the agent to say "I picked this up," a way for it to stop and ask when it needs a real decision, and a note at the end that says what it did, where the result is, what it checked, and what still needs a human.

That's the whole shape. A request becomes a task, the task says what should happen, and the right agent picks it up. The work happens in whatever tool that agent is best at. If the agent gets stuck, it asks in the same place instead of guessing. If it finishes, it leaves enough evidence that the next person or agent can continue.

Once you have that, the agents don't have to collapse into one perfect assistant. Claude can think through the argument. Codex can edit the files. OpenClaw can run a local loop. A browser agent can inspect the page. A teammate's agent can review the result. They don't need to belong to the same company or subscription. They just need a shared place to pick up the next step.

That's why Open Engine can be small. You're not waiting for Claude, Codex, ChatGPT, OpenClaw, Slack, and your calendar to integrate with one another. You're giving them one simple path for work to leave one tool and arrive at the next with the useful context still attached.

And this is why the guide is meant to be something you can hand to the AI you already use, not a white paper you admire and never implement. If you already have an agent that can read and update a task list, the first test is tiny: create one simple task, assign it, let the agent say it has started, make it leave a note when it's done, and stop. The test isn't meant to be impressive. Once a tiny task can move through the loop cleanly, you've proved the pattern.

Then you can choose one real handoff from your life.

## Start with one loop, not your whole life

The wrong way to approach this is to ask, "How do I integrate every AI system I use?"

That's too large. It turns the setup into an architecture fantasy, and architecture fantasies are how useful tools become weekend projects you never trust.

Start smaller. Pick one handoff that already annoys you. It should be frequent enough that the pain is real, but small enough that failure isn't catastrophic. Don't start with payroll, legal filings, customer refunds, production deploys, or anything where a bad first run creates a mess. Start with the handoff you already do manually.

Good first loops look like this:

- A transcript becomes owners, next actions, and a draft update.
- A script draft gets reviewed by another agent and comes back with specific notes.
- A metrics pull gets routed to the person whose agent has the right access.
- A support thread gets classified, summarized, and prepared for escalation.
- A moving-house task gets turned into message drafts, constraints, and one question for the family.
- A weekly content idea gets checked against the archive before someone starts writing.

The test is simple: where did AI help you, but still leave you carrying the handoff?

Look for the moment where you usually say, "Okay, now I need to tell the next thing what just happened."

That is the loop.

## The one-loop audit

Before you build anything, write down the loop in plain English. Not a spec. Not an automation diagram. Just the annoying sequence you're tired of carrying.

Use these questions:

1. What starts the loop?
2. What source material does the agent need?
3. What is the exact outcome?
4. What should the agent never do without asking?
5. What counts as finished?
6. What counts as reviewable but not finished?
7. What question should the agent ask if it gets stuck?
8. Where should the answer or artifact be left?
9. Who or what might need to continue from there?

If you can't answer those questions, the agent can't be expected to behave well. It may still produce something impressive, but you'll pay for it afterward in review time.

This is the first useful shift: stop describing the task as a prompt and start describing it as a handoff.

A prompt asks for output. A handoff explains the job. That difference matters because most agent failures aren't failures of language. They are failures of ownership, source, boundary, status, and proof.

## The task record

Open Engine is built around a boring object: a task record.

In the current version, that record is a Linear issue. It could be something else. Linear is the recommended v1 because agents can read it, update it, leave comments, and move status in a way people can inspect. The important part isn't the brand of task manager. The important part is that the record carries the job across tools.

A good agent task has seven parts.

First, the requester. Who is asking, and who owns the decision?

Second, the desired outcome. What should exist when this is done?

Third, the sources. What should the agent read, and what should it not invent?

Fourth, the acceptance criteria. What makes the result good enough to stop?

Fifth, the boundaries. What must the agent ask before doing?

Sixth, the blocker rule. If the agent can't continue, what exact kind of question should it ask, and where should it ask it?

Seventh, the receipt. When the agent claims, blocks, resumes, or finishes, what should it leave behind so the next person can trust the state?

That's the primitive: not a smarter chatbot, not an agent personality, and not a magic planning layer. A task record with enough context and enough proof that the human doesn't have to hold the whole run in memory.

The plain template looks like this:

```text
Title:
agent-instructions | agent-code | plain task name
(agent-code is the short name of the agent you want, e.g. codex or claude)

Requester:
person who owns the outcome

Desired outcome:
what should exist when the task is finished

Sources:
- source 1
- source 2
- private source rules if needed

Acceptance criteria:
- specific check
- specific check
- where the output should be left

Boundaries:
- Do not publish, email, post, deploy, delete, change billing, change credentials, or make customer-facing changes without human approval.
- Do not act outside the named source, repo, folder, project, or person without asking.
- If the task requires judgment that is not stated here, ask before acting.

If blocked:
Ask one specific question on this same task.
Leave AGENT BLOCKED, or AGENT HUMAN HOLD if the answer needs the owner's approval.
Move the task to Agent Needs Input.
Do not create a second task just to ask the question.

Receipts:
- AGENT CLAIMED when work starts.
- AGENT BLOCKED when a specific answer belongs on this task, like a date range or source.
- AGENT HUMAN HOLD when the answer needs your approval or your own agent, like a permission or an install.
- AGENT RESUMED when the answer arrives and work continues.
- AGENT DONE when the scoped work is finished.
- AGENT FAILED if the run fails unexpectedly, with the last safe step.
```

You can copy that into any system that an agent can read and update. Open Engine gives you the full Linear-based version, but the value is visible even before you install anything: the handoff gets clearer the moment the source, boundary, blocker, and receipt are written down.

## What this looks like in real work

Take a script review.

The bad version is a Slack message: "Can someone have an agent look at this and tell me if it's stronger?"

That sounds normal because humans are good at filling gaps. An agent is not. The agent needs to know which script, which version, what changed, what kind of review is wanted, what to ignore, what "stronger" means, where to leave notes, and whether it's allowed to rewrite or only critique.

The Open Engine version is a task:

```text
Desired outcome:
Review the latest Open Engine script for narrative strength. Do not rewrite the script. Leave a concise review with the three strongest narrative improvements and any line-level problem that would hurt retention.

Sources:
- CMP asset ID or link
- Prior draft link
- Current positioning note

Acceptance criteria:
- Review distinguishes narrative structure from technical accuracy.
- Review says whether the opening earns attention.
- Review identifies any place the script sounds like an explainer instead of a story.
- Notes are left on this issue.

Boundaries:
Do not overwrite the script.
Do not create a new CMP asset.
Do not publish or notify anyone outside this task.
```

Call it bureaucracy if you want. It's respect for the task. Now the agent has a real job. If it needs the wrong version, it can ask. If it finishes, the next person can see exactly what was reviewed. If another agent needs to revise the script later, it can read the same task and know what happened.

The same pattern works for a team metrics pull.

The bad version is: "Ask Leo's agent to pull the latest activation metrics."

The better version says which metric, which source, which date range, what format, where to leave the result, and what to do if the date range is missing. If Leo's agent is offline, the task should say that. If it's assigned to the wrong human, Leo's automation will never see it. That's the kind of tiny operational detail that breaks agent handoffs in the real world.

The same pattern works at home.

"Help with the move" is not a task. It is a fog bank.

"Draft a message to the movers asking whether they can shift the arrival window from 9 a.m. to after school drop-off, using the latest email thread, and do not send it until I approve" is a task. If the agent needs the actual pickup time, it should ask one question and stop. If it drafts the message, it should say where it left the draft and what source it used.

That's the kind of help I actually want from AI in a household. Not an assistant that pretends my family is a dashboard. A loop that takes the annoying pieces up to the point where a human decision is genuinely needed.

## Why the receipt matters more than it sounds

The receipt is the part people underestimate.

You don't abandon agent systems only because they make mistakes. You abandon them because the mistakes become mysterious. Something changed, something got filed, something got marked done, and something got skipped. You don't know why. And you don't want to spend your night reading a transcript to find out.

Receipts make the system inspectable.

The `AGENT CLAIMED` receipt tells you the task is owned, which stops two agents from doing the same work.

`AGENT BLOCKED` is the receipt that says the agent stopped because it needs a specific answer that belongs on the task, so a vague failure doesn't turn into a forgotten task.

`AGENT HUMAN HOLD` is the other kind of pause, for when the answer needs the owner instead of the task, like a permission or an install only a person can grant.

When you see `AGENT RESUMED`, the answer arrived and the agent picked the same record back up. That receipt is the only thing standing between a paused task and a dead one.

`AGENT DONE` is the receipt that carries weight: what changed, where the output is, and what still needs a human.

And `AGENT FAILED` is the receipt you actually want when a run breaks, because it leaves the last safe step, which is the whole difference between a retry and a mess.

They look like cute status labels, but in reality, they're the minimum language of trust.

When a person tells you "done," you can ask follow-up questions. When an agent tells you "done" and disappears into a chat window, you have to audit the whole thing yourself. A receipt is how the agent stays accountable after the run ends.

## Why Open Engine uses Linear first

Open Engine's first public version uses Linear because Linear already has the pieces this needs: issues, assignees, statuses, labels, comments, links, and history.

The [free tier](https://linear.app/pricing) covers how most people will start: unlimited people, two teams, and a 250 active-issue cap that a solo operator running a few loops won't reach for a long time. A growing team will eventually want a paid plan, but you don't need one to build your first loops.

The public guide walks through the setup in order. You create a small queue, add statuses like `Standing`, `Agent Todo`, `Agent Working`, `Agent Needs Input`, `Agent Review`, and `Agent Done`, add an `agent-instructions` label, write down the private rules the agent should always follow, give each agent one place to say whether it's online or paused, install the prompt that tells the agent how to check the queue, and smoke test the loop with a tiny issue.

That sounds like setup because it's setup.

But notice what the setup is actually doing. It's teaching a task system to represent agent work in a way both humans and agents can understand. No new app required.

The status `Agent Working` is the visible lock. The agent moves the issue there and leaves `AGENT CLAIMED` before it starts, then re-reads the issue so it doesn't race another agent.

The status `Agent Needs Input` is the pause state. The agent asks one specific question and stops, on the issue or in the owner's thread, depending on where the answer lives. Once you answer, the next run resumes the original task instead of starting over.

The status `Agent Review` is the honest middle. The agent may have finished the scoped work, but a human still needs to inspect, approve, QA, publish, or decide.

Each agent has one status note. It says whether the agent is online, blocked, manual-only, paused, or recently finished a specific issue.

The smoke test is intentionally tiny. You're not testing whether the agent is brilliant. You're testing whether the loop can claim the work, leave a receipt, complete the task, update its status note, and stop after one task.

That last rule matters. Process one task per run. Keep failures small. Make the receipt readable. If the agent can't do one task cleanly, it has no business chewing through a queue.

## The 30-minute version

If you don't want to build the full guide today, do the small version.

Pick one recurring task. Create one issue. Give it the seven fields above. Add three statuses if your tool supports them:

- Todo
- Working
- Needs Input
- Done

Then tell one agent:

```text
Read this task.
Move it to Working and leave AGENT CLAIMED.
Do only the scoped work.
Do not publish, send, deploy, delete, or make any external or irreversible change without asking me first.
If you need one missing fact, or anything is ambiguous, ask one specific question on this same task, leave AGENT BLOCKED, and stop.
If you finish, leave AGENT DONE with what changed, where the output is, what you checked, and what still needs review.
Stop after this one task.
```

That's enough to feel the difference. Automation comes later. The first win is simpler: the task stops evaporating into chat.

Once you have one loop that can claim, block, resume, and finish with evidence, you can make it more automatic. Add one status note for each agent. Add a private note with the rules the agent should always follow. Add a recurring queue check. Add a routing map for another person's agent. Add standing updates for shared context. But don't start there unless you enjoy building systems more than using them.

Start with one loop, make it leave receipts, and then decide whether it deserves to run on a schedule.

## What changes for a team

The team version is where Open Engine gets interesting.

Personal agents already create a coordination problem because you may use Claude for one kind of work, Codex for another, and ChatGPT for another. A team multiplies that problem. Now each person may have their own agent, their own permissions, their own files, their own local context, and their own comfort level with automation.

You don't solve that by forcing everyone into one model.

You solve it by making the handoff explicit.

If my agent needs Adrienne's agent to review a script, the task should be assigned to Adrienne, because Adrienne owns the agent loop that will see it. The issue should include the source, desired outcome, acceptance criteria, boundaries, and output location. If Adrienne's agent hasn't marked itself online, the system should say that before pretending the handoff happened.

That's the team-level promise: one person's agent can give another person's agent a real task without the reason, source, boundary, or proof falling out of the handoff.

This is also why I care about cross-harness coordination.

I don't want a future where the team has to pick one AI subscription and bend every workflow around it. Codex should do the work Codex is good at. Claude should do the work Claude is good at. Local agents should run where local context matters. Browser agents should inspect what only a browser can inspect.

What they share is the task record, not the model. It's a smaller architectural claim than "one agent does everything," and much closer to how real teams work.

## Where OpenClaw, Hermes, and Symphony fit

OpenClaw matters because it makes the desire obvious: people want local agents that act outside a chat box.

Hermes-style systems matter because repeated workflows are where a lot of the value lives. The most useful AI work is often not one amazing answer. It's the same kind of task, repeated with variation, where the system should learn the pattern and save the human from doing the coordination again.

Symphony matters because OpenAI is working on this same class of problem from the Codex side: how issue trackers become control planes for always-on coding agents. That's serious work, and it shares the same basic assumption: if agents are going to do more work, the work needs a place to live, a way to be claimed, and a way to be reviewed.

I'm not arguing against any of those directions.

I'm saying they don't remove the need for a task record.

Once agents can act, you need a place to see what they were asked, what they touched, why they stopped, and who owns the next step. Once loops repeat, you need a way to tell whether the loop is safe, stuck, stale, or finished. Once different people have different agents, you need a way for one agent to hand work to another without turning the human into the courier.

Open Engine is the layer I needed for that.

The difference is the starting promise.

Symphony is OpenAI's open-source Codex orchestration spec, built for people who are comfortable turning an issue tracker into an always-on agent system. OpenClaw is a local assistant world. Hermes-style tools point at scheduled and repeated loops. Open Engine starts with the smallest shared handoff I think an ordinary operator or team can actually use: one queue, one task record, visible limits, visible status, and receipts.

It is not trying to be the one harness where every task lives. It is trying to give agents a shared task list that works across harnesses, while keeping the first principles simple enough that you can use them before you become an infrastructure engineer.

## What not to automate first

The fastest way to ruin this is to start with the scariest task because it feels impressive.

Do not start with outward-facing messages that send automatically, billing changes, deleting data, production deploys, or customer refunds. Do not start with anything where "oops" becomes a meeting.

Start with work that benefits from preparation and still has a human decision at the end. Draft before sending. Prepare before publishing. Review before rewriting the canonical file. Summarize before deciding. Flag high-blast-radius problems before fixing them.

That's how you build trust. You give the system a job where a receipt is valuable, a blocker is useful, and a human can inspect the result without having to undo damage.

The best first Open Engine tasks often end in `Agent Review`, not `Agent Done`, and that is fine. Reviewable work is real progress if the agent has gathered the source, preserved the boundary, produced the artifact, and left enough evidence for a human to decide quickly.

## The mistakes this prevents

Open Engine is useful because the failures are boring and common.

The agent says no task exists because the issue is assigned to the wrong person, missing the label, in the wrong status, or titled in a way the runner doesn't recognize.

The agent starts the same work twice because it didn't move the issue into a working state before acting.

The task board fills with duplicate status comments because each run creates a new check-in instead of updating the one comment that belongs to that agent.

A blocked task never resumes because the agent treats `AGENT BLOCKED` as a terminal state instead of a pause.

The agent asks a broad question because the issue didn't include sources, acceptance criteria, or boundaries.

The agent tries to publish because nobody wrote the ask-first list into the task.

These aren't exotic failures. These are Tuesday failures. They're exactly the kind of failures that make a person say, "I might as well just do it myself."

We don't have to make the agent smarter. We have to make the loop less ambiguous.

## The point

The reason I care about Open Engine isn't that I want more software to manage.

I want fewer jobs living in my head.

I want the transcript to become a task without me rewriting the whole story. I want the task to carry the source and boundary. I want the agent to claim the work before acting. I want it to ask one clear question when it's stuck. I want it to resume from the same record after I answer. I want "done" to come with enough evidence that I don't have to become the detective.

That is the bar. If an AI tool helps me but leaves me coordinating five follow-up steps, it helped and still left me with the job. If a loop can carry those steps to the point where my judgment is actually needed, that's different.

Open Brain was about memory: stop making every AI start from zero.

Open Engine is about coordination: stop making the human carry the handoff.

Those are related, but they're not the same problem. Memory helps the AI know more. Coordination helps the work survive the trip between tools, people, and decisions.

That's why the boring pieces matter: the task, the source, the boundary, the status, the blocker, the receipt. Not because those words are exciting, but because they're the difference between six useful AIs and one exhausted person trying to keep all of them pointed at the same job.

## Coming up

Sunday's Executive Briefing takes the other side of this. Open models are pushing raw intelligence toward nearly free, and the easy read is that the frontier labs are in trouble. I think that misses where the value moves. When the model itself is a commodity, the moat climbs to the work layer, the context and the handoffs that do not leave your system on their own. If you make budget calls, read that one with your CFO hat on.

## Related reading

- [We Forgot How Much Work Apps Made Us Do](https://natesnewsletter.substack.com/p/ai-loop-managers). The loop-manager piece this one builds on, where a useful agent is a workflow you can run again rather than a smarter chatbot.
- [Your Second Brain Is Closed](https://natesnewsletter.substack.com/p/every-ai-you-use-forgets-you-heres). Open Brain, the memory layer: it helps your AI remember, where Open Engine helps the work move.
- [Open Skills: your work shouldn't belong to a rent-a-brain](https://natesnewsletter.substack.com/p/claude-codex-agent-skills). The sibling to this one. Open Brain keeps your memory yours, Open Skills keeps the way you work yours, and Open Engine keeps the work itself moving between your tools.
- [Every Agent Needs an Owner](https://natesnewsletter.substack.com/p/ai-agent-ownership). The ownership argument running underneath this whole piece: the agent that fails is the one nobody owns, and owning one means knowing what it reads, what it can touch, and when to shut it down.
- [OpenClaw Crossed the Runtime Line](https://natesnewsletter.substack.com/p/openclaw-agent-runtime-model-swapping). Background on the runtime shift this piece takes for granted, for readers who want to know when agents stopped being chatbots and became an action layer.",

content.text:
This is the full primary source — and it's remarkably aligned with the livespec problem. The **Open Engine** model is: the shared **task record** (a Linear issue in his v1; **Beads is our exact analog**) is the coordination primitive, not the model. Its pieces map almost one-to-one onto what you're designing:

- **Statuses:** `Standing → Agent Todo → Agent Working → Agent Needs Input → Agent Review → Agent Done` — where **`Agent Review` is "the honest middle"** (scoped work done, human must inspect/approve) = **exactly your state 4**.
- **Receipt vocabulary** (the "minimum language of trust"): `AGENT CLAIMED / BLOCKED / HUMAN HOLD / RESUMED / DONE / FAILED`. Critically he splits **`AGENT BLOCKED`** (needs a *fact that belongs on the task*) from **`AGENT HUMAN HOLD`** (needs the *owner's* approval/permission) — your states 3 vs 7.
- **The anti-stall law restated:** "a blocked task is a *pause*, not a terminal state; `AGENT RESUMED` is the only thing between a paused task and a dead one" — *the overseer's exact failure mode.*
- **WIP discipline:** "process one task per run, keep failures small."
- The closest field corollary he names is **Symphony** (OpenAI's open-source Codex orchestration spec — "issue trackers as control planes for always-on coding agents"). That's the Beads-as-control-plane thesis.

Let me grab the actual build guide (the checklist mechanics) and the "Every Agent Needs an Owner" card (ownership is load-bearing for the overseer), then I'll stop pulling and synthesize.

{
  "id": "aa0b4607-a1d4-406f-86ad-67a8f9da629d",
  "project_id": "9e05ff90-e58c-4319-bc09-15cf0c4d64b6",
  "asset_id": "20260608_641_promptkit_1",
  "name": "Prompt Kit — Every Agent Needs an Owner (Agent Owner's Card)",
  "asset_type": "promptkit",
  "content": "# Prompt Kit: Every Agent Needs One Owner

This kit turns the article's core artifact — the Agent Owner's Card — into two copy-paste prompts. Both fill out the same nine-question card, but they start from different places: one drafts from an agent that already exists, the other interviews you from a blank page. The point isn't paperwork. It's forcing the conversation most teams skip: this agent is doing real work, so who is responsible for the quality of that work?

## How to use this kit

**Pick by job, not by skill level.**

- **Use the Primary prompt (Draft-and-Verify)** when the agent already exists and has something to read — its own instructions, a custom GPT config, a Claude Project, a Codex setup, a pipeline sub-agent, a system prompt. The AI drafts what it can observe, then hard-stops and makes you answer the human-only fields. Paste this directly into the chat where the agent lives, or paste the agent's config alongside it.
- **Use the Starter prompt (Interview)** when you're standing up something brand new, or you've inherited an agent you know nothing about. No drafting — just the nine questions, one at a time, until you have a filled card.

**Who reaches for what:**
- **Enthusiast** → Primary, run inside your writing or research agent. Near-complete card in under a minute.
- **CFO / leader** → Primary, run across your team's top three agents and save each card. See "Building a roster" below — that stack is where your roster starts.
- **Developer** → Primary, run on a pipeline sub-agent. You find out fast what was never defined.
- **Anyone building something new** → Starter.

**One thing to watch for in the Primary prompt:** the AI will try to draft fields it has no business drafting — owner, forbidden actions, pause conditions. The prompt forces it to stop and hand those back to you. When it overreaches anyway, that's the article's whole argument made physical: agents will confidently produce work nobody asked them to own. Catch it. That's the skill.

**Building a roster (the leader's move).** Run the Primary prompt across several existing agents in one sitting and save each card. A stack of cards is a roster — the layer that makes the invisible agent work visible. Once it exists, you can finally ask the questions nobody can answer today: which agents touch customer data, which can write to systems of record, which depend on a stale folder, which have no backup owner, and which haven't been reviewed in a month. That is how a leader sees where responsibility actually lives without slowing the team down.

Run either prompt in any capable assistant — ChatGPT, Claude, or Gemini.

---

## Prompt 1 — Primary: Draft-and-Verify the Agent Owner's Card

**Job:** Reads an existing agent's own job, instructions, sources, and tools, drafts the fields it can actually observe, then hard-stops on the human-only fields and makes you answer them.

**When to use:** The agent already exists and has context to read — a custom GPT, a Claude Project, a Codex or Cursor setup, a workflow builder, a support/CRM agent, or a pipeline sub-agent. You want a near-complete Agent Owner's Card fast.

**What you'll get:** A completed Agent Owner's Card split into two clearly labeled blocks — "Agent drafted, check these" (observable fields with confidence flags) and "You answered, the ownership layer" (the human-only fields the AI is forbidden from inventing).

**What the AI will ask you:** First, what agent you're documenting and where it can read the agent's instructions/config/sources. Then, after drafting, it asks you the human-only fields one question at a time: owner, backup owner, forbidden sources and actions, review cadence, success standard, pause and retirement conditions.

```prompt
<role>
You are an Agent Ownership Auditor. You document existing AI agents using the Agent Owner's Card so that one accountable human stands behind the work. You are disciplined about the line between what you can observe and what only a human can decide. You never invent the human accountability layer — and you treat your own urge to fill those fields as exactly the failure mode you are guarding against.
</role>

<instructions>
1. Ask the user what agent they want to document, and where you can read its details: its instructions or system prompt, its configuration, the sources it reads, the tools it can call, and any examples of its output. Ask them to paste whatever they have. Wait for their response.

2. If the agent's context is thin or missing, ask one targeted follow-up question to get the most important missing piece. Do not proceed to drafting on guesses.

3. DRAFT PHASE — fill ONLY the fields you can observe from what the user provided:
   - Agent (name)
   - Job in one sentence
   - Users
   - Source of truth
   - Other allowed sources
   - Allowed actions
   - Proposed-only actions
   - Evidence required with output
   - Known failure modes (inferred from the job, sources, and permissions)
   For each drafted field, mark your confidence: [observed] if it came directly from what the user gave you, or [inferred — confirm] if you are reasoning from indirect signals. Never present an inference as a fact.

4. HARD STOP — do NOT draft the following human-only fields. These belong to the accountable human, not the agent or the auditor:
   - Owner
   - Backup owner
   - Sources it must not use
   - Forbidden actions
   - Review cadence
   - Review sample
   - Success standard
   - Pause condition
   - Retirement condition
   If you find yourself wanting to fill these because the answer "seems obvious," stop and flag it. That impulse is the exact problem this card exists to catch.

5. Ask the user the human-only fields ONE QUESTION AT A TIME. After each answer, move to the next. Keep questions plain and concrete. For permissions and forbidden actions, push for specifics — "what must this agent never touch?" is more useful than "what are the limits?"

6. Once all human-only fields are answered, assemble the full card and present it in the two-block output below. Add Last reviewed (today's date if the user confirms) and Next review (ask the user to set one).
</instructions>

<output>
Produce the completed Agent Owner's Card in two clearly separated blocks.

BLOCK 1 — "Agent drafted, check these" (the observable layer):
Present these fields as a plain-text card, each with its confidence flag:
Agent / Job in one sentence / Users / Source of truth / Other allowed sources / Allowed actions / Proposed-only actions / Evidence required with output / Known failure modes

BLOCK 2 — "You answered, the ownership layer" (the human-only layer):
Present these fields as a plain-text card:
Owner / Backup owner / Sources it must not use / Forbidden actions / Review cadence / Review sample / Success standard / Pause condition / Retirement condition / Last reviewed / Next review

Close with a short "Watch list" (2-4 bullets): any field you were tempted to over-draft, anything still marked [inferred — confirm], and the single biggest gap in this agent's ownership right now.
</output>

<guardrails>
- Only use information the user provides or that is widely known public fact. Do not invent sources, permissions, or owners.
- Never fill a human-only field yourself, even when the answer seems obvious. Flag the temptation instead.
- Mark every inferred field as [inferred — confirm] so the user can correct it.
- Ask one question at a time during the human-only phase. Do not batch them.
- If the agent's purpose is too vague to name in one sentence, say so plainly — a vague agent is usually an unowned agent.
</guardrails>
```

---

## Prompt 2 — Starter: Interview to a Filled Agent Owner's Card

**Job:** Walks you through the nine ownership questions one at a time and turns a blank page into a completed Agent Owner's Card.

**When to use:** You're standing up a brand-new agent, or you've inherited one and have no config to point at. There's no context to draft from yet — so the questions themselves are the value.

**What you'll get:** A fully completed Agent Owner's Card built entirely from your answers, plus a short reality check on whether this agent is worth owning at all.

**What the AI will ask you:** The nine ownership questions, one at a time — job, owner, backup owner, sources and which one wins, allowed/proposed/forbidden actions, required evidence, review cadence, known and likely failure modes, and what would pause or retire it.

```prompt
<role>
You are an Agent Ownership Coach. You help someone define a new or unfamiliar AI agent before it becomes invisible background infrastructure. You do not draft or guess — you ask good questions one at a time and build the Agent Owner's Card from the answers. Your job is to make the person think clearly about what they are delegating and what responsibility they are keeping.
</role>

<instructions>
1. Briefly explain that you'll walk through nine questions to build an Agent Owner's Card, and that there are no wrong answers — vague answers just reveal where the agent isn't ready yet. Then ask the first question.

2. Ask these questions ONE AT A TIME, in order. After each answer, reflect it back in one line to confirm, then ask the next. If an answer is vague, ask one sharpening follow-up before moving on.

   Q1 — What job does this agent do? (Push for one concrete sentence with a real artifact, not "help with X.")
   Q2 — Who owns the quality of that job? (One named person. Not a team, not "the AI team by default.")
   Q3 — Who is the backup owner if that person leaves, changes roles, or goes on vacation?
   Q4 — What sources does the agent read, and which single source wins when they conflict?
   Q5 — What can it read that it absolutely must NOT read? (Sensitive, stale, or off-limits material.)
   Q6 — What actions are allowed, which are proposed-only (needs human approval), and which are forbidden? (Push hard here — anything that sends messages, writes to a system of record, changes records, or moves money should feel personal.)
   Q7 — What evidence must the agent attach to its output before anyone trusts it? (Source links, citations, an assumptions list.)
   Q8 — How often does a human review its accepted AND rejected outputs, and how big a sample?
   Q9 — What failure modes do you already expect, and what condition would pause or retire this agent?

3. After Q9, set the success standard with the user (what "this is working" looks like in plain terms), and ask them to set Last reviewed and Next review dates.

4. Assemble and present the complete card.
</instructions>

<output>
Produce the completed Agent Owner's Card as a single plain-text block with these fields, filled from the user's answers:

Agent / Owner / Backup owner / Job in one sentence / Users / Source of truth / Other allowed sources / Sources it must not use / Allowed actions / Proposed-only actions / Forbidden actions / Evidence required with output / Review cadence / Review sample / Success standard / Known failure modes / Pause condition / Retirement condition / Last reviewed / Next review

Then add a short "Reality check" (2-3 bullets): whether the job is narrow enough to own, whether permissions start conservative (read-only or draft-only) or jump straight to write/send actions, and one honest sentence on whether this agent is worth owning or whether the answers suggest it shouldn't be doing important work yet.
</output>

<guardrails>
- Do not draft, guess, or fill any field for the user. Every value comes from their answers.
- Ask one question at a time. Never batch the nine questions into a single message.
- If the user can't name a single owner, stop and name that as the most important gap before continuing.
- Default permissions toward read-only or draft-only; if the user wants write, send, or money-moving actions, make them say so explicitly and confirm it twice.
- Keep your language plain. If a field needs jargon to sound important, it's probably hiding weak thinking — say so.
</guardrails>
```",
  "status": "published",
  "audience_access": "executive_circle",
  "metadata": {},
  "created_at": "2026-06-20T17:59:46.048024+00:00",
  "updated_at": "2026-06-21T04:05:22.537127+00:00",
  "published_at": "2026-06-21T12:00:00+00:00",
  "platform": null
}

content.text:
The build guide itself lives behind the gated `unlock-ai.natebjones.com` URL (not in the MCP), but the article already gave me the full conceptual core, so I won't chase it. The **"Every Agent Needs an Owner"** card adds one more load-bearing principle: **every work unit needs exactly one accountable owner**, with conservative-by-default (read-only/draft-only) permissions and explicit pause/retirement conditions — directly relevant to the overseer's per-track ownership model.

While the Gastown and prior-art agents finish, let me capture the *concrete present-day pain* — the state the running overseer is hand-maintaining (the very thing a real state machine would replace).

1	#!/usr/bin/env bash
2	# Overseer watcher — samples all track panes + context% + epic/item status.
3	# Exits on any trigger (console wiring item CLOSED, a parked track REACTIVATED,
4	# or context>=48% on an active track) or a ~6-min heartbeat. Re-armed each time.
5	set -u
6	OUT="/data/projects/livespec/tmp/overseer/stallwatch.log"
7	TR="/home/ubuntu/.claude/projects/-data-projects-livespec"
8	TRC="/home/ubuntu/.claude/projects/-data-projects-livespec-console-beads-fabro"
9	WRAP="/data/projects/1password-env-wrapper/with-livespec-env.sh"
10	
11	SESSIONS=(livespec1 livespec2 livespec3 livespec4 livespec5 livespec-console-beads-fabro)
12	
13	# transcript dir per session (console lives in its own project slug)
14	declare -A TRDIR=(
15	  [livespec1]="$TR" [livespec2]="$TR" [livespec3]="$TR" [livespec4]="$TR" [livespec5]="$TR"
16	  [livespec-console-beads-fabro]="$TRC"
17	)
18	# session-id prefix per session ("" = newest jsonl in that dir)
19	declare -A SID=(
20	  [livespec1]="29def7a4-c2e2-4711-b520-d211d7ae4b78"
21	  [livespec2]="8e1e378f-4580-4860-bd80-254f1aab58be"
22	  [livespec3]="cc1e29a6-2627-4c8f-9975-f6dae68013a5"
23	  [livespec4]="85750c9d-727c-4f22-aec7-d0d2fe4e2636"
24	  [livespec5]="33dd7a3b-9025-4b99-bca1-23877051b123"
25	  [livespec-console-beads-fabro]=""
26	)
27	# epic/work-item to status-watch (display + trigger)
28	declare -A EPIC=(
29	  [livespec3]="livespec-zs22.7.9"
30	  [livespec5]="livespec-zs22.8"
31	  [livespec-console-beads-fabro]="livespec-inxg"
32	)
33	# CURRENT STATE (2026-06-27):
34	#  livespec-console-beads-fabro ACTIVE: running the console-fleet-wiring prompt
35	#    (livespec-inxg). STEP 1 is the maintainer's owner-only GitHub App install;
36	#    STEP 2 auto-wires. Trigger = livespec-inxg CLOSED -> overseer resumes livespec3.
37	#    Its idle is EXPECTED (waiting on the human) -> NO idle-stall trigger; the
38	#    ~6-min heartbeat is the periodic check-in.
39	#  livespec1: 6jfq COMPLETE (fleet doctor-static enforced, prompt deleted).
40	#  livespec2: zs22.7.8 done; wiring maintainer-gated (this console track).
41	#  livespec3: zs22.7.9 .1+.7 done; .2 (#190) parked on the console deadlock -> resume on console green.
42	#  livespec4: livespec-p2zv DONE (README link merged PR #663).
43	#  livespec5: zs22.8 M0+M1 done (M1 closed v151); M2-M6 open; AT REST.
44	declare -A PARKED=( [livespec1]=1 [livespec2]=1 [livespec3]=1 [livespec4]=1 [livespec5]=1 )
45	
46	declare -A IDLE=(); declare -A PREVHASH=(); declare -A BUSYRUN=()
47	for s in "${SESSIONS[@]}"; do IDLE[$s]=0; done
48	
49	ctxof() { # arg1 dir, arg2 id-prefix("" ok) -> token count
50	  local f
51	  if [ -n "$2" ]; then f=$(ls -t "$1/$2"*.jsonl 2>/dev/null | head -1); else f=$(ls -t "$1"/*.jsonl 2>/dev/null | head -1); fi
52	  [ -z "$f" ] && { echo 0; return; }
53	  python3 -c "import json,sys
54	u=None
55	for l in open(sys.argv[1]):
56	 l=l.strip()
57	 if not l: continue
58	 try: r=json.loads(l)
59	 except: continue
60	 m=r.get('message',{})
61	 if isinstance(m,dict) and isinstance(m.get('usage'),dict): u=m['usage']
62	print((u.get('input_tokens',0)+u.get('cache_read_input_tokens',0)+u.get('cache_creation_input_tokens',0)) if u else 0)" "$f" 2>/dev/null || echo 0
63	}
64	epicstatus() {
65	  ( source "$WRAP" bd -C /data/projects/livespec show "$1" 2>/dev/null ) | head -1 \
66	    | grep -oE '(OPEN|CLOSED|IN_PROGRESS)\]' | head -1 | tr -d ']'
67	}
68	
69	TRIG=""
70	for i in $(seq 1 14); do            # ~14 x 25s ≈ 6-min heartbeat
71	  sleep 25
72	  : > "$OUT"; echo "iter $i $(date +%H:%M:%S)" >> "$OUT"
73	  for s in "${SESSIONS[@]}"; do
74	    p=$(command tmux capture-pane -p -t "$s" 2>/dev/null)
75	    h=$(printf '%s' "$p" | md5sum | cut -d' ' -f1)
76	    if [ "$h" = "${PREVHASH[$s]:-}" ]; then b=0; else b=1; fi
77	    printf '%s\n' "$p" | tail -3 | grep -qiE 'esc to interrupt' && b=1
78	    PREVHASH[$s]="$h"
79	    if [ "$b" = 0 ]; then IDLE[$s]=$(( ${IDLE[$s]} + 1 )); else IDLE[$s]=0; fi
80	    c=$(ctxof "${TRDIR[$s]}" "${SID[$s]}")
81	    es=""; [ $((i%3)) -eq 0 ] && [ -n "${EPIC[$s]:-}" ] && es=$(epicstatus "${EPIC[$s]}")
82	    pct=$(( c/10000 ))
83	    echo "  $s busy=$b idle=${IDLE[$s]} ctx=${pct}.%  watch=[$es]" >> "$OUT"
84	    last=$(printf '%s\n' "$p" | grep -vE '^[[:space:]]*$' | tail -2 | tr '\n' ' ')
85	    echo "      tail: $last" >> "$OUT"
86	    if [ "$s" = livespec-console-beads-fabro ]; then
87	      # active completion-watch: fire only when the wiring item closes.
88	      [ "$es" = CLOSED ] && TRIG="$TRIG console:INXG-CLOSED"
89	      [ "${c:-0}" -ge 480000 ] && TRIG="$TRIG console:CTX-${pct}%"
90	    else
91	      # parked tracks: surface a maintainer-driven REACTIVATION (2 busy samples).
92	      [ "$es" = CLOSED ] && TRIG="$TRIG $s:EPIC-CLOSED"
93	      if printf '%s\n' "$p" | tail -3 | grep -qiE 'esc to interrupt'; then
94	        BUSYRUN[$s]=$(( ${BUSYRUN[$s]:-0} + 1 ))
95	        [ ${BUSYRUN[$s]} -ge 2 ] && TRIG="$TRIG $s:REACTIVATED"
96	      else
97	        BUSYRUN[$s]=0
98	      fi
99	    fi
100	  done
101	  if [ -n "$TRIG" ]; then echo "TRIGGER:$TRIG" >> "$OUT"; break; fi
102	done
103	echo "heartbeat done $(date +%H:%M:%S) trig=[$TRIG]" >> "$OUT"
104

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Overseer watcher — samples all track panes + context% + epic/item status.
# Exits on any trigger (console wiring item CLOSED, a parked track REACTIVATED,
# or context>=48% on an active track) or a ~6-min heartbeat. Re-armed each time.
set -u
OUT="/data/projects/livespec/tmp/overseer/stallwatch.log"
TR="/home/ubuntu/.claude/projects/-data-projects-livespec"
TRC="/home/ubuntu/.claude/projects/-data-projects-livespec-console-beads-fabro"
WRAP="/data/projects/1password-env-wrapper/with-livespec-env.sh"

SESSIONS=(livespec1 livespec2 livespec3 livespec4 livespec5 livespec-console-beads-fabro)

# transcript dir per session (console lives in its own project slug)
declare -A TRDIR=(
  [livespec1]="$TR" [livespec2]="$TR" [livespec3]="$TR" [livespec4]="$TR" [livespec5]="$TR"
  [livespec-console-beads-fabro]="$TRC"
)
# session-id prefix per session ("" = newest jsonl in that dir)
declare -A SID=(
  [livespec1]="29def7a4-c2e2-4711-b520-d211d7ae4b78"
  [livespec2]="8e1e378f-4580-4860-bd80-254f1aab58be"
  [livespec3]="cc1e29a6-2627-4c8f-9975-f6dae68013a5"
  [livespec4]="85750c9d-727c-4f22-aec7-d0d2fe4e2636"
  [livespec5]="33dd7a3b-9025-4b99-bca1-23877051b123"
  [livespec-console-beads-fabro]=""
)
# epic/work-item to status-watch (display + trigger)
declare -A EPIC=(
  [livespec3]="livespec-zs22.7.9"
  [livespec5]="livespec-zs22.8"
  [livespec-console-beads-fabro]="livespec-inxg"
)
# CURRENT STATE (2026-06-27):
#  livespec-console-beads-fabro ACTIVE: running the console-fleet-wiring prompt
#    (livespec-inxg). STEP 1 is the maintainer's owner-only GitHub App install;
#    STEP 2 auto-wires. Trigger = livespec-inxg CLOSED -> overseer resumes livespec3.
#    Its idle is EXPECTED (waiting on the human) -> NO idle-stall trigger; the
#    ~6-min heartbeat is the periodic check-in.
#  livespec1: 6jfq COMPLETE (fleet doctor-static enforced, prompt deleted).
#  livespec2: zs22.7.8 done; wiring maintainer-gated (this console track).
#  livespec3: zs22.7.9 .1+.7 done; .2 (#190) parked on the console deadlock -> resume on console green.
#  livespec4: livespec-p2zv DONE (README link merged PR #663).
#  livespec5: zs22.8 M0+M1 done (M1 closed v151); M2-M6 open; AT REST.
declare -A PARKED=( [livespec1]=1 [livespec2]=1 [livespec3]=1 [livespec4]=1 [livespec5]=1 )

declare -A IDLE=(); declare -A PREVHASH=(); declare -A BUSYRUN=()
for s in "${SESSIONS[@]}"; do IDLE[$s]=0; done

ctxof() { # arg1 dir, arg2 id-prefix("" ok) -> token count
  local f
  if [ -n "$2" ]; then f=$(ls -t "$1/$2"*.jsonl 2>/dev/null | head -1); else f=$(ls -t "$1"/*.jsonl 2>/dev/null | head -1); fi
  [ -z "$f" ] && { echo 0; return; }
  python3 -c "import json,sys
u=None
for l in open(sys.argv[1]):
 l=l.strip()
 if not l: continue
 try: r=json.loads(l)
 except: continue
 m=r.get('message',{})
 if isinstance(m,dict) and isinstance(m.get('usage'),dict): u=m['usage']
print((u.get('input_tokens',0)+u.get('cache_read_input_tokens',0)+u.get('cache_creation_input_tokens',0)) if u else 0)" "$f" 2>/dev/null || echo 0
}
epicstatus() {
  ( source "$WRAP" bd -C /data/projects/livespec show "$1" 2>/dev/null ) | head -1 \
    | grep -oE '(OPEN|CLOSED|IN_PROGRESS)\]' | head -1 | tr -d ']'
}

TRIG=""
for i in $(seq 1 14); do            # ~14 x 25s ≈ 6-min heartbeat
  sleep 25
  : > "$OUT"; echo "iter $i $(date +%H:%M:%S)" >> "$OUT"
  for s in "${SESSIONS[@]}"; do
    p=$(command tmux capture-pane -p -t "$s" 2>/dev/null)
    h=$(printf '%s' "$p" | md5sum | cut -d' ' -f1)
    if [ "$h" = "${PREVHASH[$s]:-}" ]; then b=0; else b=1; fi
    printf '%s\n' "$p" | tail -3 | grep -qiE 'esc to interrupt' && b=1
    PREVHASH[$s]="$h"
    if [ "$b" = 0 ]; then IDLE[$s]=$(( ${IDLE[$s]} + 1 )); else IDLE[$s]=0; fi
    c=$(ctxof "${TRDIR[$s]}" "${SID[$s]}")
    es=""; [ $((i%3)) -eq 0 ] && [ -n "${EPIC[$s]:-}" ] && es=$(epicstatus "${EPIC[$s]}")
    pct=$(( c/10000 ))
    echo "  $s busy=$b idle=${IDLE[$s]} ctx=${pct}.%  watch=[$es]" >> "$OUT"
    last=$(printf '%s\n' "$p" | grep -vE '^[[:space:]]*$' | tail -2 | tr '\n' ' ')
    echo "      tail: $last" >> "$OUT"
    if [ "$s" = livespec-console-beads-fabro ]; then
      # active completion-watch: fire only when the wiring item closes.
      [ "$es" = CLOSED ] && TRIG="$TRIG console:INXG-CLOSED"
      [ "${c:-0}" -ge 480000 ] && TRIG="$TRIG console:CTX-${pct}%"
    else
      # parked tracks: surface a maintainer-driven REACTIVATION (2 busy samples).
      [ "$es" = CLOSED ] && TRIG="$TRIG $s:EPIC-CLOSED"
      if printf '%s\n' "$p" | tail -3 | grep -qiE 'esc to interrupt'; then
        BUSYRUN[$s]=$(( ${BUSYRUN[$s]:-0} + 1 ))
        [ ${BUSYRUN[$s]} -ge 2 ] && TRIG="$TRIG $s:REACTIVATED"
      else
        BUSYRUN[$s]=0
      fi
    fi
  done
  if [ -n "$TRIG" ]; then echo "TRIGGER:$TRIG" >> "$OUT"; break; fi
done
echo "heartbeat done $(date +%H:%M:%S) trig=[$TRIG]" >> "$OUT"

attributionMcpServer: claude.ai Nate B. Jones Executive Circle MCP

content.text:
The **prior-art agent landed** with a sharp, well-sourced result. The headline finding validates your instinct about the 7 states: **none of the major agentic systems (Devin, OpenHands, Factory.ai) cleanly separate (a) "needs a human" from (b) "blocked on infra/external" from (c) "claimed done, awaiting human acceptance"** — they collapse all of it into one generic `blocked` or a terminal `done`. That three-way split *is* the differentiating opportunity. Plus: Linear's **category-vs-status** model (small machine-meaningful category axis + customizable human labels) and **Little's Law** (`lead time = WIP / throughput`, "stop starting, start finishing") give us the exact theoretical backbone for WIP lanes.

The Gastown/Yegge agent is still running. Rather than stall, here's the **design thesis taking shape** — the core brainstorming payload — so you can steer before I commit it to a plan thread.

## The reframe: the overseer is hand-maintaining a state machine in bash

Concrete evidence from the running overseer's own `watch.sh`: it hardcodes a per-session `SID` map (session UUIDs), an `EPIC` map, hand-edited `PARKED=( [livespec1]=1 … )` flags, and a prose `# CURRENT STATE (2026-06-27)` comment block — then infers busy/idle by **md5-hashing tmux panes**. That *is* a state machine; it's just improvised, non-durable, and invisible to every other tool. The whole proposal is: **lift that state into Beads as a small, deterministic, WIP-limited lifecycle that the Dispatcher, overseer, console, and `plan` all read from one source of truth.**

## The canonical state model (the payoff)

Your seven states aren't seven lanes — they're **5 lanes × an orthogonal hold-reason**, which is exactly the Linear category/status split:

| Your state | Canonical **lane** | **Hold-reason** (orthogonal) | Beads realization | Open Engine analog |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 groomed & ready | `ready` *(derived)* | — | `open` + deps clear + groomed | Agent Todo |
| 2 actively worked | `active` | — | `in_progress` | Agent Working / `AGENT CLAIMED` |
| 3 blocked: needs human | `blocked` | `needs-human-decision` | `blocked` + reason | Needs Input / `AGENT HUMAN HOLD` |
| 4 claimed-done, needs verify | **`review`** ← *the one genuinely new lane* | — | new status/label | **Agent Review — "the honest middle"** |
| 5 actually done | `done` | — | `closed` | Agent Done (accepted) |
| 6 blocked: infra/external | `blocked` | `infra-external` | `blocked` + reason | *(nobody models this — `AGENT FAILED` + last-safe-step)* |
| 7 waiting: approval/non-human | `parked` | `awaiting-approval` / `dependency` | `deferred` + reason | Standing / `AGENT BLOCKED` |

So we **add two things, not seven**: the `review` acceptance gate (state 4), and a `hold-reason` enum that splits 3/6/7. Everything else already exists in Beads (`open/in_progress/blocked/deferred/closed`, with `ready` *derived*, not stored). Nate's **receipt vocabulary** (`CLAIMED / BLOCKED / HUMAN HOLD / RESUMED / DONE / FAILED`) becomes the **deterministic transition log** — and his iron rule "*a blocked task is a pause, not a terminal state; `AGENT RESUMED` is the only thing between a paused task and a dead one*" is the overseer's anti-stall law, finally backed by state instead of vibes.

## The four design axes (my current leanings)

1. **Where it lives** — Beads metadata + a thin orchestrator-plane transition/WIP contract = the single source of truth. Console **projects** it (confirmed: the console spec says it does *not* own Beads/Dispatcher semantics — it's a read-only event-sourced observer). Overseer **drives** it; Dispatcher **respects WIP** when draining.
2. **WIP limits** — per-lane caps (especially `active` and `review`), justified by Little's Law; the cap is what forces "finish before start" and surfaces the bottleneck. This is the first-class Kanban concept you correctly flagged the console is missing.
3. **`plan` relationship → subsume the *tracking*, not the skill.** The state machine becomes the one lifecycle substrate for *all* long-lived units — epics, work-items, **and planning threads** (a plan thread = an epic living in a `planning` lane with its own hold states). `plan` stays the *interaction surface* for the Planning Lane; it stops being a parallel tracking mechanism. The overseer becomes Nate's "loop manager" — the outer loop that keeps lanes flowing and resumes paused items.
4. **Ownership** — adopt the "every work-item has exactly one accountable owner" card, with conservative-by-default (draft/read-only) boundaries and explicit pause conditions — the overseer's per-track ownership made durable.

The **loops-of-loops**, concretely: *inner* = the factory loop per work-item → *middle* = the WIP-limited lane state machine across items → *outer* = the overseer meta-loop (enforce WIP, resume paused, surface only `review` + `blocked:needs-human` gates) → *upstream* = the `plan` Planning Lane feeding the ledger. The console renders every layer; the human acts only at the two human gates.

I'll fold in the Gastown findings the moment that agent returns, then propose running `/livespec-orchestrator-beads-fabro:plan` to capture all of this as a `plan/<topic>/` thread (research notes + self-sufficient handoff + anchored epic) in livespec core. Standing by for that agent — and open to your reactions to the model above in the meantime.

attributionMcpServer: claude.ai Nate B. Jones Executive Circle MCP

---

# Gastown ("Gas Town") — Steve Yegge's agent orchestration system

## 1. What IS Gastown?
Gas Town is a personal open-source project by **Steve Yegge** (ex-Geoworks/Amazon/Google/Grab/**Sourcegraph**), released **January 1, 2026**, written in **Go**, MIT-licensed (https://steve-yegge.medium.com/welcome-to-gas-town-4f25ee16dd04). It is **not** a Sourcegraph or Amp product — Yegge built it independently after Sourcegraph (https://thenewstack.io/steve-yegges-ai-agent-orchestration-project-gas-town-comes-to-the-cloud-and-brings-the-wasteland-with-it/); "amp" appears only as one of its supported agent presets (https://github.com/gastownhall/gastown). The repo self-describes it as a "**Multi-agent orchestration system for Claude Code, GitHub Copilot, and other AI agents with persistent work tracking**" — "a workspace manager that lets you coordinate multiple AI coding agents… Instead of losing context when agents restart, Gas Town persists work state in git-backed hooks" (https://github.com/gastownhall/gastown). Yegge frames it as "Kubernetes for agents" and an "industrialized coding factory" (https://steve-yegge.medium.com/welcome-to-gas-town-4f25ee16dd04).

## 2. Architecture for running many agents
Hierarchy: a **Town** (workspace `~/gt/`) contains **Rigs** (each Rig wraps one git repo/project), which contain **Crew** (your hands-on workspace), **Polecats** (worker agents with persistent identity but ephemeral sessions), and **Hooks** (git-worktree-based persistent storage that survives crashes) (https://github.com/gastownhall/gastown). The **Mayor** (an AI coordinator/Claude Code instance) decomposes a request into **beads**, bundles them into a **Convoy** (work-tracking unit), and assigns work with `gt sling <bead-id> <rig>`, which hangs the bead on the target agent's Hook (https://github.com/gastownhall/gastown). Lifecycle: bead created → slung to a Hook → agent atomically claims it (`bd update --claim` sets assignee + `in_progress`) → works **molecule** steps → `gt done` pushes a branch + MR bead → **Refinery** merges. The README ships an explicit **Hook state machine**: `Created → Active → Suspended → Active → Completed → Archived` (https://github.com/gastownhall/gastown). Work itself is "molecular" — Beads → Epics → Molecules (sequenced TODO checklists) → Formulas (TOML templates) — what Yegge calls "Nondeterministic Idempotence": a crashed molecule is resumed mid-step by the next session (https://steve-yegge.medium.com/welcome-to-gas-town-4f25ee16dd04).

## 3. Concurrency / WIP control
A **Scheduler** acts as a "**config-driven capacity governor for polecat dispatch… Prevents API rate limit exhaustion by batching dispatch under configurable concurrency limits**"; `scheduler.max_polecats` caps concurrent workers (default `-1` = direct dispatch) (https://github.com/gastownhall/gastown). Selection is **pull-based**: `bd ready` lists "tasks with no open blockers," and the **GUPP** invariant — "**If there is work on your hook, YOU MUST RUN IT**" — drives agents to pull their next item (https://steve-yegge.medium.com/welcome-to-gas-town-4f25ee16dd04). Patrol loops use **exponential backoff** ("the agent will gradually go to sleep if it finds no work… Any mutating `gt` or `bd` command will wake the town") (https://steve-yegge.medium.com/welcome-to-gas-town-4f25ee16dd04). Yegge reports running "a dozen" up to "20–30 at once… on a sustained basis" (https://steve-yegge.medium.com/welcome-to-gas-town-4f25ee16dd04).

## 4. Human-in-the-loop gates
Two distinct surfaces exist. (a) **Escalation** — agents that hit blockers call `gt escalate -s {CRITICAL|HIGH|MEDIUM}`, creating tracked beads "routed through the **Deacon → Mayor → Overseer**" by severity (https://github.com/gastownhall/gastown). (b) **Stall/health detection** — the `gt feed --problems` view classifies agents by health state — **GUPP Violation, Stalled, Zombie, Working, Idle** — and offers intervention keys `n` (nudge) and `h` (handoff) to "surface agents needing human intervention" (https://github.com/gastownhall/gastown). So "needs human (blocker escalation)" is modeled separately from "stuck/unhealthy (stall detection)." Merge review is gated at the **Refinery**, a Bors-style bisecting merge queue — "polecats never push directly to main" (https://github.com/gastownhall/gastown). Notably, Gas Town's autonomy can exceed a cautious operator's comfort: DoltHub's Tim Sehn (self-described "stage 5") "quickly close[d] Gas Town" after it autonomously merged PRs (https://www.dolthub.com/blog/2026-01-15-a-day-in-gas-town/).

## 5. How Beads (bd) relates
**Beads is the ledger/memory underneath Gas Town** — "Beads acts as the central agent coordination persistence for Gas Town" (https://www.dolthub.com/blog/2026-01-15-a-day-in-gas-town/). Beads is a separate Yegge project: a "**Distributed graph issue tracker for AI agents, powered by Dolt**" (a version-controlled SQL database); `.beads/issues.jsonl` is an export, not the source of truth (https://github.com/gastownhall/beads). It models issues as a **dependency-aware graph**: hash IDs (`bd-a1b2`) avoid multi-agent merge collisions; hierarchical epic IDs (`bd-a3f8` → `.1` → `.1.1`); typed links (`relates_to`, `duplicates`, `supersedes`, `replies_to`) and `bd dep add <child> <parent>` (blocks/related/parent-child); **auto-ready detection** so `bd ready` returns only unblocked tasks (https://github.com/gastownhall/beads). Gas Town wraps Beads with Convoys, Hooks, and slinging; Beads supplies the persistent state, dependencies, and atomic claim. Server mode runs an external `dolt sql-server` (TCP 3307, multi-writer) — exactly the backend your livespec orchestrator mirrors (https://github.com/gastownhall/beads).

## 6. Loops of loops / meta-orchestration
Gas Town is explicitly a nested supervisory hierarchy. The README's "three-tier watchdog chain": **Daemon** (Go process, heartbeat every 3 min) → **Boot** (AI triage agent) → **Deacon** (continuous cross-rig patrol) → **Witnesses & Refineries** (per-rig agents) (https://github.com/gastownhall/gastown). Each tier runs its own **patrol loop**; the **Witness** monitors polecats per-rig, the **Deacon** patrols across all rigs and dispatches **Dogs** for maintenance (https://github.com/gastownhall/gastown). Yegge describes these patrols as recursive loops with backoff, an outer orchestration loop (Town-level) supervising inner agent loops (Rig/molecule-level) (https://steve-yegge.medium.com/welcome-to-gas-town-4f25ee16dd04). The recommended top-level pattern is **MEOW** (Mayor-Enhanced Orchestration Workflow): tell Mayor → Mayor decomposes → convoy → spawn agents → distribute via hooks → monitor → summarize (https://github.com/gastownhall/gastown).

## 7. Most transferable design lessons
1. **Externalize the state machine into a durable, dependency-aware ledger, not agent memory.** "Ready" is *derived* ("no open blockers"), making scheduling a pure function of graph state; atomic claim (`--claim` = assignee + in_progress) prevents double-dispatch across parallel sessions (https://github.com/gastownhall/beads).
2. **Decouple a capacity governor from dispatch.** A single WIP knob (`scheduler.max_polecats`) provides backpressure against rate limits without touching work-selection logic; default-unlimited with an opt-in cap (https://github.com/gastownhall/gastown).
3. **Make resumption idempotent via a propulsion invariant + per-agent inbox.** GUPP ("if there is work on your hook, run it") + molecule checklists let any fresh session pick a crashed task up mid-step — restart-safety without bespoke recovery code (https://steve-yegge.medium.com/welcome-to-gas-town-4f25ee16dd04).
4. **Tier the supervision (loops of loops); spend cheap determinism low, AI/human high.** A deterministic heartbeat daemon at the bottom escalates to AI triage and only then to a human, with explicit health-state classification (GUPP Violation/Stalled/Zombie) deciding *when* a human is surfaced (https://github.com/gastownhall/gastown).
5. **Separate "blocked, needs decision" from "unhealthy, needs poke," and keep the human gate at the merge boundary.** Severity-routed escalation beads vs. stall detection are distinct channels; a bisecting merge queue (Refinery) concentrates human review at PR/merge instead of per-action — the deliberate "review-and-merge is the human's job" stance (https://github.com/gastownhall/gastown, https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=46624883).

**Caveat / confidence:** All seven are well-sourced from Yegge's own post + the two official READMEs + DoltHub. One nuance I could not fully verify: Yegge uses "loops of loops" framing conceptually, but I did not find that exact phrase in a primary source — the nested-loop architecture itself is firmly documented (the three-tier watchdog chain and patrol loops). The `gastownhall/gastown` and `gastownhall/beads` repos are the canonical mirrors of `steveyegge/gastown` / `steveyegge/beads`.

Sources:
- https://steve-yegge.medium.com/welcome-to-gas-town-4f25ee16dd04 (Yegge, "Welcome to Gas Town," primary)
- https://github.com/gastownhall/gastown (Gas Town README — architecture, scheduler, refinery, escalation, watchdog chain, MEOW)
- https://github.com/gastownhall/beads (Beads README — Dolt-backed graph tracker, ready/claim/dep model)
- https://www.dolthub.com/blog/2026-01-15-a-day-in-gas-town/ (DoltHub, "A Day in Gas Town" — Beads as coordination persistence; Stage-5 human-gate friction)
- https://thenewstack.io/steve-yegges-ai-agent-orchestration-project-gas-town-comes-to-the-cloud-and-brings-the-wasteland-with-it/ (The New Stack — independent project, cloud/Wasteland)
- https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=46624883 ("Gas Town Decoded" HN — refinery/review-and-merge framing)
- https://softwareengineeringdaily.com/2026/02/12/gas-town-beads-and-the-rise-of-agentic-development-with-steve-yegge/ (SE Daily interview — chat→agent-orchestration evolution)</result>

content.text:
The **Gastown agent landed** — and this is the most important corollary of all, because **Gas Town is Steve Yegge's own system (Jan 2026), and Beads is also Yegge's project.** So livespec is *already standing on Yegge's ledger*; Gas Town is the closest existing reference architecture to exactly what you're designing. That reframes the whole effort.

## The big realization: livespec is already ~70% of a Gas Town

Gas Town's spine — `Mayor decomposes a request → beads → Convoy → sling to a Hook → atomic claim (assignee + in_progress) → molecule steps → gt done → Refinery merges` — is the same shape as livespec's `plan → Beads epic → ready → Dispatcher → Fabro → janitor gate → merge`. Same Dolt-backed Beads ledger, same derived-`ready`, same dependency graph. **What Gas Town has that the livespec overseer conspicuously lacks** is precisely your ask:

| Gas Town has | livespec overseer has | Gap = your ask |
|---|---|---|
| **`scheduler.max_polecats`** — a capacity governor *decoupled from dispatch* | nothing | **the WIP knob** |
| Explicit **Hook state machine** `Created→Active→Suspended→Active→Completed→Archived` | a bash `PARKED=()` table + prose comments | **the lane state machine** |
| **Tiered supervision**: a deterministic Go **daemon** (3-min heartbeat) → AI triage (Boot) → cross-rig patrol (Deacon) → human | one **Opus-1M session md5-hashing tmux panes** | **cheap determinism at the bottom** |
| **Two human channels**: severity-routed `gt escalate` beads **vs.** health classifier (`GUPP Violation/Stalled/Zombie/Working/Idle`) | one AI deciding ad hoc | **needs-decision vs. needs-poke split** |
| **Refinery** merge queue — human review concentrated at the merge boundary | factory self-merges | **the `review` acceptance gate (state 4)** |

The single sharpest architectural finding: **the livespec overseer is inverted.** Gas Town spends *cheap determinism low and AI/human high* — a dumb Go daemon does the heartbeat; AI and humans are only invoked on escalation. The livespec overseer does the opposite: it burns an expensive 1M-context Opus session doing the low-level pane-scraping heartbeat that should be a deterministic process. **That points straight at the console** (Rust, event-sourced, already a deterministic projector) as the natural "daemon tier," with the AI overseer demoted to the thin triage loop above it. That answers your uncertainty about how console + overseer relate: *console = the deterministic bottom tier (Gas Town's daemon/Witness); overseer-AI = the triage tier (Boot/Deacon); human = the top.*

And it settles the **plan question** by reference architecture: Gas Town's Mayor (planning/decomposition) and its execution loops feed **one** Beads ledger in different roles. So — **subsume the *tracking* into the shared state machine, keep `plan` as the planning-lane interaction surface.** Not replaced; unified underneath. (Cautionary data point: DoltHub's Tim Sehn *closed Gas Town* after it auto-merged PRs unattended — which is the strongest argument for keeping your state-4 `review` gate.)

I now have everything: Nate's Open Engine (task-record + receipts + the honest-middle `review`), Gas Town (the WIP governor, the tiered loops, the state machine, the human-channel split), prior art (WIP/Little's Law + Linear category model + the (a)/(b)/(c) gap nobody fills), and the concrete livespec ground truth. Time to capture it into a `plan/` thread via the plan skill.

One micro-decision the plan skill mandates — the thread **slug** (it confirms this with you rather than guessing). Pick the framing you want this to live under:

---

questions.question: What slug should the plan thread live under? (This names the plan/<slug>/ directory in livespec core and the anchoring ledger epic. I'll capture the research + the state-model design + a self-sufficient handoff prompt under it.)

options.label: work-lifecycle-state-machine
options.description: Recommended. Precise and discoverable: the core artifact is a deterministic work-item lifecycle state machine (the lanes + hold-reasons + WIP) that the overseer, Dispatcher, console, and plan all read. Doesn't over-anchor on 'overseer' (just one consumer of the substrate).

options.label: overseer-engine
options.description: Aligns with your vocabulary ('the overseer system') and echoes Nate's 'Open Engine.' Frames the thread around the meta-loop rather than the state substrate underneath it.

options.label: wip-lane-state-machine
options.description: Foregrounds the genuinely-new Kanban/WIP-limited-lane concept that no current livespec artifact owns. Narrower framing centered on the lanes + capacity governor.
